chapters.name: Exploration: Entering the World of Secondary Science

subtopics.name: Exploration: Entering the World of Secondary Science

questions.question: Why are scientific theories not final?
questions.explanation: Scientific knowledge evolves as new evidence emerges.

questions.question: Why is estimation important in science?
questions.explanation: Estimation helps verify whether results are logical and realistic.

questions.question: Why do weather forecasts become less reliable over time?
questions.explanation: Tiny differences in conditions amplify over time, reducing prediction accuracy.

questions.question: What distinguishes a theory from a law?
questions.explanation: Laws describe patterns, while theories explain why those patterns occur.

questions.question: What is a scientific law?
questions.explanation: Laws describe consistent patterns observed in nature.

questions.question: Why are standard units important?
questions.explanation: Standard units ensure consistency in measurements across the world.

questions.question: Why did the airplane run out of fuel?
questions.explanation: The error occurred due to confusion between pounds and kilograms.

questions.question: What is the main purpose of scientific language?
questions.explanation: Scientific language uses precise terms, symbols, and units to avoid ambiguity.

questions.question: Which of the following is an example of simplification in science?

options.option: Treating a car as a point object

questions.explanation: Representing a car as a point object ignores unnecessary details to study motion effectively.

questions.question: Why do scientists use models?
questions.explanation: Models simplify real-world systems by focusing only on relevant aspects, making understanding easier.

questions.question: Why is the speed of light represented by ‘c’?
questions.explanation: The symbol ‘c’ comes from the Latin word celeritas, meaning speed.

questions.question: What does the magnifying glass symbol represent?
questions.explanation: The magnifying glass symbolizes careful observation, emphasizing noticing patterns and details that might otherwise be overlooked.

chapters.name: Cell: The Building Block of Life

questions.question: Which organelle is responsible for modifying, sorting, and packaging proteins and lipids?
questions.explanation: The Golgi apparatus acts like the cell's post office, modifying, sorting, and packaging proteins and/or lipids.

questions.question: What is the primary function of ribosomes?
questions.explanation: Ribosomes are specifically known as the sites of protein synthesis.

questions.question: Which organelle is referred to as the 'house of coded instructions'?
questions.explanation: The nucleus contains chromosomes with DNA, which holds the genetic information and is described as the 'house of coded instructions'.

questions.question: What are the three basic parts found in most cells?
questions.explanation: Most cells have a selectively permeable plasma membrane, a semi-fluid cytoplasm, and a prominent nucleus as their three basic parts.

questions.question: Which of the following pairs of cell organelles contains DNA?
questions.explanation: Mitochondria and nucleus both contain DNA.

questions.question: How do single-celled organisms interact with their environment?

options.option: By exchanging materials through the cell membrane.
Exclusively through passive diffusion of all substances

questions.explanation: Even single-celled organisms exchange materials and respond to their environment through the cell membrane.

questions.question: What is the role of proteins embedded in the cell membrane?

options.option: To help substances pass through the membrane.
To convert light energy into chemical energy
To provide structural rigidity to the cell

questions.explanation: Proteins in the membrane act like gatekeepers in helping substances pass through. They facilitate transport across the selectively permeable membrane.

questions.question: What is the significance of the cell wall's rigidity and permeability in plant roots?

options.option: It keeps the plant firm and helps roots absorb water and nutrients.
It prevents the plant from absorbing any water or nutrients.
It makes the plant cell fragile and easily damaged.
It enhances cellular flexibility, similar to animal cells.

questions.explanation: The cell wall helps leaves and flowers remain firm and maintain their shapes. Although rigid, the cell wall is permeable, which means water and some dissolved minerals can pass through it, aiding in absorption by plant roots.

questions.question: What is cellulose, the primary component of plant cell walls, made of?

options.option: A carbohydrate made of linked glucose units.

questions.explanation: The plant cell wall is primarily made of cellulose, a type of carbohydrate formed by many glucose units linked together.

questions.question: What is the approximate thickness of the cell membrane?
questions.explanation: Structurally, the cell membrane is extremely thin, about 7 to 10 nanometres (nm) thick.

questions.question: Which type of cells possess an additional layer around the cell membrane called the cell wall?
questions.explanation: Cells of plant, fungi, and bacteria have an additional layer around the cell membrane, called the cell wall.

questions.question: What is the primary function of the cell membrane?

options.option: Protecting the cell and maintaining its identity.

questions.explanation: The cell membrane surrounds a cell, protects its contents, and defines its individuality. It is also called the plasma membrane.

questions.question: Why are organisms like bacteria and yeast described as unicellular?

options.option: Because they consist of only one cell.

questions.explanation: Unicellular organisms, such as bacteria and yeast, are defined as consisting of only one cell.

questions.question: In what year did Robert Hooke first observe a cell?
questions.explanation: Robert Hooke observed a cell in 1665.

questions.question: Who was the first person to observe a cell?
questions.explanation: Robert Hooke was the first person to observe a cell in 1665.

questions.question: What is the limit of resolution of the human eye?
questions.explanation: The limit of resolution of the human eye is stated as 0.1 mm.

questions.question: Which of the following is considered the basic level at which life exists?
questions.explanation: The cell represents the basic level at which life exists.

questions.question: What are thermophiles primarily known for?

options.option: Being organisms that thrive in acidic conditions.

questions.explanation: Thermophiles are described as heat-loving bacteria living in hot springs.

questions.question: What role do chromoplasts play in stimulating plant reproduction?

options.option: Their bright colors attract pollinators and seed dispersers.
They synthesize chlorophyll, which is vital for the growth of reproductive organs.
They produce hormones that directly trigger flowering.
They store nutrients essential for seed development.

questions.explanation: Chromoplasts, with their yellow, orange, or red pigments, provide bright colors to flowers and fruits, which visually attract pollinators and animals for seed dispersal, thus aiding plant reproduction.

questions.question: Which type of plastid is responsible for synthesizing food in plant cells?
questions.explanation: Chloroplasts contain chlorophyll and are the sites of photosynthesis, where plants synthesize their food.

questions.question: What are the finger-like projections found on the inner membrane of mitochondria called?
questions.explanation: The inner membrane of mitochondria is folded into finger-like projections called cristae, increasing the surface area for chemical reactions.

questions.question: What is the function of lysosomes?

options.option: Breaking down waste materials and damaged organelles

questions.explanation: Lysosomes are single membrane-bound sacs filled with enzymes that break down unwanted proteins, carbohydrates, fats, and damaged parts of the cell, functioning as the 'clean-up system'.

questions.question: Which scientist expanded the Cell Theory by stating that new cells are formed only from pre-existing cells?
questions.explanation: Rudolf Virchow in 1855 expanded the Cell Theory with the principle that all cells arise from pre-existing cells.

questions.question: What is the primary characteristic that distinguishes cancer cells from normal animal cells in terms of growth control?

options.option: Cancer cells grow and divide in a controlled way.
Cancer cells lose contact inhibition and divide uncontrollably.
Cancer cells always die when they are no longer needed.
Cancer cells have a definite life span.

questions.explanation: Normal animal cells stop dividing when they come into contact with neighboring cells (contact inhibition), but cancer cells lose this control and divide uncontrollably.

questions.question: Define 'totipotency' as proposed by Gottlieb Haberlandt.

options.option: The process by which plant cells secrete substances together.
The ability of plant cells to produce energy efficiently.
The ability of plant cells to stop dividing on contact.

questions.explanation: Haberlandt proposed that totipotency is the special ability of a plant cell to develop into a complete plant and form different types of cells, given suitable conditions.

questions.question: A researcher observes that a type of animal cell continues to divide even after forming a dense layer in a culture dish, completely ignoring the presence of adjacent cells. Based on the provided information, what common cellular control mechanism has failed in these cells?
questions.explanation: Normal animal cells cease division when they come into contact with neighboring cells, a process called contact inhibition. The observed uncontrolled division suggests this mechanism has failed, similar to cancer cells.

questions.question: If a botanist successfully cultures an entire plant from a single mature leaf cell, which concept, foundational to Plant Tissue Culture Technology, is best demonstrated?
questions.explanation: The ability of a single mature plant cell to develop into a complete plant is defined as totipotency, a concept central to Plant Tissue Culture Technology.

questions.question: An embryo developing fingers specifically involves the elimination of cells between digits. What biological process is primarily responsible for ensuring this precise shaping and removal of cells?
questions.explanation: The formation of fingers by eliminating cells between digits is cited as an example of Programmed Cell Death (PCD), a genetically regulated process essential for normal development and cellular quality control.

questions.question: What is the primary role of meiosis in sexually reproducing organisms?
questions.explanation: Meiosis produces gametes with half the chromosome number and introduces genetic variation through processes like crossing over and independent assortment. This variation is essential for sexual reproduction and evolution.

questions.question: In which organisms do eukaryotic cells primarily divide in a more controlled and orderly manner?
questions.explanation: Eukaryotic cells divide through regulated processes like mitosis and meiosis, which are highly controlled and organized. Prokaryotes divide mainly by binary fission, which is simpler and less structured compared to eukaryotic cell division.

questions.question: What is the outcome of mitosis regarding the genetic information in daughter cells?

options.option: The genetic information is randomly distributed, leading to variation.
Each daughter cell gets the same DNA and chromosome number.
Daughter cells get half the DNA but the same chromosome number.
Each daughter cell gets different DNA and chromosome numbers.

questions.explanation: In mitosis, the parent cell divides to produce two genetically identical daughter cells. Each daughter cell receives the same DNA and the same number of chromosomes as the parent cell, ensuring growth, repair, and replacement of cells.

questions.question: What happens to the number of chromosomes in each daughter cell after the first division in meiosis?

options.option: It is reduced to half of the parent cell's number.
It remains the same as the parent cell.
It doubles compared to the parent cell.

questions.explanation: After the first division of meiosis (Meiosis I), homologous chromosome pairs separate, reducing the chromosome number by half. As a result, each daughter cell contains half the number of chromosomes compared to the original parent cell.

subtopics.name: The Cell Interior - A Coordinated Working System

questions.question: Which of the following is not a primary function of cell division?
questions.explanation: The primary functions of cell division are forming new cells, growing living organisms, and repairing damaged tissues. Cell division does not primarily change an organism's genetic makeup for adaptation; adaptation occurs through genetic variation and evolution over generations.

questions.question: What is the key difference between how a body grows and how individual cells grow?

options.option: The body grows by enlarging cells, while cells grow by forming new cells.
Cells divide to form new cells, while the body grows by cell enlargement.
Individual cells grow larger, while the body grows new organs.
Cells grow only to a limit, but the body grows by cell division.

questions.explanation: Individual cells grow only to a limited size. The body grows mainly because cells divide and produce more cells, increasing the total number of cells and allowing tissues and organs to grow.

questions.question: What is the primary reason for a small cut on the skin healing and falling hair growing back?

options.option: Cells in our body can differentiate into different types.

questions.explanation: Small cuts heal and hair grows back because cells undergo cell division, producing new cells that replace damaged, dead, or lost cells. This process helps repair tissues and maintain normal body functions.

questions.question: An experiment involves placing animal cells and plant cells in a concentrated sugar solution. Which of the following observations would be expected and why?

options.option: Animal cells would shrink, plant cells maintain shape due to cell wall.
Animal cells would burst, and plant cells would swell.
Both animal and plant cells would swell due to water intake.
Both animal and plant cells would maintain their original shape.

questions.explanation: A concentrated sugar solution is hypertonic, so water leaves both cells by osmosis. Animal cells shrink because they lack a cell wall. Plant cells lose water too, but the rigid cell wall helps maintain their overall shape.

questions.question: A scientist is observing a tissue sample from a human embryo. If the cells in the sample are undergoing extensive division to increase the number of cells for growth, what type of cell division would be predominantly observed?

options.option: Cell fusion, where cells combine to form larger structures.
Meiosis, as it leads to genetic diversity necessary for development.
Mitosis, as it increases cell number for growth.
A combination of mitosis and meiosis, equally contributing to growth.

questions.explanation: During embryonic development, rapid growth requires the production of many new cells. Mitosis increases cell number while preserving the same genetic information in each daughter cell. This ensures proper growth, development, and formation of tissues and organs.

questions.question: A patient suffers a severe injury, leading to significant tissue damage. Which type of cell division would primarily be responsible for repairing this damaged tissue?

options.option: Mitosis, as it forms identical cells for repair.
Both mitosis and meiosis equally contribute to tissue repair.

questions.explanation: Mitosis produces daughter cells that are genetically identical to the parent cell. This process is essential for growth, replacement of worn-out cells, and repair of damaged tissues. Meiosis is involved in gamete formation and does not contribute to tissue repair.

questions.question: A sexually reproducing plant produces pollen grains and egg cells. Which type of cell division is exclusively responsible for the formation of these reproductive cells?

options.option: Binary fission, which is common in plant reproduction.
Meiosis forms gametes with half the chromosomes.
Mitosis, as it ensures rapid cell multiplication for reproduction.

questions.explanation: Meiosis is the specialized cell division that produces reproductive cells (gametes) such as pollen grains and egg cells. It reduces the chromosome number by half, ensuring that the normal chromosome number is restored after fertilization and promoting genetic variation.

questions.question: An organism is undergoing intense repair and replacement of cells that are regularly lost, such as skin cells. Which cell division process would be most prevalent in these tissues?

options.option: Meiosis, because it ensures genetic recombination for adaptation.
Mitosis, which continuously replaces lost cells while maintaining genetic consistency.
Both mitosis and meiosis equally, depending on the organism's age.
Only cell enlargement without division, to compensate for loss.

questions.explanation: Mitosis helps in growth, repair, and replacement of worn-out or damaged cells. The daughter cells formed are genetically identical to the parent cell.

questions.question: A gardener wants to grow a specific plant variety that is genetically identical to the parent plant. Which method of propagation would be most suitable, considering the cell division processes described?

options.option: Sexual reproduction through seeds formed by meiosis.
Inducing errors in meiosis to create novel traits.

questions.explanation: Asexual reproduction involves only one parent and occurs through mitotic cell division. The new plants produced are genetically identical to the parent plant and are called clones.

questions.question: A chemical interferes with the function of cristae within the mitochondria. What would be the direct impact on cellular processes?
questions.explanation: Cristae are folds of the inner mitochondrial membrane that contain enzymes involved in cellular respiration. Damage to or interference with cristae reduces the efficiency of ATP generation, limiting the energy available for the cell’s activities.

questions.question: A scientist observes a cell under a microscope and notes the presence of a cell wall, chloroplasts, and a large central vacuole. This cell is most likely to be absent in which of the following organisms?
questions.explanation: The presence of a cell wall, chloroplasts, and a large central vacuole is characteristic of plant cells. Animal cells lack all three of these features. Therefore, a cell with these structures would be absent in animals but found in plants and many algae.

questions.question: If a manufacturing defect causes the ribosomes in a pancreatic cell to become detached from the endoplasmic reticulum, what would be the most immediate consequence on the cell's function?
questions.explanation: Pancreatic cells produce and secrete large amounts of proteins, such as digestive enzymes. Ribosomes attached to the rough endoplasmic reticulum synthesize these proteins. If the ribosomes detach, protein production and secretion would be disrupted, affecting the cell’s primary function.

questions.question: A cell type is observed under an electron microscope to have a dense, round body within its nucleus where ribosomal subunits are assembled. Which of the following cells would exhibit this feature?
questions.explanation: The dense, round body where ribosomal subunits are assembled is the nucleolus, which is found inside the nucleus of eukaryotic cells. Plant cells are eukaryotic and possess a nucleus with a nucleolus, whereas prokaryotic cells lack a true nucleus and nuclear membrane.

questions.question: Which cellular component is involved in breaking down unwanted proteins, carbohydrates, and fats, essentially acting as the 'clean-up system'?
questions.explanation: Lysosomes contain digestive enzymes that break down unwanted proteins, carbohydrates, fats, worn-out organelles, and cellular waste. By recycling useful materials and removing debris, they function as the cell’s clean-up and waste disposal system.

questions.question: Which type of cells typically lack a well-defined nucleus and membrane-bound organelles?
questions.explanation: Prokaryotic cells lack a true, membrane-bound nucleus and do not contain membrane-bound organelles such as mitochondria or the Golgi apparatus. Their genetic material is located in a nucleoid region, making their cellular organization simpler than that of eukaryotic cells.

questions.question: What is the primary function of the Golgi apparatus in a cell?
questions.explanation: The Golgi apparatus receives proteins and lipids from the endoplasmic reticulum, modifies them, sorts them, and packages them into vesicles. These vesicles transport the materials to their required destinations inside or outside the cell for proper cellular functioning.

questions.question: Which cellular component is responsible for synthesizing ribosomal subunits?
questions.explanation: The nucleolus is a dense region inside the nucleus where ribosomal RNA (rRNA) is produced and combined with proteins to form ribosomal subunits. These subunits later assemble into ribosomes, which are essential for protein synthesis.

questions.question: When a Rhoeo leaf peel is placed in a concentrated sugar solution, the inner content shrinks, but the overall cell size remains unchanged due to the cell wall. In contrast, onion peel cells, when similarly treated, would also maintain their overall shape despite plasmolysis. What critical property of the cell wall does this demonstrate in both plant types?

options.option: The cell wall actively transports water into the cell.
The cell wall is flexible and allows cells to change shape.
The cell wall is rigid, providing structural support.
The cell wall is selectively permeable and regulates solute movement.

questions.explanation: During plasmolysis, water leaves the cell and the cell membrane pulls away from the cell wall. However, the rigid cell wall maintains the cell’s original shape and size. This demonstrates the structural support function of the cell wall in plant cells.

questions.question: Consider the differences between onion peel cells and human cheek cells when observed under a microscope, particularly regarding their arrangement and shape. What best explains these differences?

options.option: Onion cells have a more developed nucleus than cheek cells.
Onion cells have a rigid cell wall, unlike cheek cells.
Cheek cells have a thicker cytoplasm, influencing their irregular distribution.
Cheek cells are more metabolically active, causing irregular shapes.

questions.explanation: Onion peel cells are plant cells that have a rigid cell wall, giving them a regular, rectangular shape and orderly arrangement. Human cheek cells lack a cell wall and have only a cell membrane, resulting in more irregular shapes and distribution.

questions.question: A cell is described as having a thin boundary that defines its individuality and controls substance movement. If this cell were observed to have proteins acting as 'gatekeepers' within this boundary, what specific part of the cell is being described?
questions.explanation: The cell membrane is a thin, selectively permeable boundary that surrounds the cell and controls the movement of substances. Proteins embedded in the membrane act as gatekeepers, regulating transport, communication, and the exchange of materials between the cell and its environment.

questions.question: The fluid-mosaic model describes the cell membrane as fluid. What does this fluidity primarily allow for within the membrane structure?

options.option: Molecules to move sideways, flip, and rotate.

questions.explanation: The fluid-mosaic model states that phospholipids and some proteins within the cell membrane can move within the bilayer. This fluidity allows molecules to move sideways, rotate, and occasionally flip, helping the membrane remain flexible and functional.

questions.question: Given that Red Blood Cells (RBCs) in humans lack a nucleus, what can be inferred about their capacity for self-repair and lifespan?

options.option: Their lifespan is short, and they cannot repair or divide themselves.
They have an extended lifespan due to reduced metabolic activity.
They depend entirely on other cells for repair and division.
They are capable of self-repair but cannot divide.

questions.explanation: Human red blood cells lack a nucleus and most organelles, so they cannot synthesize new proteins, divide, or effectively repair cellular damage. As a result, they have a limited lifespan (about 120 days) and must be continuously replaced by the body.

questions.question: Consider a condition where a cell's DNA cannot be organized into distinct rod-shaped structures during cell division. Which cellular component's function is impaired, and what is its normal role in this process?

options.option: Chromatin; organizing into chromosomes for cell division

questions.explanation: Chromatin consists of DNA and proteins within the nucleus. Before cell division, chromatin condenses into distinct rod-shaped chromosomes, ensuring accurate distribution of genetic material to daughter cells. If this process is impaired, proper chromosome formation and cell division cannot occur.

subtopics.name: How do Normal Cells Grow and Divide?

questions.question: A newly discovered organism has cells that conduct all metabolic activities directly in its semi-fluid internal substance, and genetic material is present in a region without a surrounding membrane. How would this organism's cellular structure be categorized, and what implications does this have for its internal complexity?

options.option: Plant cell; implying the presence of chloroplasts for energy.

questions.explanation: The absence of a membrane-bound nucleus and the presence of genetic material in an uncovered region indicate a prokaryotic cell. Prokaryotes lack membrane-bound organelles, so most metabolic activities occur in the cytoplasm, resulting in a simpler internal structure than eukaryotic cells.

questions.question: If the nuclear membrane of an eukaryotic cell were to become completely impermeable, what would be the immediate and critical impact on cellular function?

options.option: Material transfer would stop, halting gene expression.
It would enhance the protection of genetic material.

questions.explanation: The nuclear membrane contains pores that allow the exchange of RNA, proteins, and other molecules between the nucleus and cytoplasm. If it became completely impermeable, essential communication would stop, preventing protein synthesis, ribosome formation, and normal cellular function.

questions.question: Consider a case where a plant cell develops a defect that prevents its leucoplasts from storing starch effectively. What visible consequence would this have on the plant?

options.option: The plant would experience reduced water uptake.
The plant's flowers and fruits would lose their vibrant colors.
The plant would struggle to grow and store excess energy.
The plant would exhibit abnormal leaf coloration.

questions.explanation: Leucoplasts are colorless plastids that store food materials, especially starch. If they cannot store starch effectively, the plant will have difficulty storing excess energy produced during photosynthesis. This can reduce growth, development, and the plant’s ability to survive unfavorable conditions.

questions.question: An animal cell is treated with a substance that specifically inhibits the continuous network structure of the Endoplasmic Reticulum. What would be the most significant immediate impact on the cell's function related to synthesis and transport?

options.option: Disruption of protein, fat, and hormone synthesis and transport

questions.explanation: The Endoplasmic Reticulum (ER) forms a network responsible for synthesis and transport within the cell. Rough ER synthesizes proteins, while Smooth ER produces fats and some hormones. Inhibiting the ER would severely disrupt these processes and intracellular transport.

questions.question: A cell is under investigation, and researchers confirm that its internal cytoplasm contains a network of fine fibers, visible only under an electron microscope, crucial for structural support, shape maintenance, and internal transport. Which type of cell is being studied, and what are these fibers called?
questions.explanation: The cytoskeleton is a network of fine protein fibers found in the cytoplasm of eukaryotic cells. It provides structural support, maintains cell shape, aids movement, and facilitates the transport of materials within the cell. These fibers are visible only under an electron microscope.

questions.question: A potato piece placed in a solution experienced a decrease in weight. This observation indicates that the movement of water occurred from the potato cells to the surrounding solution. What type of solution was the potato likely placed in?
questions.explanation: A hypertonic solution has a higher solute concentration than the potato cells. Water moves out of the cells by osmosis toward the more concentrated solution, causing the potato piece to lose water and decrease in weight.

questions.question: Which characteristic of the cell wall in plant cells helps plant roots absorb water and nutrients from the soil?

options.option: It is permeable to water and dissolved minerals.

questions.explanation: The plant cell wall is freely permeable, allowing water and dissolved minerals from the soil to pass through it and reach the cell membrane. This property helps root cells efficiently absorb essential nutrients and water needed for plant growth and survival.

questions.question: What components primarily make up the cell membrane, according to the fluid-mosaic model?
questions.explanation: According to the fluid-mosaic model, the cell membrane consists mainly of a phospholipid bilayer with embedded proteins. The lipids form a flexible barrier, while the proteins perform functions such as transport, communication, and recognition, enabling the membrane to function effectively.

questions.question: What is the term for the diffusion of water across a selectively permeable membrane?
questions.explanation: Osmosis is the diffusion of water molecules across a selectively permeable membrane from an area of higher water concentration to an area of lower water concentration. This process helps maintain water balance and proper cellular function in living organisms.

questions.question: What is the primary function of the cell membrane in a cell?

options.option: It produces energy for the cell's activities.
It provides structural support to the cell.
It controls the movement of substances into and out of the cell.
It stores genetic material of the cell.

questions.explanation: The cell membrane acts as a selectively permeable barrier, regulating the entry of nutrients and oxygen and the exit of wastes. This control helps maintain the cell’s internal environment, allowing it to function properly and remain healthy.

questions.question: The concept of 'magnification' in microscopy is crucial for viewing cells. How does magnification fundamentally differ from 'resolution' in the context of studying cells?

options.option: Magnification enlarges objects, while resolution separates close objects.
Magnification improves contrast, while resolution sharpens focus.
Magnification depends on lenses, while resolution depends on power.
Magnification enlarges objects, while resolution increases brightness.

questions.explanation: Magnification enlarges the image of an object, making it appear bigger. Resolution, however, determines the level of detail visible by allowing two closely spaced points to be seen as separate. High resolution is essential for clearly observing fine cell structures.

questions.question: How do electron microscopes achieve 'remarkable clarity' in viewing cell structures at the nanometre scale, differentiating them from light microscopes?

options.option: By utilizing visible light passing through the specimen.
By using convex lenses for greater magnification.
By using an electron beam for higher magnification and resolution.
By observing objects under reduced atmospheric pressure.

questions.explanation: Electron microscopes use beams of electrons, which have much shorter wavelengths than visible light. This enables them to achieve far greater magnification and resolution than light microscopes, allowing scientists to observe cell structures and organelles at the nanometre scale.

subtopics.name: Cell Theory — The Unifying Principle of Biology

questions.question: An observer uses a microscope with a field of view diameter of 5000 µm. If 50 cells are counted along the diameter, what is the estimated real size of one cell?
questions.explanation: The estimated size of one cell is calculated by dividing the field of view diameter by the number of cells across it: 5000 µm ÷ 50 = 100 µm. Therefore, the approximate real size of each cell is 100 µm.

questions.question: If a light microscope has an eyepiece with 10X magnifying power and an objective lens with 20X magnifying power, what would be the total magnification?
questions.explanation: The total magnification of a microscope is found by multiplying the magnification of the eyepiece lens by that of the objective lens. With a 10X eyepiece and a 20X objective lens, the total magnification is 10 × 20 = 200X.

questions.question: Why are cells generally not visible to the unaided human eye?

options.option: Because they are smaller than the eye's resolution limit.

questions.explanation: Most cells are microscopic and measure only a few micrometres in size, which is below the resolving power of the unaided human eye. Therefore, microscopes are needed to observe cells and study their structures in detail.

questions.question: The total magnification of a microscope is determined by combining the magnifying power of which two components?

options.option: Fine adjustment knob and Coarse adjustment knob

questions.explanation: The total magnification of a microscope is calculated by multiplying the magnifying power of the eyepiece lens by that of the objective lens. For example, a 10x eyepiece and a 40x objective lens provide a total magnification of 400x.

questions.question: What unit conversion is given for one millimetre (mm)?
questions.explanation: A millimetre and a micrometre are units of length in the metric system. Since 1 millimetre equals 1000 micrometres, a micrometre is one-thousandth of a millimetre. This conversion is commonly used when measuring very small objects and cells.

questions.question: What kind of microscope uses a beam of electrons to produce highly magnified images?
questions.explanation: An electron microscope uses a beam of electrons instead of light to create highly magnified and detailed images. Because electrons have much shorter wavelengths than visible light, these microscopes can reveal structures too small to be seen with ordinary microscopes.

questions.question: Who was the first person to observe a cell and in what year?
questions.explanation: Robert Hooke was the first scientist to observe and describe cells in 1665. While examining a thin slice of cork under a microscope, he saw tiny box-like compartments and named them “cells,” laying the foundation for cell biology.

questions.question: Vertical growth in plants takes place by :-
questions.explanation: Apical meristem is responsible for the growth of root and shoot of a plant. The division of these cells always results in primary (vertical) growth.

questions.question: Which of these components of blood fight infection?
questions.explanation: WBC, also called leucocyte are involved in the protection of the body against infectious diseases and foreign invaders.

questions.question: In desert plants, the rate of water loss gets reduced due to the presence of:-
questions.explanation: The cuticle is a thick waxy layer that prevents the loss of water from the surface of the leaf.

questions.question: Cartilage does not found in
questions.explanation: Cartilage is soft bone work as connective tissue found in ear, nose, and larynx.lt provides support and flexibility. The kidney is a muscular structure it is an excretory organ consist of nephrons.

questions.question: Which of these types of cells is most likely to divide?
questions.explanation:
The meristematic tissue is of the following types:

  1. Apical meristem (responsible for vertical growth of root and shoot)
  2. Lateral meristem (responsible for the increase in girth of a stem)
  3. Intercalary meristem (responsible for the branching in a plant).

The three types of growth take place due to division in tissues.

questions.question: Companion cells are associated with:-
questions.explanation: Companion cell is a specialized parenchyma cell, located in the phloem of flowering plant in close association with sieve tube.

questions.question: Which tissue has chloroplast in cells?
questions.explanation: Chlorenchyma is a parenchyma tissue, consist of chloroplasts and performs photosynthesis found in the mesophyll of leaves.

questions.question: The meristematic tissue is found:
questions.explanation: Meristematic tissue divides rapidly which results in the growth of root and shoot apex.

questions.question: Movement of the passage of food in the intestine is caused by the contraction of:
questions.explanation: Unstriated muscles also called involuntary or smooth muscles, do nor work under our control. It secretes enzymes and digestive juices hence perform digestion regularly.

questions.question: A long tubular outgrowth of a nerve cell which conducts impulses away from the cell body is termed as :-
questions.explanation: Axon

questions.question: What is the function of White Blood Corpuscles:
questions.explanation: WBC protects the body against diseases because they produce antibodies that help in fighting against invaders.

questions.question: A person met with an accident in which 2 long bones of hand were dislocated. Which among the following may be the possible reason?
questions.explanation: Ligament is a connective tissue that connects one bone to another .

questions.question: What constitutes thickening in collenchyma?
questions.explanation: Collenchyma cells are elongated cells with irregularly thick cell walls that provide support and structure. Their thick cell walls are composed of the compounds cellulose and pectin. These cells are often found under the epidermis or the outer layer of cells in young stems and in leaf veins.

questions.question: Cork is impervious to water because it has deposition of
questions.explanation: The walls of cork cells are heavily thickened with organic substance suberin. Suberin is a fatty, waxy substance that covers the thin walls of the cell and prevents the flow of water, gases, or any nutrients.

questions.question: Which of the following conducts water and minerals in plants?
questions.explanation: Xylem is composed of tracheids, vessels, xylem parenchyma, and xylem fibres. The cells of xylem are dead; except the cells of xylem parenchyma. Tracheids and vessels are tubular structures and thus they provide a channel for conduction of water and minerals.

questions.question: Squamous epithelium is found in _____
questions.explanation: Simple squamous epithelium lines the air sacs, or alveoli of the lungs. The alveoli are sites where the air is exchanged in the lungs. Simple squamous epithelial cells in the alveoli allow oxygen from the air to enter the blood in the capillaries of the lung. Carbon dioxide, a waste product, passes across the epithelium of the alveoli to be removed from the body.

questions.question: Large intercellular spaces are present in _____.
questions.explanation: There are large intercellular spaces between the cells. Intercellular spaces are filled with a large amount of extracellular materials formed of insoluble protein fibres lying in an amorphous, transparent ground substance called matrix. The ageing of an animal body is associated with deterioration in its connective tissues. Connective tissue supports and binds other tissues of the body.

questions.question: Ligament connects _____.
questions.explanation: A ligament is a fibrous connective tissue which attaches bone to bone, and usually serves to hold structures together and keep them stable.

questions.question: Fibrinogen is absent in _____.
questions.explanation: Plasma is that part of the blood, which contains blood clotting agent called as fibrinogen, while serum is the fluid part of the blood and does not contain clotting agent (fibrinogen). The plasma and serum can be extracted from the centrifugation of blood. The serum is obtained after the clotting of blood, while plasma can be obtained before the coagulation of the blood.

questions.question: Intercalated disc is present in ______.
questions.explanation: The heart of human beings is made up of cardiac muscles which are different from smooth and skeletal muscles. Inside the cardiac muscles are the intercalated discs which are known to join various adjacent cells together. There are a number of functions of intercalated discs. It is only when it functions properly that the heart can work effectively.

questions.question: Heart muscles are _____.
questions.explanation: Cardiac Muscles are commonly called as Heart muscles. These are cylindrical, branched, striated, and uni-nucleate and Involuntary in nature.

questions.question: Haversian canals are present in _____.
questions.explanation: Haversian canals are found in the bone matrix of a long bone, like humerus of mammals. Haversian canals contain artery and veins, osteoblasts in areolar tissue, nerves, and lymph. It is also called an osteon.

questions.question: Which of the following is non – vascular?
questions.explanation: Epithelial tissue is composed of cells laid together in sheets with the cells tightly connected to one another. Epithelial layers are non – vascular but innervated.

questions.question: Which of the following is a dead cell?
questions.explanation: Tracheids are dead and lignified cells. The deposition of lignin on the cell wall is responsible to form a different type of thickenings.

questions.question: Chloroplasts may occur in
questions.explanation: The main function of parenchyma is to provide support and to store food. In some plant parts, parenchyma has chlorophyll as well. In that case, parenchyma carries out photosynthesis and is then termed as chlorenchyma. Collenchyma tissue contains chloroplast and carry out photosynthesis.

questions.question: The plant tissue provides mechanical strength and consists of living cells, is
questions.explanation: Collenchyma cells are refractile, non lignified, living cells with pectocellulose thickening in specific areas of walls, and without intercellular spaces. It provides mechanical strength with flexibility thus allows bending. Besides, it shows growth and elongation of organs, photosynthesize, and stores food and prevents tearing of leaves.

questions.question: Which of the following tissues of plants provides flexibility and strength for bending and swaying?
questions.explanation: Collenchyma is a strong and flexible simple tissue to provide mechanical strength to the growing organs.

questions.question: Lignified or thickened cell wall is a characteristic feature of
questions.explanation: The xylem is one of the conductive tissues in plants. It is a complex tissue composed of many types of cells. The main function of xylem is to conduct water and minerals from roots to leaves. The secondary xylem also provides mechanical support due to the presence of a thick lignified cell wall.

questions.question: The conducting cells of xylem are
questions.explanation: There are two types of conducting cells in xylem, tracheids and vessel elements. Both have thick lignified secondary walls and are dead at maturity. These cells create hollow cylinders that have high tensile strength. Materials moving within the xylem are under tension.

questions.question: Bones are connected to muscles at the joints by
questions.explanation: The bone is a connective tissue with a hard matrix, composed of calcium and phosphorus. A bone is connected by muscle with connective tissue, called a tendon.

questions.question: _____ smoothens the bone surface at the joints.
questions.explanation: Cartilage smoothens the bone surface at the joints. The cartilage is a connective tissue with a solid matrix composed of proteins and sugars. It is commonly seen in the nose, ear, trachea, and larynx.

questions.question: Which of the following tissues has dead cells?
questions.explanation: Sclerenchyma Tissue makes the plant hard and stiff, thickened due to lignin and no inter cellular space. Cells of this tissue are dead and commonly seen in the husk of coconut.

questions.question: A person met with an accident in which two long bones of hand were dislocated. Which among the following may be the possible reason?
questions.explanation: Dislocation of joint occurs when there is an abnormal separation in joint, which are held together by ligament. Therefore ligament break may result in dislocation of bone. Areolar tissues join skin to muscles, fills spaces inside organs and is found around muscles, blood vessels and nerves. Hence are not concerned with bones.

questions.question: Meristematic tissues in plants are
questions.explanation: Meristematic tissues consists of actively dividing cells and is present in the growing regions of plants, e.g., the tips of roots and stems. The cells of meristematic tissue are round, oval, polygonal or rectangular. They are packed closely without intercellular spaces, have thin cellulose walls, dense cytoplasm and prominent nuclei. Vacuoles are almost absent in such cells because they are completely filled with sap.

questions.question: Which is not a function of epidermis?
questions.explanation: Skin has three layers: The epidermis, the outermost layer of skin, provides a waterproof barrier and creates our skin tone. The dermis, beneath the epidermis, contains tough connective tissue, hair follicles, and sweat glands. The deeper subcutaneous tissue (hypodermis) is made of fat and connective tissue

questions.question: Cartilage is not found in
questions.explanation: The cartilage is a connective tissue with solid matrix composed of proteins and sugars. It is commonly seen in nose, ear, trachea, and larynx. Cartilage is not found in kidney.

questions.question: Fats are stored in human body as
questions.explanation: Adipose connective tissue is filled with fat globules for the storage of fat. It acts as insulator.

questions.question: Bone matrix is rich in
questions.explanation: The bone is a connective tissue with hard matrix, composed of calcium and phosphorus. A bone is connected by another bone with another connective tissue called ligaments. A bone is connected by muscle with another connective tissue called tendon.

questions.question: Contractile proteins are found in
questions.explanation: Contractile proteins are found in muscles, as they are associated with the movement of body or limbs. The contraction and relaxation of contractile proteins, present in muscles bring about movements of limbs, internal organs, etc.

questions.question: Voluntary muscles are found in
questions.explanation: Voluntary muscles are the muscles, which are under our complete control for, e.g., the working and movement of limbs. On the other hand involuntary muscles are controlled by hypothalamus, i.e., they are regulated rhythmically, e.g., alimentary canal, iris of the eye and bronchi of lungs.

questions.question: Nervous tissue is not found in
questions.explanation: Tendons are the connective tissue, which join skeletal muscles to bones, hence nervous tissue is absent is them. Nervous tissue is specialised to transmit messages within our body. It contains highly specialised unit cells called nerve cells or neurons. They have the ability to receive stimuli from within or outside the body and to conduct (send) impulses (signals) to different parts of the body. Brain, spinal cord are all associated with the regulation of body activity and are composed of nervous tissue.

questions.question: Nerve cell does not contain
questions.explanation:
Each nerve cell or neuron Is composed of three parts

  1. Cyton or cell body It contains central nucleus and cytoplasm with characteristic deeply stained particles called Nissl’s granules (i.e., clumps of ribosome).
  2. Dendron These are short processes arising from cyton and further branching into dendrites.
  3. Axon It is a single long cylindrical process of uniform diameter which forms fine branches terminally. The dendrites receives impulses and the axon takes impulses away from the cell body.

questions.question: The columnar epithelial cells possess cilia in
questions.explanation: Columnar epithelium in the intestine and columnar epithelium with cilia in the lining of the respiratory tract.

questions.question: Branched involuntary muscles fibres are found in
questions.explanation: Involuntary muscles are found in walls of hollow tubular organs like an alimentary canal, ducts of glands, urogenital ducts, and blood vessels except the heart. They show slow contractions but remain contracted for a long period of time.

questions.question: Chlorenchyma and aerenchyma are modified/specialised
questions.explanation: Parenchyma cells containing chloroplasts are collectively termed as chlorenchyma. Special parenchyma tissue is found in the aquatic plants and some land plants (e.g., petiole of Banana, Canna). It is known as aerenchyma. It consists of a network of parenchyma cells that enclose very large air cavities.

questions.question: The substance found in the cell wall of cork or bark that makes it impervious to water is
questions.explanation: The cork is impervious to water due to suberin deposition in the cell wall of its cells. The walls of cork cells are heavily thickened with an organic substance, suberin. Suberin makes these cells impervious to water and gases.

questions.question: The extremely thin and flat cells forming a delicate lining in the lung alveoli constitute
questions.explanation: Tongue, esophagus, and the lining of the mouth are made up of simple squamous epithelium. It is also found in blood vessels and alveoli. It protects the underlying parts of the body from mechanical injury, entry of germs, chemicals, and drying. It also forms a selectively permeable surface through which filtration occurs.

questions.question: The mineral elements found in our bone making it hard, are
questions.explanation: Bone cells are embedded in a hard matrix, which is strengthened by fibers, and hardened by calcium and phosphorus salts. The matrix is deposited in the form of concentric layers of lamellae formed around a central Haversian canal.

questions.question: Glands in our body are formed by
questions.explanation: A gland is a group of cells. Epithelial tissues are thin tissues that cover all the exposed surfaces of the body. They form the external skin, the inner lining of the mouth, digestive tract, secretory glands, the lining of hollow parts of every organ such as the heart, lungs, eyes, ears, the urogenital tract, as well as the ventricular system of the brain and central canals of the spinal cord.

questions.question: The epithelium is separated from the underlying connective tissue by
questions.explanation: Epithelial tissues are physically separated from underlying connective tissues by a basement membrane or basal lamina. The portion of an epithelial cell attached to the basement membrane is called its basal surface.

questions.question: Tendons help to connect
questions.explanation: A tendon is a fibrous connective tissue that attaches muscle to bone. Tendons may also attach muscles to structures such as the eyeball.

questions.question: Most of the metabolic functions of plants are carried out by
questions.explanation: Parenchyma carries out most of the plant's metabolism. They are "lifetime dividers" and important in healing. They also store starch, oils, and water (H2O).

questions.question: The mechanical strength and rigidity of the cell wall is due to
questions.explanation: The cell walls of sclerenchyma are greatly thickened due to the presence of lignin. Lignin is a complex polymer with high tensile strength e., it does not break easily on stretching and high compressional strength, it does not buckle easily.

questions.question: Lignified, narrow, elongated and dead cells are found in
questions.explanation: Sclerenchyma tissue is dead simple permanent tissue of the plant. The cells of sclerenchyma are closely packed without intercellular spaces, like tiles in the mosaic floor so that, it can provide the strength, rigidity, flexibility, and elasticity to the plant to withstand various strains.

questions.question: Which of the following sets includes simple permanent tissues?
questions.explanation: Two simple permanent tissue in plants are parenchyma and collenchyma while two complex permanent tissue in plants are xylem and phloem. Sclerenchyma tissue is dead simple permanent tissue of the plant.

questions.question: Rhythmic contraction and relaxation throughout life, are shown by
questions.explanation: Cardiac muscles are present in the heart. They contract and relax rapidly, rhythmically, and tirelessly. They help to pump the blood to various parts of the body.

questions.question: Those organs of the body like oral cavity, esophagus, etc., which are subjected to mechanical abrasions are lined by
questions.explanation:
A nonkeratinizing stratified squamous epithelium is found at three prominent sites in the animal body:

  1. lining the esophagus,
  2. lining the sides and floor of the oral cavity, and
  3. lining the vagina.

questions.question: Which of the following helps in repair of tissue and fills up the space inside the organ?
questions.explanation: Areolar tissues join skin to muscles, fills spaces inside organs and is found around muscles, blood vessels and nerves. Hence are not concerned with bones.

questions.question: The muscular tissue which function throughout the life continuously without fatigue is
questions.explanation: Cardiac muscles show characteristics of both smooth and striated muscles. These muscles are striated, cylindrical, branched and involuntary in nature. In addition to the light and dark bands, these muscles show densely stained cross bands called intercalated dises. These act as impulse boosters. Cardiac muscles contract and relax rapidly, rhythmically and tirelessly throughout a lifetime, from early embryonic stage until death.

questions.question: Which of the following cells is found in the cartilaginous tissue of the body?
questions.explanation: The matrix of cartilage has delicate network of collagen fibres and chondrocytes (living cells). These are present in fluid-filled spaces called lacunae. Chondrocytes multiply by mitosis and help in internal growth of cartilage.

questions.question: The dead element present in the phloem is
questions.explanation: Phloem fibres are thick walled, elongated spindle shaped dead cells which possess narrow lumen. They provide mechanical support to the tissue. Phloem parenchyma are thin walled-living cells of parenchyma. They have two functions, storage and lateral food conduction.

questions.question: Which of the following does not loss their nucleus at maturity?
questions.explanation: Companion cells are present along the sieve tube, connected to them via plasmodesmata. These cells are metabolically active and sieve tube elements are dependent on these cells they do not lose nucleus at maturity. RBC vessels and sieve tube cells lose their nucleus at maturity.

questions.question: In desert plants, rate of water loss gets reduced due to the presence of
questions.explanation: Cuticle are protective, hydrophobic waxy covering produced by epidermal cells of leaves, young shoots and other aerial parts. It minimises the water loss through transpiration (with the help of stomata) and also reduces pathogen entry.

questions.question: A long tree has several branches. The tissue that helps in the side ways conduction of water in the branches is
questions.explanation: Xylem vessels are very long tube-like structures formed by a row of cells placed end to end. The transverse walls between these cells are partially or completely dissolved to form continuous water channels.

questions.question: If the tip of sugarcane plant is removed from the field, even then it keeps on growing in length. It is due to the presence of
questions.explanation: If the tip of sugarcane plant is removed the apical meristem is also removed as it is situated in the apices of growing roots and stem. Intercalary meristem are located at the base of leaves or nodes and leads to the increase in the length of an organ such as leaves and internodes.

questions.question: A nail is inserted in the trunk of a tree at a height of 1 metre from the ground level. After 3 years the nail will
questions.explanation: When a nail is inserted in the trunk of a tree at a height of 1 metre from the ground, even after 3 year the nail remains at same level. It does not moves upwards as the apical meristem responsible for growth (length) is present in the apices only and lateral meristem responsible of increase in girth will lead to no change in length.

questions.question: Parenchyma cells are
questions.explanation: Parenchyma cells form the bulk of the plant body. Its cells are living and they possess the power of division. The cells are rounded or isodiametric, i.e., equally expanded on all sides. The cells are oval, round, polygonal or elongated in shape with a thin cell wall. It encloses a dense cytoplasm, which contains small nucleus and surrounds large central vacoule.

questions.question: Flexibility in plants is due to
questions.explanation: Collenchyma consist of living cells and are characterised by the presence of cellulose. Collenchyma is a mechanical tissue in young dicotyledonous stems and provides mechanical support and elasticity. It provides great tensile strength with flexibility to those organs in which it is found. It allows easy bending in various parts of a plant mainly young growing stem without breaking them.

questions.question: Cork cells are made impervious to water and gases by the presence of
questions.explanation: The walls of cork cells are heavily thickened with an organic substance, suberin. Suberin makes these cells impervious to water and gases.

questions.question: Survival of plants in terrestrial environment has been made possible by the presence of
questions.explanation: The conducting tissues in plants conduct different saps and have different structures. The primary conducting tissues of plants are xylem and phloem. Xylem conducts water from roots to the other parts of the plant, whereas phloem transports food and other material from the leaves to other parts of plants.

questions.question: The water conducting tissue generally present in gymnosperm is
questions.explanation: The gymnosperms are characterised by the presence of tracheids as their major conducting tissue. These are elongated dead cells with hard lignified walls. They conduct water and do not have open ends like the vessels.

questions.question: The number of cellular layers keeps on changing in
questions.explanation: The stratified epithelium contains more than one layer of cells i.e. 2 to 20 or more layers. Skin is an example of stratified epithelium. Stratification of layers prevents wear and tear. The number of cellular layers keeps on changing in the stratified epithelium.

questions.question: In the stratified epithelium, the basement membrane is in contact with

options.option: the second layer of cells from the top
the second layer of cells from base

questions.explanation: Stratified epithelium contains two or more layers of epithelial cells with the cells in the deepest (basal) layer in contact with the basement membrane. The multiple cell layers make it strong and capable of resisting stress and protecting underlying tissue.

questions.question: Actively diving cells are present in
questions.explanation: Cambium in plants is a layer of actively dividing cells between xylem (wood) and phloem (bast) tissues that are responsible for the secondary growth of stems and roots.

questions.question: A permanent tissue is described as complex tissue when it has

options.option: more than one type of cells, performing different functions
one type of cells, performing two or more different functions
more than one type of cells, performing one common function
one type of cells, performing one function

questions.explanation: The complex tissue consists of more than one type of cell. All the cells coordinate to perform a common function. they transport water, minerals salts, food material to various parts of the plant body.

questions.question: Smooth muscle consists of __________ filaments that are not arranged into sarcomeres giving it a non-striated pattern.
questions.explanation: Smooth muscle consists of thick and thin filaments that are not arranged into sarcomeres giving it a non-striated pattern.

questions.question: ________ connects two bones at a joint.
questions.explanation: A bone is connected with another bone by a connective tissue called ligaments. A bone is connected by muscle with another connective tissue called a tendon.

questions.question: ________ in the cell wall of cork/bark makes it impervious to water.
questions.explanation: The cork is impervious to water due to suberin deposition in the cell wall of its cells. The walls of cork cells are heavily thickened with an organic substance, suberin. Suberin makes these cells impervious to water and gases.

questions.question: The simple permanent tissue that consists of thick-walled, narrow, elongated, dead cells is ________.
questions.explanation: Sclerenchyma Cells are long, thick-walled, and lignified with tapering ends. These are fiber-like in appearance and also known as sclerenchymatous fiber. These are dead cells and perform a mechanical function.

questions.question: The common characteristic of xylem tracheids and sieve tubes is that both are:
questions.explanation: Xylem is composed of tracheids, vessels, xylem parenchyma, and xylem fibres. Tracheids and vessels are tubular structures and thus they provide a channel for conduction of water and minerals. Phloem is composed of sieve tubes, companion cells, phloem fibre and phloem parenchyma. Sieve tubes are tubular cells with perforated walls. Sieve tubes are the conducting elements of phloem.

questions.question: We use glycerine in temporary mount of the material because:

options.option: it increases the clarity of the material
it provides the medium for floating the material

questions.explanation: Glycerine is used for temporary mounting as it is hygroscopic and does not allow the mounted material to dry up.

questions.question: Which of the following components are seen in a slide of human cheek cell when observed under a microscope?
questions.explanation: The cells do not have a cell wall. However, each cell has a thin cell membrane. A large vacuole is present at the center of each cell and is surrounded by the cytoplasm. A lightly stained cytoplasm is observed in each cell. A deeply stained nucleus is observed at the center of each cell.

questions.question: Roshini is making a temporary mount of onion peel. What precautions should be taken to avoid the entry of air bubbles in the slide?

options.option: Peel should be immersed in stain for over an hour
Coverslip should be gently dropped over the peel
Coverslip should be dropped on to the peel from a certain height
Peel should be allowed to fold over itself once or twice

questions.explanation:
Precautions:

  1. The staining of the peel must be appropriate. Excess stain can be removed by rinsing the peel with water taken in the watch glass.
  2. Always hold the slide by its edges to avoid making the slide dirty.
  3. Use a brush to transfer the peel to the slide.
  4. The peel should never be folded.
  5. The peel should be mounted in the centre of the slide.
  6. Always keep the coverslip gently to avoid the entry of air bubbles.

questions.question: The extra stain from the slide if present is removed by:
questions.explanation: The staining of the peel must be appropriate. Excess stain can be removed by rinsing the peel with water taken in the watch glass. The extra stain from the slide if present is removed by soaking with filter paper. Filter paper absorbs the excess water and stain.

questions.question: To prepare a mount of human cheek cell, the sample is collected from:

options.option: inner side of cheek with a blade
outer side of cheek with a blade
inner side of cheek with a toothpick
outer side of cheek with a toothpick

questions.explanation: While preparing a mount of human cheek cell, the sample is collected from the inner side of the cheek using a toothpick, which will collect some cheek cells.

questions.question: A student used a red stain for mounting a peel of onion. This corresponds to the stain:
questions.explanation:
A student used a red stain for mounting a peel of onion This corresponds to the stain Safranin.

  1. It is used in histology and cytology.
  2. The formula of Safranin is  C20H19N4+Cl-
  3. The molar mass of Safranin is 350.85 g·mol⁻¹
  4. It is soluble in water.

questions.question: Which one of the following is not visible in the cheek cells?
questions.explanation: The cells do not have a cell wall. However, each cell has a thin cell membrane. A large vacuole is present at the center of each cell and is surrounded by the cytoplasm. A lightly stained cytoplasm is observed in each cell. A deeply stained nucleus is observed at the center of each cell.

questions.question: On observing onion peel slide under low power of compound microscope which set of structures are clearly seen.
questions.explanation:
1. There are a large number of regularly shaped cells lying side by side and each cell has a distinct cell wall.
  2. A distinct nucleus is present on the periphery of each cell.
  3. A lightly stained cytoplasm is observed in each cell.
  4. A large vacuole is present at the center of each cell and is surrounded by the cytoplasm.

questions.question: You are viewing a prepared slide of striated muscle fibres from a cockroach leg. When you focus the microscope, the striations appear pale and indistinct. To make the stations clearly visible, you would:

options.option: remove the mirror to cut out light
replace the objective to decrease the magnification
slowly close the diaphragm to reduce the light

questions.explanation: To make the stations clearly visible slowly close the diaphragm to reduce the light. The reduction of light gives a better contrast.

questions.question: Which among the following helps to increase the diameter or girth of plant organs like stem?
questions.explanation: Lateral meristem is responsible for increasing the girth or circumference of the root or stem in a plant. It is found parallel to the long axis in a plant body. The secondary growth in this meristem enhances the girth more than the overall length of the plant. Apical meristem is responsible for the increase of girth of the root.

questions.question: Which of the following statements is the best description of the composition of blood?
questions.explanation:
Blood is classified as connective tissue and consists of two main components:

  1. Plasma, which is a clear extracellular fluid.
  2. Formed elements, which are made up of the blood cells and platelets.

Formed elements are:

  • Erythrocytes, also known as red blood cells (RBCs)
  • Leukocytes, also known as white blood cells (WBCs)
  • Platelets

questions.question: The smooth muscle consists of _______________. Each fibre contains a single oval nucleus in its thick middle part. The cross-striations are absent so that the fibres look smooth, hence the name unstriated.
questions.explanation: The smooth muscle consists of long, narrow unbranched spindle-shaped fibres. Each fibre contains a single oval nucleus in its thick middle part. The cross-striations are absent so that the fibres look smooth, hence the name unstriated.

questions.question: Name the muscle which is found in visceral organs.
questions.explanation: Visceral muscle tissue, or smooth muscle, is tissue associated with the internal organs of the body, especially those in the abdominal cavity.

questions.question: The efferent part of neuron is _____
questions.explanation: In the peripheral nervous system, an efferent nerve fiber is the axon of a motor neuron.

questions.question: Tongue is made up of _____.
questions.explanation: The tongue is under voluntary control via the somatic nervous system, therefore it is made up of skeletal muscle.  Skeletal muscle is found in other places as well - not just attached to the bones.

questions.question: Which cell does not have perforated cell wall?
questions.explanation: Tracheids and vessels are xylem elements and are concerned with the transport of water. They are long tube-like structures with partially or completely dissolved walls to form water pipes (in vessels) and pits in cell wall (in tracheids) for conducting water. Sieve tubes are slender tube-like structures with their end walls perforated by numerous pores and are called sieve plates. They are phloem elements and are main food conducting elements. Companion cells possess numerous mitochondria and ribosomes and are supporting units of sieve tubes.

questions.question: The end of a long bone is connected to another bone by
questions.explanation: Two bones can be connected to each other by another type of connective tissue called the ligament.

questions.question: Cambium is an example of
questions.explanation: Lateral meristem is present along the side of the stem, vascular cambium in the gymnosperms is a good example of the lateral meristem.

questions.question: Ligaments and Tendons are formed of
questions.explanation: Two bones can be connected to each other by another type of connective tissue called the ligament. Tendons connect bones to muscles and are another type of connective tissue.

questions.question: One of the following is not a characteristic feature of parenchyma tissue. That feature is:

options.option: large cells are placed together with intercellular spaces
a large single vacuole is present in each cell

questions.explanation:
Intercellular spaces are present between the cells. Cells are thin-walled (primary wall only except wood parenchyma), with large vacuole and wide intercellular spaces. Cells of Parenchyma tissue are isodiametric.
Cells are thick at the corners is not a characteristic feature of parenchyma tissue.

questions.question: While preparing a temporary mount of cheek cells, teacher asked Rohit to pick up the stain. He had four bottles A, B, C, and D containing methylene blue, glycerine, distilled water, Canada balsum. Which one should he pick?
questions.explanation: Methylene blue is used to stain human cheek epithelial cells better. Methylene blue stains negatively charged molecules in the cell, including DNA and RNA. This dye is toxic when ingested and it causes irritation when in contact with the skin and eyes.

questions.question: Choose the chemical compound with which the specimen is temporarily mounted.
questions.explanation: Glycerine is a good dehydrating agent. It avoids the drying of the specimen. Besides, glycerine tends to reflect light due to its refractive nature. As a result of it, the image appears clearer under the microscope. Due to these reasons, glycerine is used while preparing a temporary mount of leaf peel.

questions.question: The cell appear elongated, tapering at ends as observed under a microscope. It is
questions.explanation:
There are two types of sclerenchyma cells:

  1. fibres which are elongated cells with tapering ends and
  2. sclereids (also called stone cells), which are roughly isodiametric cells with narrow cavities.

questions.question: Which of the following tissues divide continuously and are found on growing regions of the plants?
questions.explanation: Meristematic tissues divide continuously and are found in growing regions of the plants. Thus they are responsible for increasing the length and girth of the plant.

questions.question: Parenchyma which contains chlorophyll and helps in photosynthesis is called ________.
questions.explanation: Parenchyma tissues which contain chlorophyll and helps in photosynthesis are called chlorenchyma.

questions.question: Which of the following is not a function of the epidermis?
questions.explanation: The epidermis does not conduct the food to the various parts of the plant. Conduction of food is carried by phloem tissue.

questions.question: Girth of stem increases due to
questions.explanation: The lateral meristematic tissues are responsible for an increase in the diameter or girth of the plant.

questions.question:
Which muscles act involuntarily?

  1. Striated muscles
  2. Smooth muscles
  3. Cardiac muscles
  4. Skeletal muslces
questions.explanation: The muscles which are not under the control of our will, are called involuntary muscles. Smooth (unstriated) muscles and cardiac muscles are involuntary muscles.

questions.question: Which connective tissue supports and provides flexibility to the body parts?
questions.explanation: Cartilage is a connective tissue that provides support and flexibility to the body parts. It smoothens bone surfaces at joints. It is also present in the nose, ear, trachea, and larynx.

questions.question: Which is the most widely distributed connective tissue?
questions.explanation: Areolar connective tissue is the simplest and most widely distributed connective tissue. Areolar connective tissue is found between the skin and muscles, around blood vessels and nerves, and in the bone marrow.

questions.question: The walls of cork cells are thickened by the deposition of an organic substance which make these cells impermeable to water and gases. This substance is
questions.explanation: Cork cells are dead cells without intercellular spaces. Their cell wall is very thick due to the deposition of an organic substance called suberin, which is impermeable to water and gases.

questions.question: Which of the following statements regarding apical meristem is incorrect?

options.option: It develops by dedifferentiation of permanent tissues.
It brings about increase in length of the root and stem.

questions.explanation: Apical meristem is present at the root, shoots apex and is always a meristematic tissue and not dedifferentiated and is responsible for primary growth.

questions.question:
Select the correct statement regarding meristematic tissues.

  1. They are made up of mature cells and are fully differentiated.
  2. They have cells with thick secondary walls.
  3. They perform the function of growth and division.
  4. Both they are made up of mature cells and are fully differentiated and they perform the function of growth and division.
questions.explanation: Meristematic tissues are plant tissues found at specific locations of the plant body and performs the function of growth and division. They are simple tissues composed of a group of similar and immature cells which divide to form new cells. They have cells with thin and elastic primary walls and abundant cytoplasm.

questions.question: X is a living tissue that provides mechanical support, tensile strength and elasticity in young dicotyledonous stem. X is ________.
questions.explanation: Collenchyma is a living tissue that provides mechanical support, tensile strength and elasticity in young dicotyledonous stem.

questions.question: The tissue shown here is ________.
questions.explanation: The figure shown here is of adipose tissue. It is primarily a fat storing tissue in which the matrix is packed with adipocytes. The tissue is found beneath the skin, in the covering of heart, around the blood vessels and kidneys, etc.

questions.question: Which of the following plant tissues provide mechanical support and a protective covering around seeds?
questions.explanation: Sclerenchyma consists of thick walled dead cells and their cell walls are thickened due to the deposition of a water resistant material lignin. Main function of sclerenchyma is to provide mechanical support to plant parts and it also provides protective covering around seeds.

questions.question: Which of the following epithelial tissues, lines the stomach and intestine?
questions.explanation: Columnar epithelium consists of pillar-like cells that are much taller than wide. It lines the stomach, intestine, etc.

questions.question:
Which of the following are correctly matched?

  1. Dense regular - Blubber connective tissue
  2. Cartilage - Pinnae of ear
  3. Areolar tissue - Packaging tissue
  4. Cuboidal epithelium - Kidney tubules
  5. Cardiac muscle - Branched and multinucleate
  6. Striated muscle - Unbranched and uninucleate
questions.explanation: Blubber of whales is a type of adipose tissue. Adipose tissue is a type of loose connective tissue. Cardiac muscles are present in the wall of heart. They are uninucleate and branched whereas striated muscles are attached to the bones, they are multinucleate and unbranched.

questions.question: Pankaj was asked to draw a labelled diagram of scierenchyma tissue as seen in longitudinal section. The figure he drew is shown here. Point out the mistake in the figure.

options.option: Instead of simple pit pore, it should be bordered pit.
The cell walls are not lignified in scierenchyma.
The nucleus and cytoplasm are not shown.
The figure shows transverse section instead of longitudinal section of tissue.

questions.explanation: The figure shows transverse section of sclerenchyma tissue. It is a simple supportive tissue of highly thick-walled cells with little or no cytoplasm. The thickening of cell walls may be due to deposition of lignin or both lignin and cellulose. The adjacent cells possess simple oblique pits.

questions.question: The tissue shown in the given figure is

options.option: Angular collenchyma, in which wall thickenings are present at the angles
Scierenchyma, in which uniform wall thickenings are present
Xylem vessel that forms long channels for conduction of water and minerals

questions.explanation: Figure shows Xylem vessel that forms long channels for conduction of water and minerals

questions.question:
Match column I with column II and select the correct option from the given codes.
 

Column I Column II
(a)  (i) Phagocytosis
(b)  (ii) Release heparin
(c)  (iii) Allergic response
questions.explanation: The given figures (a), (b) and (c) represent eosinophil, monocyte and basophil respectively.

questions.question: Which of the following statements is correct about the cell shown in figure?

options.option: P conducts impulse away from cyton while S conducts impulse towards cyton.
P receives nerve impulse from Q and conveys it to other cells.
