questions.explanation: In the given figure, ‘P represent dendrites, ‘Q’ is neurilemma, ‘R’ is node of Ranvier and ‘S’ is axon ending. The dendrites receive impulses while axon takes impulses away from the cell body or cyton.

questions.question:
Select the incorrect differences between collenchyma and scierenchyma.

  Collenchyma Scierenchyma
(i) It is made up of dead cells. Its cells are living
(ii) Lumen or cell cavity is wide. Lumen or cell cavity is usually narrow.
(iii) It provides hardness to the region where it occurs. It keeps the organ soft.
(iv) Wall thickening is not uniform. Wall thickening is uniform.
(v) Pits are simple and straight Pits are simple, oblique and may be branched.
questions.explanation: In plants, collenchyma is a permanent living tissue of primary body. It provides flexibility to soft aerial parts like leaves, young stem of plant so that they can bend without breaking. Sclerenchyma consists of thick-walled, dead cells. It provides hardness to the region where it occurs.

questions.question: Observe the given figures of three different plant tissues and select the correct statement.

options.option: Cell walls of a, b and c bear uniform thickening of suberin.
a provides mechanical strength to the plant while b and c serve as storage tissues.
a and b consist of living cells while c consists of dead cells.
Cytoplasm is present in cells of a while it is absent in cells of b and c.

questions.explanation: In the given figure of plant tissues ‘a’ and ‘b’ represent parenchymatous and collenchymatous tissues respectively that are living tissues whereas figure ‘c’ represents sclerenchymatous tissue which comprises of dead cells.

questions.question:
Match column I with column II and select the correct option from the given codes.

Column I Column II
(a) Food storage (i) Phloem
(b) Food conduction (ii) Xylem
(c) Mechanical strength (iii) Parenchyma
(d) Water conduction (iv) Sclerenchyma
questions.explanation:
(a) - (iii), (b) - (i), (c) - (iv), (d) - (ii)

Column I Column II
(a) Food storage Parenchyma
(b) Food conduction Phloem
(c) Mechanical strength Sclerenchyma
(d) Water conduction Xylem

subtopics.name: Why are Plant and Animal Tissues Different?

questions.question: Why are the epithelial tissues that line an animal’s internal organs usually only one or a few cells thick?

options.option: To allow quick exchange of materials across them.

questions.explanation: Epithelial tissues lining internal organs are thin so that diffusion of substances (like gases, nutrients, wastes) can occur rapidly and efficiently across them.

questions.question: If a plant is unable to transport food from leaves to roots which tissue is malfunctioning?
questions.explanation:
• Phloem transports food (sugars) from leaves to other parts like roots.
  • Xylem transports water and minerals (not food).
  • Epidermis is protective tissue.
  • Sclerenchyma provides support.

questions.question: Meristematic tissues divide repeatedly. What property of their cells allows them to do this?

options.option: They have thin walls, dense cytoplasm and large prominent nucleus.
They contain large vacuoles that store nutrients.

questions.explanation:
Meristematic cells are actively dividing cells. For continuous division:

  • Thin cell walls allow easy division
  • Dense cytoplasm supports high metabolic activity
  • Large nucleus controls rapid cell division

They lack large vacuoles and are not differentiated, which keeps them capable of dividing repeatedly.

questions.question: What is the primary function of fixed joints found in the skull?

options.option: To enable circular movements of the neck
To allow flexible movement of facial muscles
To connect the arm to the skeleton
To protect the brain, eyes, and ears by preventing bone movement

questions.explanation: Fixed joints in the skull are immovable and serve the crucial role of protecting vital organs like the brain, eyes, and ears by securing the skull bones together.

questions.question: The joint connecting the skull to the backbone, which enables side-to-side head movement, is classified as what type of joint?
questions.explanation: The pivot joint connects the skull to the backbone and facilitates rotational movements such as turning the head from side to side.

questions.question: What type of joint is found at the elbow and knee, permitting movement in only one direction like a door hinge?
questions.explanation: Hinge joints, such as those in the elbow and knee, are designed to allow movement primarily in one plane, similar to a door hinge.

questions.question: Which type of joint allows forward, backward, sideways, and circular movements, such as the movement of the arm at the shoulder?
questions.explanation: The ball and socket joint, exemplified by the shoulder joint, is characterized by its ability to allow movements in multiple directions: forward, backward, sideways, and circular.

questions.question: Which bone is located at the front, to which the ribs are attached?
questions.explanation: The ribs are attached to the spine at the back and to the breast bone (sternum) in the front.

questions.question: What is the flexible column extending from the base of the skull called?
questions.explanation: From the base of the skull, it extends a flexible column called the backbone or vertebral column (spine).

questions.question: How many pairs of ribs are typically found in the human body?
questions.explanation: Humans typically have 12 pairs of ribs.

questions.question: What is the function of the cartilage disc located between each vertebra?

options.option: To protect internal spinal cord and allow flexibility

questions.explanation: Between each vertebra is a cartilage disc, which acts as a cushion and allows flexibility, so we can bend and twist without injuring the internal spinal cord.

questions.question: Which component of the skeletal system protects the heart and lungs?
questions.explanation: The rib cage acts like a protective cage to protect vital organs, such as the heart and lungs.

questions.question: What is the role of muscles in movement?

options.option: To produce movement by pulling on bones
To support delicate organs without moving them

questions.explanation: Muscles pull on bones to produce movement.

questions.question: Which system controls the functions of the musculoskeletal system?
questions.explanation: The musculoskeletal system functions under the control of the nervous system.

questions.question: What are the strong, flexible bands that attach muscles to bones?
questions.explanation: Muscles are attached to bones by strong, flexible bands called tendons.

questions.question: What percentage of an adult human's body weight is typically made up by the skeleton?
questions.explanation: On average, the adult human skeleton makes up about 12-15 percent of body weight.

questions.question: Which components are part of the musculoskeletal system?

options.option: Bones, muscles, joints, cartilage, tendons, and ligaments

questions.explanation: The musculoskeletal system is explicitly stated to be made up of bones, muscles, joints, cartilage, tendons, and ligaments.

questions.question: Which type of muscle tissue is characterized by unbranched, multinucleate, and striated cells, and is responsible for voluntary movements?
questions.explanation: Skeletal muscles are attached to the skeleton, control voluntary movements, and are composed of unbranched, multinucleate, and striated muscle fibres.

questions.question: The red color of blood is attributed to which component?
questions.explanation: Haemoglobin, an iron-rich protein present in Red Blood Cells (RBCs), is responsible for the red color of blood.

questions.question: What is the primary role of platelets in the human body?
questions.explanation: Platelets are crucial for initiating blood clotting at the site of an injury, preventing excessive blood loss.

questions.question: Which of the following functions is not primarily associated with epithelial tissues?
questions.explanation: Epithelial tissues are involved in protection, absorption, secretion, and sensory functions, but not in the transport of oxygen to tissues, which is a primary function of blood, a connective tissue.

questions.question: A farmer observes that the grass in his field quickly regrows after grazing by animals. Which type of meristematic tissue is primarily responsible for this phenomenon?

options.option: Apical meristem ensures the growth from the tips of grass blades.
Vascular meristem enhances nutrient transport for quick recovery.
Intercalary meristem located at the nodes allows for rapid regeneration from the base.
Lateral meristem increases the width of individual grass blades.

questions.explanation: Intercalary meristems, present at the nodes of grass stems, allow for regrowth and regeneration after being mowed or grazed by animals, enabling the grass to quickly recover.

questions.question: The process by which meristematic tissue cells undergo changes in structure and function to become permanent tissues is known as:
questions.explanation: Differentiation is the process where newly formed meristematic cells lose the ability to divide and become specialized to perform specific functions, forming permanent tissues.

questions.question: Which of the following characteristics is typical of meristematic tissue cells?

options.option: Small size, dense cytoplasm, and prominent nucleus.

questions.explanation: Cells of meristematic tissues are typically small, have thin cell walls, a large and prominent nucleus, dense cytoplasm, and are tightly packed with little or no intercellular space, facilitating continuous cell division.

questions.question: What is the primary function of the lateral meristem in plants?

options.option: To increase the girth or diameter of the stem.
To regrow parts after cutting or grazing.
To increase the height of the stem.

questions.explanation: Lateral meristems are responsible for the increase in the girth or diameter of stems due to their actively dividing cells arranged in a ring.

questions.question: Which type of meristematic tissue is primarily responsible for the increase in the length of a plant's stem and roots?
questions.explanation: Apical meristems are located at the tips of roots and shoots, and their cell division leads to an increase in the length of the plant body.

questions.question: Which of the following describes the function of muscle tissue in animals?
questions.explanation: In animals, muscle tissue is explicitly stated to enable movement.

questions.question: What primary function does a cell wall serve in plant cells?
questions.explanation: Plant cells have a cell wall that provides rigidity and strength, allowing plants to stay firm and upright.

questions.question: A gardener notices that an older tree has developed a thick, protective outer layer. From which plant tissue did this layer originate?

options.option: Cork cambium, formed from lateral meristematic cells

questions.explanation: In older trees, the initial single-layered epidermis is replaced by a secondary protective layer called the cork (or bark). This is produced by the cork cambium, which is a type of lateral meristem that undergoes cell division to add thickness and protective tissue to the plant's exterior as it grows in girth.

questions.question: If a tissue is specialized for rapid diffusion of liquids and gases, which epithelial structure would it most likely possess?

options.option: Many layers of flat and tightly packed cells
Cuboidal or columnar cells specialized for secretion
A single layer of thin, flat cells

questions.explanation: A single layer of thin, flat cells (simple squamous epithelium) provides the shortest possible distance for substances to travel, making it the most efficient structure for the rapid diffusion of liquids and gases across membranes.

questions.question: A person sustains a cut that causes swelling and redness around the wound. Which components of blood are primarily responsible for this inflammatory response?

options.option: White Blood Cells (WBCs) collecting at the infected area

questions.explanation: When a cut occurs, White Blood Cells (WBCs) are the primary responders that travel to the site of injury. They accumulate there to fight potential pathogens and remove damaged cells, which triggers the characteristic inflammatory symptoms of redness, heat, and swelling as part of the body's natural immune defense.

questions.question: If epithelial tissue serves various functions like protection, secretion, and absorption, how does its structural diversity contribute to these varied roles?

options.option: All epithelial tissues have the same structure but different biochemical pathways for each function.
Structural diversity only affects tissue repair, not the primary functions.
Different epithelial structures are specifically adapted to optimize each function.
The varied functions are primarily regulated by nerve impulses, not structural differences.

questions.explanation: Epithelial cells are specialized for their roles through structural variation. Thin, simple layers facilitate rapid diffusion and absorption, while stratified, robust layers provide necessary protection against abrasion and environmental stress in different bodily regions.

questions.question: Why is the pioneering work on anther culture by Sipra Guha Mukherjee and S. C. Maheshwari considered to have 'greatly contributed to crop improvement and brought significant progress in modern agriculture'?

options.option: It directly allowed for the genetic modification of crops for increased yield.
It enabled developing complete plants from tissue, allowing controlled breeding and disease-free propagation.
It provided a direct increase in plant photosynthetic efficiency.
The research streamlined the process of discovering new plant species for cultivation.

questions.explanation: Their pioneering work enabled the production of haploid plants from pollen grains through tissue culture. This technique significantly accelerates breeding programs by allowing scientists to develop homozygous lines and identify beneficial traits more efficiently for crop improvement.

questions.question: If a body process needs rapid diffusion (e.g., in lungs for gas exchange), what type of epithelial tissue would be most efficient, and why?

options.option: Stratified squamous epithelium because of its protective layers resisting tearing during diffusion.
Simple squamous epithelium because its thinness minimizes diffusion distance.
Cuboidal epithelium because its cube-like structure offers rigidity and efficient molecular movement.
Columnar epithelium with cilia because cilia increase surface area for faster diffusion.

questions.explanation: Efficiency in diffusion is governed by Fick's Law, which states that the rate of diffusion is inversely proportional to the thickness of the membrane. Simple squamous epithelium consists of a single layer of flattened, plate-like cells. This ultra-thin barrier minimizes the distance gases must travel, allowing for the rapid exchange of oxygen and carbon dioxide between the air sacs (alveoli) of the lungs and the blood.

questions.question: Considering the structural differences in animal tissues, why is it crucial for epithelial cells lining the intestine to be taller and often hair-like (ciliated columnar) compared to the epithelial cells lining blood vessels?

options.option: The difference is random and does not impact functional efficiency.
Hair-like structures are solely for sensory functions in the intestine, not absorption.
Taller, hair-like cells are better for protection against mechanical injury in the intestine.
Tall cells maximize absorption and movement in the intestine, while flat cells in blood vessels prioritize rapid exchange.

questions.explanation: Columnar cells provide surface area for efficient nutrient absorption in the intestine, while the flat, thin structure of blood vessel epithelium minimizes the distance required for rapid gas and nutrient exchange.

questions.question: Why would the lining of the mouth and oesophagus require a different epithelial structure compared to the lining of the lungs?

options.option: Lungs need stratified cells to prevent germ entry, whereas the mouth and oesophagus do not.
The mouth and oesophagus need protective layers against mechanical stress, while lungs need thin layers for gas exchange.
The mouth and oesophagus require a single layer of thin cells for rapid nutrient absorption, unlike the lungs.
Both locations primarily require cells specialized for secretion.

questions.explanation: The mouth and oesophagus require stratified squamous epithelium to withstand mechanical abrasion from food. Conversely, the lungs use thin, simple squamous epithelium to facilitate efficient gas exchange through diffusion.

questions.question: If an individual suffers a severe calcium deficiency, which connective tissue would be most directly and significantly impacted in terms of its primary function?

options.option: Bone, as its rigid matrix relies on calcium compounds.
Blood, due to its role in calcium transport.
Tendons, as they connect muscles to bones.
Cartilage, as it provides flexibility and cushioning.

questions.explanation: 'Bones have a rigid matrix containing calcium and phosphorus compounds, giving them strength and rigidity.' A severe calcium deficiency would directly compromise these structural properties, impacting bone's primary function of strength and support.

questions.question: Why are skeletal muscles classified as 'voluntary' movements, while the movement of food in the intestine is 'involuntary'?

options.option: Involuntary movements are initiated by hormones, while voluntary movements are initiated by nerves.
Only voluntary movements involve striated muscle fibers.
Skeletal muscles are under conscious control, while intestinal movements occur automatically.
Voluntary movements use different types of proteins than involuntary movements.

questions.explanation: 'Voluntary movements' are those under our conscious control, such as running, writing or lifting objects, and are carried out by skeletal muscles attached to the skeleton. Conversely, 'involuntary movements' occur automatically without conscious control, for example the movement of food in the intestine.

questions.question: How does the structure of cartilage, with its soft, jelly-like matrix, contribute to its function, particularly in comparison to bone?

options.option: The matrix of cartilage is primarily for nutrient storage, while bone's matrix is for mineral storage.
Cartilage provides flexibility and cushioning, whereas bone provides structural support.
Cartilage's soft matrix allows it to connect muscles to bones, unlike bone's matrix.
The soft, jelly-like matrix gives cartilage strength and rigidity, similar to bone.

questions.explanation: 'Bones have a rigid matrix containing calcium and phosphorus compounds, giving them strength and rigidity. In contrast, cartilage has a soft, jelly-like matrix, and provides flexibility and cushioning.' This highlights how their distinct matrices enable different functions.

questions.question: The development of a complete plant through anther culture represents a significant advance in basic biology. Why is obtaining a complete organism from just an anther an important scientific breakthrough?

options.option: It demonstrates the totipotency of plant cells and offers a controlled method for plant regeneration and breeding.
The primary importance is in discovering new plant growth hormones unique to anther tissue.
It shows that sexual reproduction in plants is not necessary for propagation under laboratory conditions.
It proves that anthers contain all necessary organelles for independent cellular activity, but not whole plant development.

questions.explanation: Anther culture allowing 'the development of a complete plant' signifies that cells within the anther (often haploid) possess the genetic potential (totipotency) to regenerate an entire organism. This is a fundamental concept in plant biology and a powerful tool for genetics and breeding.

questions.question: What is the primary function of the musculoskeletal system, besides enabling movement?
questions.explanation: The musculoskeletal system, particularly the bones, provides protection for delicate internal organs.

questions.question: A person experiences difficulty moving their arm after a fall, specifically when trying to lift it. An examination reveals damage to the structures connecting muscles to the shoulder bone. Which component is most likely affected?
questions.explanation: Tendons are the strong, flexible bands that attach muscles to bones. Damage to these structures would directly impair the transmission of muscle contraction force to the bone, making movement like lifting difficult.

questions.question: If an individual suffers from a condition that weakens the contractile ability of their muscles, how would this primarily impact their musculoskeletal system's function to produce movement?

options.option: It would weaken the tendons' ability to attach to bones.
It would increase the protective function of bones for organs.
It would directly reduce the flexibility of joints.
It would reduce the force transmitted to bones, hindering movement.

questions.explanation: Muscles pull on bones to produce movement. If muscles have weakened contractual ability, they cannot generate sufficient force to be transmitted by tendons to the bones, thus hindering movement.

questions.question: Consider a scenario where the nervous system's ability to send signals to muscles is compromised. How would this impairment affect the overall functioning of the musculoskeletal system regarding movement?

options.option: Muscles would still contract but with reduced efficiency due to weakened tendons.
The musculoskeletal system would maintain posture but lose its ability to protect organs.
Bones would become denser to compensate for the lack of muscle activity.
Movement would be impaired or impossible because muscle contractions would not be initiated or controlled.

questions.explanation: The musculoskeletal system functions under the control of the nervous system. If nerve signals are compromised, muscles cannot receive the commands to contract, directly leading to impaired or impossible movement.

questions.question: An athlete suffers an injury that makes the connection between a muscle and a bone severely fragile, prone to tearing even with normal contraction. Which component of the musculoskeletal system has been primarily damaged?

options.option: The cartilage, causing increased friction at the joint.
The ligaments, failing to stabilize the joint during muscle action.
The bone itself, leading to poor muscle attachment points.
The tendons, which transmit muscle force to the bone.

questions.explanation: Tendons are the strong, flexible bands that attach muscles to bones and transmit the force of muscle contraction. If this connection is fragile and tears easily, the tendons are the injured component.

questions.question: Which of the following medical conditions can be treated using a bone marrow transplant to replace damaged or diseased blood cells with healthy stem cells?
questions.explanation: Bone marrow transplants using stem cells are given to patients with blood cancers like Leukemia and disorders such as Thalassemia.

questions.question: A person is performing an action that involves extensive rotation of their arm, including movements like circling their arm overhead. Based on the provided joint types, which joint is primarily enabling this action?
questions.explanation: The shoulder joint is a ball and socket joint, which specifically allows for free movement including circular rotation, unlike hinge or pivot joints that have more restricted movement.

questions.question: If an individual is able to turn their head from side to side in a 'no' gesture, which type of joint is primarily responsible for facilitating this specific movement between the skull and the backbone?
questions.explanation: The pivot joint connects the skull to the backbone and explicitly allows for side-to-side head movement, as described when shaking one's head 'no'.

questions.question: Imagine a patient requires a bone marrow transplant. What is the fundamental characteristic of stem cells that makes them suitable for treating blood cancers like Leukemia or disorders like Thalassemia?

options.option: Their ability to fight off infections directly.
Their role in forming hard bone structures.
Their function in protecting vital organs like the brain.
Their capacity to divide and generate new types of cells.

questions.explanation: Stem cells are described as special cells that can divide and make new cells, which is why they are used in bone marrow transplants to replace diseased blood cells.

questions.question: How does the structural difference between a ball and socket joint and a hinge joint account for the difference in movement capabilities, particularly concerning the arm and knee?

options.option: Hinge joints provide more stability for weight-bearing activities compared to ball and socket joints.
Ball and socket joints have a kneecap for protection, while hinge joints do not.
Ball and socket joints allow multi-directional movement, unlike hinge joints which only allow bending and straightening in one plane.
The collarbone is involved in ball and socket joints but not in hinge joints, limiting hinge joint movement.

questions.explanation: The ball and socket joint's structure (rounded top in a hollow) enables extensive, multi-directional movements (forward, backward, sideways, circular). In contrast, the hinge joint, like a door hinge, is structured to allow movement in only one direction (bending and straightening), hence the functional difference in the arm (ball and socket) versus the knee (hinge).

questions.question: Analyze the potential consequences if the bone marrow stem cells, which typically divide and make new cells to treat blood disorders, were instead highly specialized and incapable of producing diverse cell types.

options.option: Treatments for Leukemia and Thalassemia would be ineffective.
It would enhance the body's ability to produce specific types of antibodies.
The skeletal system would become stronger due to increased bone density.
It would lead to an overproduction of red blood cells, causing polycythemia.

questions.explanation: Stem cells must be able to differentiate into various blood cell types. If they were highly specialized and static, they could not regenerate the diverse, healthy blood cells required to treat diseases.

questions.question: What is the primary function of the skeletal system?

options.option: To regulate hormone production and nutrient absorption.
To produce blood cells and store minerals.
To provide strength and protect delicate internal organs.
To facilitate movement and maintain body temperature.

questions.explanation: The skeletal system provides the structural framework (strength) that supports the body, while specialized bones, such as the skull and rib cage, act as shields to protect delicate internal organs.

questions.question: Which component allows for flexibility in the vertebral column?

options.option: The firm attachment of ribs to the spine.
The skull's connection to the first vertebra.

questions.explanation: The vertebral column is made of individual bones called vertebrae. To allow for movement like bending, twisting, and shock absorption, there are intervertebral discs made of flexible, tough cartilage situated between each vertebra. These discs act as pivots and cushions, enabling the spine's flexibility while maintaining its structural role.

questions.question: How does the rib cage contribute to the process of breathing?

options.option: It produces hormones that stimulate lung function.
It directly muscles the lungs to inflate and deflate.
It expands and contracts due to flexible cartilage.

questions.explanation: The rib cage is not a solid, immovable structure. It is connected to the sternum (breastbone) by flexible cartilage. During inhalation, muscles pull the ribs up and out, while the diaphragm contracts downwards. This increases the volume of the chest cavity, allowing the lungs to expand and fill with air. During exhalation, the ribs move back into their resting position, helping to push air out of the lungs.

questions.question: A person experiences difficulty and pain during breathing after a fall that impacted their chest. Based on the description of the skeletal system, which component is most likely injured?

options.option: The vertebral column, as it controls all body movements.
The cartilage discs between vertebrae, affecting posture.
The rib cage, including the ribs or their cartilaginous attachments.
The skull, due to its role in protecting the brain.

questions.explanation: The rib cage surrounds and protects the lungs. Since the injury occurred to the chest and resulted in painful breathing, the ribs or their flexible cartilaginous attachments-which must expand for respiration-are the structures most likely damaged.

questions.question: How do the growth patterns of plants differ from animals, particularly concerning the tissues responsible for growth?

options.option: Plants and animals both have similar growth patterns, with identical tissues responsible for growth.
Animals exhibit continuous growth throughout their lifespan, while plant growth is limited to specific stages.
Growth patterns in plants and animals vary due to differences in the structure and function of their respective growth-responsible tissues.
Plant growth is solely dependent on solar energy, unlike animal growth.

questions.explanation: Plant growth is restricted to specific regions called meristems, enabling localized, continuous development throughout their lives. Conversely, animals exhibit diffuse, uniform growth patterns that cease after reaching maturity, reflecting distinct structural and functional tissue specializations.

questions.question: What is a primary distinction between plant and animal cells concerning structural support and movement?

options.option: Animal cells possess a rigid cell wall for structural integrity, while plant cells do not.
Plant cells have a cell wall providing rigidity, whereas animal cells lack a cell wall, allowing for cellular flexibility and locomotion.
Neither plant nor animal cells possess structures for rigidity or movement.
Both plant and animal cells have rigid cell walls that enable locomotion.

questions.explanation: Plant cells are characterized by a cell wall that provides rigidity and strength, enabling them to stay fixed. Animal cells, without a rigid cell wall, can change shape easily, which facilitates locomotion.

questions.question: A gardener wants to encourage a plant to grow taller rapidly. Based on the understanding of plant growth, which part of the plant should receive optimal conditions to enhance this specific type of growth?

options.option: The apical meristem in the stem, as it is responsible for the increase in length of the stem.
The roots to increase water absorption and overall plant mass.
The lateral parts of the stem to promote increased girth.
The leaves to maximize photosynthesis and energy production.

questions.explanation: Increase in length (height of stem and depth of roots) is a way plants grow, and this growth is attributed to actively dividing cells forming meristematic tissue, specifically apical meristem for length.

questions.question: Consider a case where a farmer regularly trims the branches of a shrub. What type of plant growth is the farmer trying to manage or stimulate through this action?

options.option: Increase in the height of the shrub.
Regrowth after cutting the branches, often leading to a bushier form.
Increase in the thickness of the stem.

questions.explanation: Regrowth after cutting the branches or grazing by animals is a way plants grow. Trimming branches stimulates this regrowth, often leading to denser, bushier foliage rather than increased height.

questions.question: An environmental scientist observes an area where grass is frequently grazed by animals, yet it consistently regrows. Based on plant physiology, what type of tissue is primarily responsible for this persistent regrowth?

options.option: Transport tissues that relocate nutrients to the damaged areas.
Tissues specialized for photosynthesis, which provide energy for regrowth.
Meristematic tissues, composed of actively dividing cells, which enable regrowth.
Structural tissues that provide support and rigidity.

questions.explanation: Regrowth after cutting the branches or grazing by animals is a way plants grow, and this growth require actively dividing cells that together form a tissue called a meristematic tissue.

questions.question: Considering the differing cellular structures and modes of nutrition, analyse why plants and animals have distinct tissues for transporting food and water.

options.option: Transport tissues in plants are only for water, and in animals, only for food.
All organisms have similar transport tissues regardless of their mode of nutrition or cellular structure.
Plants transport food and water from fixed zones, while animals distribute digested nutrients body-wide.
The presence of a cell wall in plants dictates the need for different mineral transport tissues.

questions.explanation: Plants perform photosynthesis in specific parts and absorb water from roots, thus needing transport for produced food and absorbed water. Animals, consuming various food sources, require tissues for digesting and then transporting absorbed nutrients throughout their mobile bodies. Animals have tissues that help them digest food obtained from different food sources, while plants have tissues that help them utilise solar energy for synthesising the food components through photosynthesis. Plants and animals have distinct tissues for transporting food and water to different parts of the body.

questions.question: What is the primary function of parenchyma tissue in plants?
questions.explanation: Parenchyma primarily stores food and also performs photosynthesis in the green parts of the plants.

questions.question: Which type of plant tissue consists of actively dividing cells arranged in a ring in the stem that lead to an increase in diameter?
questions.explanation: The lateral meristem is responsible for the increase in girth or diameter of the stem due to its ring-like arrangement of actively dividing cells.

questions.question: What is the primary role of the epidermis in plant bodies?
questions.explanation: The epidermis forms the outermost layer and protects plants from mechanical injury, water loss, and harmful microorganisms.

questions.question: Which component of xylem is the only living part, while the rest are primarily sclerenchymatous?
questions.explanation: Xylem parenchyma are the only living component of xylem, whereas tracheids, vessels, and xylem fibres are primarily sclerenchymatous.

questions.question: An experiment measures the growth of onion roots. If the root tips of one onion are cut, what would be the expected outcome compared to an uncut onion, and why?

options.option: The cut onion's roots would start growing new tips from the sides.
The cut onion's roots would grow faster to compensate for the damage.
The cut onion's roots would stop growing in length, while the uncut onion's roots would continue, demonstrating the role of apical meristems.
Both onions would stop growing because nutrients are limited.

questions.explanation: Cutting the root tips removes the apical meristem, which is responsible for growth in length. Therefore, the cut onion's roots will stop growing in length, while the uncut onion's roots will continue to grow.

questions.question: A botanist examines a cross-section of a tree trunk and observes distinct annual growth rings. What information can these rings provide?

options.option: The tree's age and the climatic conditions during its growth.
The type of soil the tree grew in.
The presence of pest infestations throughout its life.

questions.explanation: Annual growth rings can be used by scientists to estimate the age of a tree and understand the climatic conditions experienced during its growth based on their width (wide for favorable, narrow for unfavorable).

questions.question: A plant adapted to a very dry habitat has an unusually thick layer of cuticle on its epidermis. What is the most likely reason for this adaptation?

options.option: To deter herbivores from eating the plant.

questions.explanation: Plants in dry habitats often develop a thick cuticle to minimize water loss by transpiration, which is a critical adaptation for survival in such environments.

questions.question: If a plant's collenchyma tissue were damaged, which of the following functions would be most severely impaired?

options.option: Transport of sugars from leaves to other parts

questions.explanation: Collenchyma provides support and flexibility, allowing parts of the plant like stems and tendrils to bend without breaking.

questions.question: A plant geneticist develops a mutant plant where the companion cells in the phloem are non-functional. What would be the immediate consequence for this plant?

options.option: Impaired water transport from roots to leaves.
Disrupted regulation of sugar loading and unloading in sieve tubes.

questions.explanation: The main function of companion cells is to monitor and regulate the loading and unloading of sugars in sieve tubes. If they are non-functional, sugar transport would be negatively affected.

questions.question: An aquatic plant has specialized parenchyma with large air spaces. What is the adaptive advantage of this specialization?

options.option: To store excess water during dry periods.
To increase mechanical strength for resisting water currents.
To facilitate gas exchange with the surrounding water.
To provide buoyancy, helping the plant to float.

questions.explanation: In aquatic plants, specialized parenchyma forms air spaces, which aid in buoyancy, allowing the plants to float.

questions.question: Why are meristematic tissue cells characterized by small size, thin cell walls, dense cytoplasm, a prominent nucleus, and a general absence of vacuoles?

options.option: They are designed for long-term storage of nutrients.
These features provide structural rigidity to the plant.
They facilitate rapid and continuous cell division.
These features optimize them for efficient photosynthesis.

questions.explanation: The characteristics of meristematic cells (small size, thin walls, dense cytoplasm, prominent nucleus, no vacuoles, tightly packed) are all adaptations that enable continuous and rapid cell division, which is critical for plant growth.

questions.question: Although both xylem and phloem are complex permanent tissues, they differ significantly in their primary cellular composition. How does this difference relate to their respective functions?

options.option: Xylem is mostly living to actively transport water, while phloem is mostly dead for passive sugar transport.
Xylem is predominantly sclerenchymatous for support, while phloem is mainly parenchymatous for storage.
Xylem is mainly dead cells for passive water movement, whereas phloem is mostly living cells for active sugar transport.
Both xylem and phloem are entirely composed of living cells for active transport.

questions.explanation: Xylem consists mostly of dead cells (tracheids, vessels, fibres) for passive water conduction, with only parenchyma being living. Phloem, in contrast, is mainly composed of living cells (sieve tubes, companion cells, parenchyma) to actively transport and regulate sugars.

questions.question: The outer layer of a plant, the epidermis, forms a protective barrier. How does its structure, including the cuticle and projections like root hairs and stomata, contribute to its diverse roles in different plant parts?

options.option: Cuticle reduces water loss, root hairs maximize absorption, and stomata regulate gas exchange.
The epidermis is uniformly structured across the plant, providing universal protection and nutrient absorption.
All epidermal structures primarily provide mechanical support and deter pests.
The cuticle enhances water absorption, while root hairs expel excess water and stomata are for protection only.

questions.explanation: The tightly packed epidermal cells and waxy cuticle are crucial for reducing water loss. Root hairs are specialized epidermal projections that greatly increase surface area for water and mineral absorption. Stomata, also epidermal components, regulate gas exchange and transpiration, linking water transport, waste elimination, and temperature regulation.

questions.question: Sclerenchyma provides hardness and strength to plant parts like seed coats and nut shells. How does its cellular composition and state contribute to these properties?

options.option: It consists of living cells with thin, flexible walls that allow movement and resilience.
It has large intercellular spaces that store water, making the tissues turgid and strong.
Its primary role is active transport of water, which indirectly strengthens the tissues.
It is made of mostly dead cells with thick, lignified walls, which impart rigidity and mechanical strength.

questions.explanation: Sclerenchyma cells are characterized by thick, lignified walls, and most are dead at maturity. Lignin provides hardness and strength, making this tissue suitable for protective and supportive roles in plant parts.

questions.question: Which of the following scenarios best illustrates the functional difference between apical and lateral meristems?

options.option: A plant grows taller by 10 cm in a month (apical meristem), and its stem increases in diameter by 1 cm (lateral meristem).
A root branches outward into the soil (apical meristem), and the root cap protects the growing tip (lateral meristem).
The shoot produces flowers (apical meristem), and new roots grow from a cutting (lateral meristem).
Leaves emerge from a stem node (apical meristem), while the stem bends without breaking (lateral meristem).

questions.explanation: Apical meristems are at the tips of roots and shoots and contribute to increase in length (height or depth). Lateral meristems, located along the circumference of stems, lead to an increase in girth or thickness.

questions.question: If a plant had only apical meristems and no lateral or intercalary meristems, what would be the most apparent growth characteristic of this plant?

options.option: It would be unable to produce leaves or flowers.
It would grow very tall but remain slender, with difficulty regenerating after pruning.
It would be short and stout, with many branches.
It would only increase in girth and not in length.

questions.explanation: Apical meristems are solely responsible for growth in length. Without lateral meristems, the plant would lack an increase in girth, making it slender. Without intercalary meristems, it would not regenerate new growth from cut parts, thus hindering bushiness after pruning.

questions.question: Consider a case where a plant requires urgent transport of water and minerals from its roots to its leaves, especially in a tall tree. Evaluate which adaptations of xylem tissue are crucial for this function.

options.option: The tubular and thick-walled nature of tracheids and vessels.
The ability of xylem cells to perform photosynthesis for energy production.
The flexibility provided by xylem fibres to withstand pressure changes.
The presence of living xylem parenchyma for active pumping of water.

questions.explanation: The tubular and thick-walled structure of tracheids and vessels creates a continuous pipe-like system that allows efficient, passive bulk flow of water and minerals against gravity in tall trees, with minimal resistance. This structural feature is essential for long-distance transport.

questions.question: Which type of tissue is primarily responsible for performing diverse functions like exchange, protection, and secretion in animals?
questions.explanation: Epithelial tissue acts as a versatile covering and lining. Its structural diversity-ranging from simple layers for efficient exchange to stratified layers for protection and glandular cells for secretion-enables these varied essential bodily functions.

questions.question: Which characteristic differentiates connective tissues like blood and bone, despite both being classified as connective tissues?

options.option: The composition and consistency of their matrix

questions.explanation: Connective tissues are defined by their cells suspended in an extracellular matrix. The primary differentiator is the consistency of this matrix: blood has a fluid, non-living matrix called plasma, whereas bone has a hard, calcified, and rigid matrix.

questions.question: What is the primary function of cork cells in older plants?
questions.explanation: In older plants, cork cells (also known as phellem) are dead at maturity and contain a waxy substance called suberin. This makes them waterproof and gas-impermeable, allowing them to form the thick bark that provides essential protection against mechanical injury, pathogens, and extreme environmental conditions.

questions.question: What prevents the entry of germs and reduces water loss in the body?
questions.explanation: Epithelial tissue forms the body's outer covering, such as the skin. Because these cells are tightly packed together without intercellular spaces, they create a formidable barrier that effectively prevents the entry of harmful pathogens (germs) and minimizes excessive water loss through evaporation.

questions.question: Which type of human tissue is composed of closely packed cells with very little space between them?
questions.explanation: Epithelial tissue is characterized by cells that are tightly packed together with very little intercellular matrix. This dense arrangement forms a continuous sheet, which is essential for its primary functions of lining body surfaces, protecting underlying structures, and controlling the selective absorption and secretion of substances.

questions.question: Which of the following functions is not typically performed by epithelial tissues?
questions.explanation: Epithelial tissues are specialized for protection, absorption, secretion, and the exchange of materials. However, the conduction of electrical impulses is the primary function of nervous tissue, which is composed of neurons and glial cells designed for rapid communication throughout the body.

questions.question: A patient experiences difficulty moving a joint due to excessive movement. Which connective tissue is most likely impaired?
questions.explanation: Ligaments are tough, fibrous connective tissues that connect bone to bone. Their primary function is to provide joint stability and limit range of motion to prevent dislocation or excessive movement. When ligaments are damaged or impaired, they can no longer hold the joint components in their proper alignment, often leading to instability and "loose" or excessive movement.

questions.question: When an individual exercises vigorously, what physiological change occurs in the body, and what is its primary purpose?

options.option: Faster breathing and increased blood flow to supply oxygen to muscles.
Reduced white blood cell activity to prevent inflammation
Increased blood clotting to prevent internal bleeding

questions.explanation: During exercise or running, muscles need more oxygen, so breathing becomes faster and blood flow increases (face appears red).

questions.question: The numerical ratio of displacement to distance for a moving object is
questions.explanation:
We know, the magnitude of displacement is the straight line distance between the initial point and the final point of the path of the moving body.
Displacement could be negative, positive, or zero. but the distance is always positive or zero when there is no motion.
So, the ratio of displacement to distance for a moving object is equal to less than 1.

questions.question: What is the slope of the body when it moves with uniform velocity?
questions.explanation: The slope is zero (because the slope for the velocity time graph gives the acceleration).

questions.question: Which of the following is the position-time graph for a body at rest?
questions.explanation:
The above graph shows an object which is not moving i.e. it is at rest.
The straight horizontal line parallel to the time axis shows that the distance covered by the body remains constant with the change in time.

questions.question: What does the area of the velocity-time graph give?
questions.explanation:
The area enclosed by the velocity-time graph and time axis gives the magnitude of displacement.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} velocity = {tex} \frac{displacement}{Time}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Velocity {tex}\times{/tex} time = displacement

questions.question: If a body starts from rest, what can be said about the acceleration of the body?
questions.explanation: If a body starts from rest, it starts moving. that means the change in velocity is positive. That means there is a POSITIVE acceleration.

questions.question: What does the slope of the position-time graph indicate?
questions.explanation: The slope of a position graph represents the velocity of the object. The steeper the slope is, the faster the motion is changing. So the value of the slope at a particular time represents the velocity of the object at that instant. Average velocity can be calculated from a position-time graph as the change in position divided by the corresponding change in time.

questions.question: When a body moves uniformly along the circle, then:-

options.option: its speed changes but velocity remain the same
its velocity changes but speed remain the same
both speed and velocity remain the same.

questions.explanation: Speed remains the same as it is 'uniform' circular motion and velocity changes at every point because of direction changes. The tendency of the object at every point is to go tangential to its position.

questions.question: What does the slope of the velocity-time graph give?
questions.explanation:
We can find out the value of acceleration from the slope of the velocity-time graph of a moving body.
Acceleration = {tex}Change\,\,in\,\,velocity\over\, time{/tex} = Slope of the velocity-time graph provided.

questions.question: The displacement of the body can be-
questions.explanation: Displacement is the shortest distance travelled so it can be positive or negative or zero.

questions.question: Which of the following gives both direction and magnitude?
questions.explanation: Any quantity that needs to be fully described by identifying its magnitude and direction is referred to as a vector quantity. By magnitude, we mean the size of the quantity, such as length or strength. By direction, we mean where the vector is pointing or where it is being directed, such as left or right, north, south, east, or west, or even up or down.

questions.question: If a moving body comes to rest, then it's acceleration is:
questions.explanation: If a moving body comes to rest, then its acceleration is negative, as it’s velocity becomes zero. therefore the acceleration is also zero.

questions.question: A body is said to be in rest when:

options.option: It's position changes with time w.r.t observer.
The body moves in uniform motion, w.r.t observer.
Its position doesn’t change with time with respect to the observer.

questions.explanation: When a body does not change its position with respect to time, it is said that body is in a state of rest. Rest and motion are relative to the observer.

questions.question: A person sitting in the truck projected a ball vertically upwards. The ball:
questions.explanation: A person sitting in the truck projected a ball vertically upwards. The ball will fall back in his hand as the ball and the truck are moving at the same speed.

questions.question: Which of the following is the characteristic of distance travelled by an object?

options.option: The distance travelled by an object is less than the magnitude of the displacement of the object.
It has a magnitude as well as specific direction
It has only magnitude and no specific direction

questions.explanation: Distance is the length of path covered by a moving object in the given time irrespective of direction. Distance has only magnitude and no direction.

questions.question: Which of the following is the characteristic of displacement of an object?

options.option: The magnitude of the displacement is greater than the distance travelled by a moving object
Displacement has only magnitude and no specific direction
Displacement has magnitude as well as specific direction

questions.explanation: The shortest distance between the initial point and the final point is called displacement. Displacement has both magnitude and direction while distance has only magnitude.

questions.question: The displacement of a body is proportional to the cube of the time lapsed. The magnitude of the acceleration is:
questions.explanation: Acceleration is directly proportioned to time, it varies linearly with time and increases with respect to time.

questions.question: A body moves on three-quarters of a circle of radius r. The displacement and distance travelled by it
questions.explanation:
Distance covered is {tex}\frac34{/tex} circumference. 
{tex}\frac34\times2\times\mathrm\pi\times\mathrm r\;=\frac{3\mathrm{r}}2{/tex}
Displacement = Shortest Path

If We Join Point A and B we get right angle triangle with
Hypotenuse(displacement) and other two sides of a triangle with sides r.
Displacement = {tex}\sqrt{r^2+\;r^2}\;=\sqrt{2r^2}\;=\sqrt2r{/tex}

questions.question: A body moving with uniform acceleration has velocities 20 ms-1 and 30 ms-1. when passing two points A and B. Then the velocity midway between A and B is:
questions.explanation:
Let the acceleration of the car is = a
And the distance between A and B is = d
v2 - u2 = 2ad
v = 30m/s and u = 20m/s
2ad = 302 - 202
Ad = {tex}\frac{(900-400)}2{/tex} = 250 
When the car is at the mid point of AB then let the speed of the car is v1
v12 - 202 = 2a ({tex}\frac d2{/tex})
v12 = ad + 400 = 250 + 400 = 650
therefore v1 is = 25.4950m/s.

questions.question: A moving body is covering a distance directly proportional to the square of time. The acceleration of the body is:
questions.explanation:
Displacement is proportional to square of time
s is displacement and t is time then,
s {tex}\propto{/tex} t2
s = kt2
velocity(v) = 2kt
Acceleration(a) = 2k = constant.
So, the motion of the body is constant acceleration.

questions.question: The area under the velocity-time graph gives the value of:
questions.explanation: The area under a velocity-time graph represents the distance covered and the gradient of a velocity-time graph represents the acceleration.

questions.question: Usha swims in a 90 m long pool. She covers 180 m in one minute going either way. The average velocity is:
questions.explanation: The average velocity for the entire swing would be zero because its final position and initial position are identical.

questions.question: A car accelerates uniformly from 18 km/h to 36 km/h in 5 sec. The acceleration is
questions.explanation:
Given,
u = 18km/h = 18×1000 / 60×60 = 5m/s
v = 36km/h = 36×1000 / 60×60 = 10m/s
t = 5 sec
acceleration, a = ?
1st equation of motion,
v = u + at
10 = 5 + 5a
5a = 5
a = 5/5 = 1m/s

questions.question: A ball is thrown up with a velocity of 20 ms-1. What is the time of flight, neglecting air resistance?
questions.explanation:
Using kinematic equation, v = u + a{tex}\times{/tex}t, when ball reaches to top of its trajectory, it's velocity becomes zero. Ball is thrown against gravity of earth, therefore a = - g, u = 20 m/s, we can find time of ascend as
0 = 20 - 10 {tex}\times{/tex} t
20 = 10 {tex}\times{/tex} t
t = {tex}\frac{20}{10}{/tex} = 2 sec
Time of ascend is same as time of descend = 2 sec.
Time of flight = time of ascend + time of descend
Therefore time of flight = 4 sec.

questions.question: A car travels 10 m in 5 seconds, 20 m in the next 10 seconds, and 30 m in the last 10 seconds. The average speed of the motion is:
questions.explanation:
The average distance covered in unit time by a moving object is called average speed. The average speed is the ratio of total distance covered and total time taken.
Average speed = {tex}\frac{Total\;distan ce\;covered}{Total\;time\;taken}{/tex} = {tex}\;\frac{10+20+30}{5\;+\;10\;+10}{/tex} = {tex}\;\frac{60}{25}{/tex} = 2.4 ms-1

questions.question: A particle is moving in a circular path of radius r. The displacement after half a circle would be
questions.explanation:
Given, after half the circle, the particle will reach the diametrically opposite point i.e., from point A to point 6. And we know displacement is shortest path between initial and final point.
Displacement after half circle = AB=OA + OB [{tex}\because{/tex} Given, OA and OB = r]

= r + r = 2r
Hence, the displacement after half circle is 2r.

questions.question: A body is thrown vertically upwards with velocity u, the greatest height h to which it will rise is
questions.explanation:
If a body is thrown vertically upwards with initial velocity "u"
Maximum Height of body = {tex}\frac{u^2}{2g}{/tex}
As  2gh = v² - u²
At maximum height final velocity of body is zero.
⇒ v = 0
So, 2gh = v² - u²
 2gh = -u²
 h= - {tex}\frac{u^2}{2g}{/tex}
Distance can't be negative so,  h= {tex}\frac{u^2}{2g}.{/tex}

questions.question: Equation of motion can be used for a body having:
questions.explanation: Relation among velocity, distance, time, and acceleration is called equations of motion. There are three equations of motion. Equation of motion can be used for a body having uniform acceleration.

questions.question: For a uniformly accelerated body with initial and final velocities as u and v ms-1, the average velocity is:
questions.explanation: Average velocity is the ratio of total displacement or total distance travelled by a body in a given interval of time.

questions.question: The area below v-t graph is a measure of:
questions.explanation: The area under the velocity-time graph gives the distance (magnitude of displacement) which has the unit is metre (m).

questions.question: If the velocity of a body is reducing, it is said to have
questions.explanation: If there is a decrease in acceleration, it is called Retardation. This means the rate of decrease in velocity is called retardation or negative acceleration or deceleration.

questions.question: If the v-t graph is a straight line inclined to the time axis, then:
questions.explanation: The negative value of acceleration signifies deceleration or in other words, the velocity is decreasing.

questions.question: A body starting at a point, say A, reaches, say B, ahead in a straight line and returns back to A. Then there is:
questions.explanation: When the body comes back to its starting point, it has zero resultant displacements but covers a certain non-zero distance.

questions.question: If the displacement of an object is proportional to square of time, then the object moves with
questions.explanation:
From second equation of motion, s = ut + {tex}\frac12{/tex} at2
if object starts from rest i.e., initial velocity(u) = 0 and acceleration(a) in time (t)
Then, s= 0 × t + {tex}\frac12{/tex} at2
s = {tex}\frac12{/tex}at2
s α t2, if a = constant
So, the object with constant or uniform acceleration.

questions.question: Suppose a boy is enjoying a ride on a merry-go-round which is moving with a constant speed of 10ms-1. It implies that the boy is
questions.explanation: In a merry go round,the speed is constant but the velocity is not constant because its direction will be changing i.e, there is acceleration in the motion.So the boy is in accelerated motion.

questions.question: Area under a v - t graph represents a physical quantity which has the unit
questions.explanation: The area under the velocity-time graph gives the distance (magnitude of displacement) which has the unit: metre (m)

questions.question: Which of the following figures represent uniform motion of moving object correctly?
questions.explanation: Uniform motion of a moving object

questions.question: Slope of a velocity-time graph gives
questions.explanation: The area under a velocity-time graph represents the distance covered and the gradient of a velocity-time graph represents the acceleration.

questions.question: In which of the following cases of motion, the distance moved and the magnitude of displacement are equal?

options.option: A car is moving in a circular path
The earth is revolving around the Sun
The pendulum is moving to and fro
A car is moving on a straight road

questions.explanation: The distance moved and magnitude of displacement are equal only in the case of motion along a straight line. Because displacement is the shortest path between initial and find path. So, for car moving on straight road, distance moved and magnitude of displacement are equal.

questions.question: A body moving in a circle of radius r covers {tex}\frac 34{/tex}th of the circle. The ratio of the distance to displacement is:
questions.explanation:
r' = {tex}r\sqrt2{/tex} displacement
distance = {tex}\frac{3\mathrm{{tex}\pi{/tex}r}}2{/tex}
ratio {tex}=\frac{\frac{3\mathrm{{tex}\pi{/tex}r}}2}{r\sqrt2}{/tex}
ratio = {tex}3\pi{/tex} : {tex}2\sqrt 2 {/tex}

questions.question: A signal from a space ship reaches the ground in 5 minutes. What was the distance of the space ship from the ground station? The speed of the signal is 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 108 m/s.
questions.explanation:
Time = 5 minutes = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 60 = 300 sec.
Distance = speed {tex}\times{/tex} time
= 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 108 m/s {tex}\times{/tex} 300 = 9 {tex}\times{/tex} 1010 m

questions.question: Two cars A and B race each other. The Car A ran for 2 min at a speed of 7.5 km/h, slept for 56 min and again ran for 2 min at a speed of 7.5 km/h. The average speed of the car A in the race is:
questions.explanation:
Distance = speed {tex}\times{/tex} time
Distance travelled in first 2 min = 7.5 {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac2{60}{/tex} = 0.25 km
Distance travelled in last 2 min = 7.5 {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac2{60}{/tex}= 0.25 km
Total distance = 0.25 + 0.25 = 0.5 km
Total time = 2 + 2 + 56 = 60 min = 1 hr
Average speed = {tex}\frac{0.5}1{/tex}
= 0.5 km/hr

questions.question: The maximum speed of a train is 90 km/h. It takes 10 hours to cover a distance of 500 km. The ratio of its average speed to maximum speed is:
questions.explanation:
Average speed = {tex}\frac{500}{10}{/tex} = 50 km/hr
Ratio of average speed to maximum speed = 50 : 90 = 5:9

questions.question: A boy runs for 10 minutes at a uniform speed of 9 kmh-1. At what speed should he run for the next 20 minutes so that the average speed becomes 12 kmh-1?
questions.explanation:
Let, the boy runs with speed v for next 20 min
Given that, average speed = 12 km/h-1
As, average speed = {tex}\frac{\text { Total distance covered }}{\text { Total time taken }}{/tex}
12 = {tex}\frac{\text { Total distance covered }}{\frac{(10+20)}{60} \mathrm{~h}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Total distance covered = 12 {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac {30}{60}{/tex} = 12 {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac 12{/tex} = 6 km
Distance covered in 10 min = 9 {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac {10}{60}{/tex} = 1.5 km
Remaining distance = 6 - 1.5 = 4.5 km
For next 20 min, he should run with speed
v = {tex}\frac{4.5}{\frac{20} {60}}{/tex} = 4.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 = 13.5 km h-1

questions.question: A parachutist jumps off the plane and opens up the parachute at time t1. He eventually reaches a steady speed and finally hits the floor at time t2. Which of the following shows the speed-time graph of the parachutist’s descent?
questions.explanation:
When the parachutist jumps off the plane his vertical speed is 0 m/s. The speed will increase constantly due to the gravitational field strength of the Earth, which makes the parachutist accelerate uniformly downwards. At this point, choice B and choice D are eliminated because the two graphs show a deceleration.
When the parachute opens, there is a sudden strong opposing air resistance acting against the parachutist, which causes the speed of the parachutist to drop drastically and eventually reach a constant speed known as terminal velocity.

questions.question: A particle is moving in a straight line with initial velocity u and uniform acceleration a. If the sum of the distances travelled in tth and (t + 1)th seconds is 100 cm, then its velocity after t seconds in cm s-1 is
questions.explanation:
Distance travelled in tth second of uniformly accelerated motion is
St = u +{tex}\frac a2{/tex}(2t - 1) ...(i)
Distance travelled in (t + 1)th second can be written as
St+1 = u +{tex}\frac a2{/tex}[2(t + 1) - 1]
or St+1 = u +{tex}\frac a2{/tex}(2t + 1) ...(ii)
st + st+1 = 100 cm (given)
u +{tex}\frac a2{/tex}(2t -1) + u +{tex}\frac a2{/tex}(2t + 1) = 100 [Using (i) and (ii)]
or 2u + 2at = 100 or u + at = 50;
{tex}\therefore{/tex} v = 50 cms-1

questions.question: Two identical balls are at rest side by side at the bottom of a hill. Sometime after ball A is kicked up the hill, ball B is given a kick up the hill. Ball A is headed downhill when it passes ball B headed up the hill. At the instant when ball A passes ball B, it has the same
questions.explanation: Position and acceleration of ball A is same as that of ball B at the instant when ball A passes ball B.

questions.question:
A dancer is demonstrating dance steps along a straight line. The position-time graph of the dancer is given here.

The average speed for the dance step depicted by CD is
questions.explanation:
Distance covered for dance step CD = 4 m
Time taken for CD is greater than 1 s but less than 2 s
Since, average speed ={tex}\frac{\text { total distance }}{\text { total time }}{/tex}
Therefore, average speed is less than 4 m s-1 but greater than 2 m s-1. Hence, option (C) is the only possibility.

questions.question:
A dancer is demonstrating dance steps along a straight line. The position-time graph of the dancer is given here.

The average velocity of the dancer during time interval t = 2 s to t = 9 s is
questions.explanation:
During time interval t = 2 s to t = 9 s, six dance steps namely AB, BC, CD, DE, EF and FG have been demonstrated by the dancer.
Total displacement = (0 - 2) = -2 m
Total time taken = 9 - 2 = 7s
Average velocity = {tex}\frac{\text { total displacement }}{\text { total time taken }}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-2}7{/tex} = -0.29 m s-1

questions.question: Figure shows the x-t plot of a particle moving along a straight line. Let v1 and v2 be average speeds in time intervals 1 and 2 respectively. Then
questions.explanation:
In time interval 1, magnitude of net displacement is more as compared to that time interval 2 i.e., {tex}\triangle{/tex}x1 > {tex}\triangle{/tex}x2
{tex}\therefore{/tex} v1 > v2

questions.question:
Which of the following is a correct statement?

  1. Distance is the magnitude of displacement in all cases.
  2. When a body moves with uniform speed, then the average speed is same as instantaneous speed.
  3. Average speed is greater than the average velocity if a body is moving in a straight line without reversing its direction.
  4. When a body moves with constant velocity, the average velocity is zero.
questions.explanation: Statement (ii) is correct.

questions.question:
Match the column I with column II and mark the correct option from the following given codes.

Column I Column II
(a) 36 km h-1 (i) 20000 mm
(b) 1 m s-2 (ii) 980 cm s-2
(c) 9.8 m s-2 (iii) 12960 km h-2
(d) 0.02 km (iv) 10 m s-1
A ball is dropped onto the floor from a height of 20 m. It rebounds to a height of 10 m. If the ball is in contact with the floor for 0.1 seconds, what is the average acceleration during contact?
questions.explanation:
Here height from which ball is dropped,
h = 20 m
Using v2 = u2 + 2gh; v2 = 2gh ({tex}\because{/tex} u = 0)
or v ={tex}\sqrt{2 g h}{/tex} (downward)
Ball rebounds to a height, h' = 10 m
So, 02 = v2 - 2gh' or v' ={tex}\sqrt{2 g h'}{/tex} (upward)
Acceleration of the ball,
a = {tex}\frac{\left(v^{\prime}-v\right)}{t}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\left[\sqrt{2 g h^{\prime}}-(-\sqrt{2 g h})\right]}{t}{/tex}
or a = {tex}\frac{[\sqrt{2 \times 9.8 \times 10}+\sqrt{2 \times 9.8 \times 20}]}{0.1}{/tex} {tex}\simeq{/tex} 338 ms-2

questions.question: What would you conclude about the variation in velocity of a cyclist from the given graph?

options.option: Velocity changes linearly if acceleration is changing non-linearly.
Velocity becomes uniform if acceleration becomes infinite.
Velocity becomes zero if acceleration becomes zero.
Velocity changes non-linearly if acceleration is changing linearly.

questions.explanation: For the given graph, a = kt, where k is some positive constant. So, velocity v = u + at = u + (kt)t = 4 + kt2 i.e., varies non-linearly.

questions.question:
Statement 1: Reducing the initial velocity to half the initial value, the stopping distance of moving object decreases by a factor of 2 (for the same deceleration).
Statement 2: Stopping distance is proportional to the square of the initial velocity.

options.option: Statement 1 is true but statement 2 is false.
Both statements 1 and 2 are true and statement 2 is the correct explanation of statement 1.
Both statements 1 and 2 are true but statement 2 is not the correct explanation of statement 1.
Statement 1 is false but statement 2 is true.

questions.explanation:
Let the distance travelled by the vehicle before it stops be ds, then substituting v = 0, x = ds (stopping distance) in v2 = u2 - 2ax, we get ds ={tex}\frac{u^2}{2 a}{/tex}.
Thus, the stopping distance is proportional to the square of the initial velocity.
So, if the initial velocity is reduced to half of its value, the stopping distance decreases by a factor of four.

questions.question: The water drop falls at regular intervals from a tap 5 m above the ground. The third drop is leaving the tap at instant the first drop touches the ground. How far above the ground is the second drop at that instant? (Take g = 10 m S-2)
questions.explanation:
Height of tap = 5 m
For the first drop, 5 = ut + {tex}\frac 12{/tex}gt2 = {tex}\frac 12{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex}10t2 = 5t2 or t2 = 1 or t = 1 s. It means that the third drop leaves after one second of the first drop, or each drop leaves after every 0.5 s. Distance covered by the second drop in 0.5 s
= {tex}\frac 12{/tex}gt2 = {tex}\frac 12{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 10 {tex}\times{/tex} (0.5)2 = 1.25 m
Therefore distance of the second drop above the ground = 5 - 1.25 = 3.75 m.

questions.question:
A train moves from one station to another in two hours time. Its speed-time graph during the motion is shown in figure. Identify the correct statement(s) regarding this graph.

  1. The maximum acceleration during the journey is 120 km/h2.
  2. The distance covered during the time interval from 0.75 hr to 1 hr is 8.75 km.
  3. During the time interval from 0 to 0.5 hrs, the train moves with constant velocity.
questions.explanation:
1. As the part BC of the speed-time graph has maximum slope, so acceleration is maximum during this interval.
    Maximum acceleration = Slope of BC
    = {tex}\frac{(50-20) \mathrm{km} \mathrm{h}^{-1}}{(1.00-0.75) \mathrm{h}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac {30}{0.25}{/tex} = 120 km h-2
  2. Distance covered from 0.75 h to 1.00 h
    = Area of trapezium BEFC
    = {tex}\frac 12{/tex}[BE + CF] {tex}\times{/tex} EF
    = {tex}\frac 12{/tex}[20 + 50] {tex}\times{/tex} (1.00 - 0.75) km = 8.75 km

questions.question: A man walks on a straight road from his home to a market 5.5 km away with a speed of 5.5 km h-1. Finding the market closed he instantly turns and walks back with a speed of 7.5 km h-1. What is the average speed and average velocity of the man between t = 0 to t = 104 min?
questions.explanation:
Time taken by the man to go from his home to the market, t1 = {tex}\frac{5.5 \mathrm{~km}}{5.5 \mathrm{~km} \mathrm{~h}^{-1}}{/tex} = 1.0 h
Time taken by the man to return back from the market to his home, t2 = {tex}\frac{5.5 \mathrm{~km}}{7.5 \mathrm{~km} \mathrm{~h}^{-1}}{/tex} = 0.73 h
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Total time taken = t1 + t2 = 1.0 h + 0.73 h = 1.73 h = 140 min
In t = 0 to 104 min, total distance travelled = 5.5 km + 5.5 km = 11 km
Displacement = 0 (As the boy returns back home)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Average speed
= {tex}\frac{\text { Distance travelled }}{\text { Time taken }}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{11 \mathrm{~km}}{1.73 \mathrm{~h}}{/tex} = 6.36 km h-1
Average velocity = {tex}\frac{\text { Displacement }}{\text { Time taken }}{/tex} = 0

questions.question: Two racing cars of masses m1 and m2 are moving in circles of radii r1 and r2 respectively. Their speeds are such that each makes a complete circle in the same length of time T. The ratio of angular speed of the first car to that of the second car is
questions.explanation:
Both the cars make a complete circle in the same time T, which means that angular speeds of both the cars are same.
{tex}\because{/tex} {tex}\omega{/tex} = {tex}\frac vr{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2 \pi r}{T r}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2 r}{T }{/tex}

questions.question: The speed of a train increases at a constant rate a from zero to v, and then remains constant for an interval, and finally decreases to zero at a constant rate p. If L be the total distance travelled, then the total time taken is
questions.explanation:
Velocity increases from 0 to v:
We know that v = u + at
Here, u = 0, v = v, a = {tex}\alpha{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} t = {tex}\frac v {\alpha}{/tex}
and s = ut + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} at2; s1 = {tex}\frac{v^2}{2 \alpha}{/tex}
Velocity decreases from v to 0:
Here, u = v, v = 0, a = -{tex}\beta{/tex}
Using v = u + at
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} t = {tex}\frac{v}{\beta}{/tex}
and s = ut + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}at2; s2 = {tex}\frac{v^2}{2 \beta}{/tex}
So, distance travelled during acceleration and retardation,
d = s1 + s2 = {tex}​​\frac{v^2}{2}\left(\frac{1}{\alpha}+\frac{1}{\beta}\right){/tex}
Thus, distance travelled during constant velocity
S3 = L -{tex}​​\frac{v^2}{2}\left(\frac{1}{\alpha}+\frac{1}{\beta}\right){/tex}
So, time taken to travel this distance
{tex}t=\frac{L-\frac{v^2}{2}\left(\frac{1}{\alpha}+\frac{1}{\beta}\right)}{v}{/tex} = {tex}\frac Lv{/tex} - {tex}\frac v2{/tex}{tex}\left(\frac{1}{\alpha}+\frac{1}{\beta}\right){/tex}
Hence, total time taken to cover distance L
= {tex}\left(\frac{v}{\alpha}\right){/tex} + {tex}\left[\frac{L}{v}-\frac{v}{2}\left(\frac{1}{\alpha}+\frac{1}{\beta}\right)\right]{/tex} + {tex}\left(\frac{v}{\beta}\right){/tex}
= {tex}\frac Lv{/tex} + {tex}\frac 2v{/tex}{tex}\left(\frac{1}{\alpha}+\frac{1}{\beta}\right){/tex}

questions.question: A train starts from a station P and travels some distance with a uniform acceleration a1, then it goes with uniform retardation a2 for some more distance and come to rest at the station Q. If the distance between the stations P and Q is 4 km and the train takes 4 minutes to complete this journey, then {tex}\frac{1}{a_1}+\frac{1}{a_2}{/tex} =
questions.explanation:
For motion with uniform acceleration a1:
From v = u + at
v = a1t1 ({tex}\because{/tex} u = 0), {tex}\therefore{/tex} t1 = {tex}\frac{v}{a_1}{/tex} ...(i)
and from s = ut + {tex}\frac 12{/tex}at2,
Using eqn. (i)
s1 = {tex}\frac 12{/tex}a1{tex}\left(\frac{v}{a_1}\right)^2{/tex} = {tex}\frac{v^2}{2 a_1}{/tex} ...(ii)
For motion with uniform retardation a2:
From v = u + at
v = a2t2 ({tex}\because{/tex} v = 0, u = v, a = -a2)
t2 = {tex}\left(\frac{v}{a_2}\right){/tex} ...(iii)
and from s = ut + {tex}\frac 12{/tex}at2
s2 = {tex}v \frac{v}{a_2}+\frac{1}{2}\left(-a_2\right) \cdot \frac{v^2}{a_2^2}{/tex}
s2 = {tex}\frac{v^2}{a_2}-\frac{v^2}{2 a_2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{v^2}{2 \mathrm{a}_2}{/tex} ...(iv)
Given, s1 + s2 = 4 km and t1 + t2 = 4 min, so using (ii) and (iv),
{tex}\frac{v^2}{2}\left(\frac{1}{a_1}+\frac{1}{a_2}\right){/tex}= 4 ...(v)
and v{tex}\left(\frac{1}{a_1}+\frac{1}{a_2}\right){/tex} = 4 ...(vi)
Dividing eqn. (v) by eqn. (vi), we get v = 2
Putting this in eqn. (vi),
{tex}\frac{1}{a_1}+\frac{1}{a_2}{/tex} = 2{tex}\frac{\mathrm{min}^2}{\mathrm{~km}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2 \times 3600}{1000} \frac{s^2}{m}{/tex}
= 7.2 m-1 s2

questions.question: The diagram shows the velocity-time graph of two moving cars P and Q. The graph indicates that

options.option: Acceleration of the car Q is not zero at any point during whole journey.
The velocity of car P is increasing at a decreasing rate from 40 s to 45 s in same direction.
Car Q is moving with a constant acceleration from 0 to 40 seconds.

questions.explanation: From 40 s to 45 s, direction of car P is same but velocity is increasing at a decreasing rate of acceleration.

questions.question: After jumping out from the plane, a parachutist falls 80 m without friction. When he opens up the parachute, he decelerates at 2 m s-2. He reaches the ground with a speed of 4 m s-1. How long did the parachutist spend his time in the air? (Take g = 10 m s-2)
questions.explanation:
Time taken when he falls 80 m in the air is t1, and U1= 0, a1=10 m s-2
{tex}\therefore{/tex} d = U1t1 + {tex}\frac 12{/tex}{tex}a_1 t_1^2{/tex} or 80 = 0 + {tex}\frac 12{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 10{tex}\frac 12{/tex}{tex}t_1^2{/tex}
u1 = 0
a1 = 10 m s-2
t1 = ?
u2 = v1
a2 = 2 m s-2
t2 = ?
u2 = 4 m s-1
or {tex}t_1^2{/tex} = 16; t1 = 4 s {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} v1 = u1 + a1t1
v1 = 0 + 10 {tex}\times{/tex} 4 = 40 m s-1
Since v1 = u2 {tex}\therefore{/tex} u2 = 40 m s-1
Let the time taken after he opens up his parachute and before reaches the ground is t2.
v2 = 4 ms-1, a2 = -2 m s-2

v2 = u2 + a2t2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 4 = 40 + (-2) {tex}\times{/tex} t2
2t2 = 36; t2 = 18 s
Total time = 4 s + 18 s = 22 s

questions.question: A graph in the figure shows the change in position with time for two objects A and B moving in a straight line from 0 to 10 seconds. Choose the correct option(s).

options.option: Average velocities are equal due to identical initial and final positions.
Average speed of A is greater because B slows down.
Average speed of A is lower because it covers less distance.
Average speeds are equal because both cover equal distance.

questions.explanation: From the position-time graph: both A and B start and end at the same position, so displacement is same → average velocity same. But distance covered is different (B is curved, so longer path).

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of using graphical representation for motion?
questions.explanation: Graphical representation provides a visual representation of how position, velocity, and acceleration change with time.

questions.question: What does a straight line position-time graph indicate about the motion of an object?
questions.explanation: A straight line position-time graph means the object is moving with a constant velocity.

questions.question: What physical quantity can be calculated from the slope of a velocity-time graph?
questions.explanation: The slope of the line on velocity-time graph gives how fast the velocity is changing with time, i.e., the acceleration.

questions.question: What does the area enclosed by the velocity-time graph and the time axis represent?
questions.explanation: The area enclosed by the velocity-time graph and the time axis for a desired time interval is equal to the displacement in that time interval.
