questions.question: For a vehicle in uniform motion, what does its position-time graph look like?
questions.explanation: The position-time graph is a straight line, it proves the vehicle covers equal distances in equal intervals of time', indicating uniform motion.

questions.question: What is the term for the steepness of a line on a graph?
questions.explanation: The slope of a line is the steepness of the line.

questions.question: Which of the following equations correctly relates final velocity (v), initial velocity (u), acceleration (a), and time (t) for constant acceleration?
questions.explanation: The kinematic equation relating final velocity (v), initial velocity (u), acceleration (a), and time (t) for constant acceleration is v = u + at.

questions.question: The displacement (s) of an object with initial velocity (u), constant acceleration (a), over a time interval (t) is given by which kinematic equation?
questions.explanation: The second kinematic equation describes displacement (s) in terms of initial velocity (u), acceleration (a), and time (t) as s = {tex}s = ut + (1/2)at^2{/tex}

questions.question: What is the relationship between final velocity (v), initial velocity (u), constant acceleration (a), and displacement (s)?
questions.explanation: The third kinematic equation, which relates final velocity (v), initial velocity (u), constant acceleration (a), and displacement (s), is {tex}v^2 = u^2 + 2as.{/tex}

questions.question: In a velocity-time graph for motion with constant acceleration, what does the slope of the graph represent?
questions.explanation: The slope of a velocity-time graph represents the acceleration of the object. For constant acceleration, this slope is a straight line.

questions.question: Which of the following scenarios represents motion in two dimensions?

options.option: A car moving straight on a highway.
A car climbing up a mountain road.
A bird flying straight up a tree.

questions.explanation: Motion in a plane, such as a satellite moving in a circular path, is called motion in two dimensions.

questions.question: What is represented by a straight line that meets a circle at one and only one point?
questions.explanation: A straight line that meets the circle at one and only point is called a tangent to the circle at that point.

questions.question: In uniform circular motion, what remains constant?
questions.explanation: In uniform circular motion, the speed of the object is constant.

questions.question: If an object takes time T to make one revolution in a circular path of radius R, what is its average speed?
questions.explanation:
The average speed is the total distance (2πR) divided by the total time (T), so
Average speed = 2πR/T.

questions.question: What information does the slope of a position-time graph provide?
questions.explanation: The slope of a line on a position-time graph gives the rate of change of position with respect to time, which is velocity.

questions.question: If an object's position changes from 10 m to 30 m in 4 seconds, what is its average velocity?
questions.explanation:
Average velocity = displacement / time interval
Displacement = 30 m - 10 m = 20 m
Time interval = 4 s
Average velocity = 20 m / 4 s = 5 m/s

questions.question: A car travels 200 km east in 2 hours. What is its average speed?
questions.explanation:
Average speed = total distance travelled / time interval 
Average speed = 200 km / 2 h = 100 km/h.

questions.question: What is the formula for average acceleration?

options.option: Average acceleration = change in velocity / time interval
Average acceleration = total distance travelled / time interval

questions.explanation: The average acceleration of an object over a time interval is the change in its velocity divided by the time interval.

questions.question: What is the SI unit of average velocity?
questions.explanation: The SI unit of average velocity is metre per second which is represented by ms-1 or m/s.

questions.question: Which physical quantity is defined as the change in position (or displacement) divided by the time interval?
questions.explanation: The average velocity of an object over a time interval is the change in its position (or displacement) divided by the time taken for that change.

questions.question: What is the formula for average speed?

options.option: Average speed = total distance travelled × time interval
Average speed = total distance travelled / time interval
Average speed = change in velocity / time interval

questions.explanation: The average speed of an object is the total distance travelled divided by the time interval during which this distance is covered.

questions.question: What is displacement defined as?

options.option: The time taken to cover a certain distance
The total path length covered by an object
The speed of an object in a specific direction
The net change in the position of an object.

questions.explanation: Displacement is the net change in the position of an object between the two given instants of time.

questions.question: What are physical quantities that can be completely described by only their numerical value called?
questions.explanation: Physical quantities that are fully described by numerical value only and have no direction are called scalar quantities.

questions.question: When is an object said to be in motion?
questions.explanation: If the position of the object with respect to the reference point changes with time, the object is said to be in motion.

questions.question: What is the simplest type of motion an object can exhibit?
questions.explanation: When an object moves along a straight line, the motion is called linear motion. It is considered the simplest type of motion because it occurs in one direction along a straight path.

questions.question: If the initial velocity (u) is 54 km/h and the final velocity (v) is 0 m/s due to an acceleration of -4 m/s2, what is the distance traveled (s) by the object before stopping? (Note: 54 km/h = 15 m/s)
questions.explanation:
Using the kinematic equation v2 = u2 + 2as.
Given u = 15 m/s, v = 0 m/s, a = -4 m/s2.
So, 02 = (15)2 + 2(-4)s
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 0 = 225 - 8s
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 8s = 225
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} s = 225/8 = 28.125 m, which rounds to 28.1 m.

questions.question: A car moving at an initial velocity of 15 m/s applies brakes, resulting in a constant acceleration of -3 m/s2. How long will it take for the car to come to a complete stop?
questions.explanation:
Using the kinematic equation v = u + at,
where u = 15 m/s, v = 0 m/s (comes to a stop),
and a = -3 m/s2.
Solving for t: 0 = 15 + (-3)t
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3t = 15 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} t = 5 seconds.

questions.question: An object starts from rest and accelerates at a constant rate of 2 m/s2. What is its velocity after 5 seconds?
questions.explanation:
Using the kinematic equation v = u + at, where u = 0 m/s (starts from rest),
a = 2 m/s2, and t = 5 s.
So, v = 0 + (2 m/s2)(5 s) = 10 m/s.

questions.question: A car drives 200 km north in three hours, then 200 km south in two hours. Which of the following is true regarding its average speed and average velocity for the entire trip?

options.option: Both average speed and average velocity are 80 km/h.
Average speed is 80 km/h and average velocity is 0 km/h.
Both average speed and average velocity are 0 km/h.
Average speed is 0 km/h and average velocity is 80 km/h.

questions.explanation:
Total distance = 200 km + 200 km = 400 km.
Total time = 3 h + 2 h = 5 h.
Average speed = 400 km/5 h = 80 km/h.
Displacement = 200 km (North) - 200 km (South) = 0 km.
Average velocity = 0 km/5 h = 0 km/h.

questions.question: Two postmen walk towards each other from a distance of 210 yojanas. One travels 9 yojanas per day, and the other 5 yojanas per day. How many days will it take for them to meet?
questions.explanation:
Their combined speed is 9 + 5 = 14 yojanas per day.
To meet, they need to cover a total of 210 yojanas.
Time = Total distance / Combined speed = 210/14 = 15 days.

questions.question: A bus is moving with a velocity of 54 km/h and then applies brakes, bringing it to a stop in 5 seconds. What is its average acceleration during braking?
questions.explanation:
First, convert 54 km/h to m/s: 54 {tex}\times{/tex} (1000/3600) = 15 m/s.
Initial velocity (u) = 15 m/s,
final velocity (v) = 0 m/s,
time (t) = 5 s.
Average acceleration = (v - u) / t = (0 - 15) / 5 = -3 m/s2.

questions.question: A bus moving on a highway increases its velocity from 36 km/h to 54 km/h in 10 seconds. What is its average acceleration during this period?
questions.explanation:
First, convert velocities to m/s: 36 km/h = 10 m/s and 54 km/h = 15 m/s.
Average acceleration = (final velocity - initial velocity) / time

Average acceleration = (15 m/s - 10 m/s) / 10 s = 5 m/s / 10 s = 0.5 m/s2.

questions.question: A swimmer takes 50 seconds to swim from one end of a 25 m pool to the other end and back to the starting point. What is the swimmer's average speed for this 50-second interval?
questions.explanation:
The total distance travelled is 25 m (one way) + 25 m (back) = 50 m.
The time taken is 50 s.
Average speed = Total distance / Time = 50 m / 50 s = 1 m/s.

questions.question: Imagine an object starts at position 0 cm, moves to 40 cm, and then moves back to 0 cm. According to the definition of displacement, what is its final displacement from the starting point?
questions.explanation: Displacement is the net change in position. Since the object starts at 0 cm and returns to 0 cm, the net change in position is 0 cm.

questions.question: An athlete starts at point O, runs to point A (100 m from O), then turns and runs back to point B (40 m from O). If the total time taken is 10 seconds to reach A and 6 seconds to reach B on the return journey (total time is 16 seconds for the entire movement), what is the total distance travelled by the athlete?
questions.explanation:
The athlete runs 100 m from O to A, and then 60 m(100m - 40m) from A to B.
Total distance travelled = 100 m + 60 m = 160 m.

questions.question: An object's motion is classified as 'motion in a straight line' when it moves along a linear path. Which of the following examples exhibits motion in a straight line?

options.option: A train moving on a straight track.
A ball rolling down an inclined track.

questions.explanation: A train moving on a straight track is a direct example of motion in a straight line, as its path is explicitly stated as linear.

questions.question: What is the primary characteristic that defines if an object is 'in motion' or 'at rest' relative to a reference point?
questions.explanation: An object is considered in motion if its position changes relative to a reference point over time. If its position does not change, it is at rest.

questions.question: In the context of describing an object's position, what does the 'reference point' represent?

options.option: The speed at which the object is moving.
The total distance the object has travelled.

questions.explanation: The reference point is a specified fixed point used to determine the distance and direction of an object at any given instant.

questions.question: An instant of time is distinct from a time interval. How is an 'instant of time' best defined?

options.option: The total time elapsed since an event started.
The measurement of how long an event lasts.
A single reading of a clock at a given point.

questions.explanation: An instant of time refers to a specific, single moment as indicated by a clock reading.

questions.question: What is the key difference between 'total distance travelled' and 'displacement'?

options.option: Distance is actual path length, while displacement is straight-line separation.
Distance is scalar, while displacement is vector.
Distance uses metres, while displacement uses kilometres.
Total distance travelled can be negative, but displacement cannot.

questions.explanation: Total distance travelled is a scalar quantity, only requiring magnitude, whereas displacement is a vector quantity, requiring both magnitude and direction, representing the net change in position.

questions.question: The SI unit for both total distance travelled and displacement is the metre. What characteristic determines whether a physical quantity, like displacement, is a vector or scalar?
questions.explanation: Displacement is a vector quantity because it requires both a magnitude (numerical value) and a direction for its complete description.

questions.question: What is the definition of 'average speed'?

options.option: The rate at which an object changes its direction.
The total displacement divided by the time interval.
The final velocity minus the initial velocity.

questions.explanation: Average speed is defined as the total distance travelled divided by the total time taken for that travel.

subtopics.name: Kinematic Equations for Motion in a Straight Line with Constant Acceleration

questions.question: Under what specific condition is the magnitude of an object's average velocity equal to its average speed?

options.option: When the object moves in a straight line without reversing.
When the object travels in a circular path.
When the object returns to its starting point.

questions.explanation: If an object moves in a straight line without changing direction, its total distance travelled will be equal to the magnitude of its displacement. Thus, average speed (total distance/time) will equal the magnitude of average velocity (magnitude of displacement/time).

questions.question: Consider a ball thrown vertically upwards from point O, which moves up to B and then falls back to O. Can this motion be considered 'motion in a straight line'?

options.option: Yes, because its path is a single vertical line.
No, because the ball changes direction at point B.
Only if the ball travels at a constant velocity.

questions.explanation: Motion in a straight line means the object follows a linear path. A ball thrown vertically upwards and falling back down follows the same vertical line, making it a straight-line motion, despite the change in direction of travel.

questions.question: A car accelerates from rest. Its average acceleration is in the direction of its increasing velocity. If the car then slows down to a stop, how does its average acceleration relate to its direction of velocity during deceleration?

options.option: It is opposite to the direction of the slowing velocity.
It is in the same direction as the slowing velocity.
It remains in the initial direction of motion.

questions.explanation: If an object is slowing down, its acceleration is directed opposite to its velocity. This causes the decrease in the magnitude of velocity.

questions.question: When is an object moving at a constant speed, yet its average velocity is zero over a certain period?

options.option: The object completes a closed loop or returns to its starting point.
The object is accelerating steadily in one direction.
The object is instantaneously at rest at some point during the motion.
The object moves in a straight line without changing direction.

questions.explanation: If an object completes a closed loop or returns to its starting point, its net displacement is zero. Therefore, its average velocity (displacement/time) will be zero, even if it traveled a significant distance at a constant speed.

questions.question: An object can be moving very fast and yet have zero acceleration. What condition must be met for this to be true?

options.option: Its velocity is constant, so speed and direction do not change.
Its position is changing rapidly, but its mass is negligible.
It is momentarily at rest before changing direction.
It is moving in a perfect circle at a constant speed.

questions.explanation: Acceleration is the rate of change of velocity. If an object is moving very fast but its velocity (both speed and direction) is constant, then its acceleration is zero.

questions.question: If an object's position-time graph is a straight line parallel to the time axis, what does that indicate about its motion?

options.option: The object is completely at rest state.
The object is changing direction of motion.
The object is moving with constant acceleration.
The object is moving with increasing velocity.

questions.explanation: A position-time graph that is a straight line parallel to the time axis indicates that the position of the object is not changing, meaning it is stationary or at rest.

questions.question: What is represented by the term 'instantaneous acceleration'?

options.option: The total change in velocity over a given time.
The acceleration at a specific moment in time.
The velocity of an object as it begins to move.

questions.explanation: Instantaneous acceleration describes the acceleration of an object at a particular instant, similar to instantaneous velocity referring to velocity at a specific moment.

questions.question: A vehicle starts from rest and its position-time data are (0s, 0m), (2s, 1m), (4s, 4m), (6s, 9m). If this data is plotted, what would be the characteristic of the resulting graph?

options.option: A straight line parallel to time axis showing rest

questions.explanation: Given data shows that the displacement increases non-linearly with time. This non-linear relationship on a position-time graph forms a curve, which is characteristic of accelerated motion.

questions.question: If a position-time graph is used to calculate average velocity, how is it determined using two points (s1, t1) and (s2, t2)?

options.option: By dividing the change in position (s2 - s1) by the change in time (t2 - t1).
By finding the product of (s2 - s1) and (t2 - t1).
By dividing the change in time (t2 - t1) by the change in position (s2 - s1).

questions.explanation: Average velocity is defined as the total displacement divided by the total time taken. On a position-time graph, this corresponds to (s2 - s1) / (t2 - t1).

questions.question: Why is it crucial to determine a suitable scale for each quantity when plotting motion graphs?

options.option: To ensure the graph fills exactly half of the available paper.
Scale only affects appearance, not accuracy of graph.
To represent data effectively using available space.
It helps calculate physical quantities without using formulas.

questions.explanation: Choosing an appropriate scale is essential to make the graph legible, allow for accurate interpretation, and prevent data points from being too clustered or too spread out, thereby optimizing the use of space and clarity.

questions.question: When interpreting a velocity-time graph, a negative value for acceleration (such as -0.5 m/s2) means what about the object's motion?

options.option: The object is accelerating at a faster rate.
The object is moving in the opposite direction.

questions.explanation: A negative acceleration value, often called deceleration or retardation, indicates that the acceleration vector is in the direction opposite to the velocity vector, causing the object to slow down.

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of the kinematic equations?

options.option: To determine the forces acting on an object.
To calculate the mass of a moving object.
To analyze motion in a plane with varying acceleration.

questions.explanation: Kinematic equations relate displacement, time, initial velocity, final velocity, and acceleration to describe and predict an object's motion over time, specifically under constant acceleration.

questions.question: In uniform circular motion, what best describes the velocity of the object?

options.option: Speed is constant, but direction changes continuously.

questions.explanation: In uniform circular motion, the speed of the object is constant, but its direction continuously changes as it moves along the circular path.

questions.question: If a child on a merry-go-round makes one complete revolution, what is their total displacement?
questions.explanation: Displacement is the straight-line distance and direction from the starting point to the ending point. After one complete revolution, the child returns to their original position, resulting in zero displacement.

questions.question: Why is uniform circular motion considered an accelerated motion?

options.option: Because the speed of the object is increasing.
Because the object is moving in a curved path.
Because the magnitude of its velocity is changing.
Because the direction of the velocity continuously changes.

questions.explanation: Acceleration is defined as the rate of change of velocity. Even if the speed is constant in uniform circular motion, the continuous change in the direction of velocity means there is an acceleration.

questions.question: What happens to the direction of an athlete's velocity as they run along a closed track with an increasing number of sides?
questions.explanation: As the number of sides in a closed track increases, the athlete must change direction more frequently to stay on the path, causing the velocity direction to change more often.

questions.question: What is the characteristic of velocity at any point for an object in circular motion?

options.option: It is always parallel to the radius of the circle.
It is directed towards the center of the circle.
It is directed perpendicular to the tangent at that point.
It is along the tangent to the circle at that point.

questions.explanation: The velocity at a given point in circular motion is always tangential to the circle at that specific point, indicating the instantaneous direction of motion.

questions.question: If a marble is rotating inside a ring and the ring is suddenly lifted, in which direction will the marble continue moving immediately after leaving the ring?
questions.explanation: According to Newton's first law of motion (or inertia), an object in motion will continue in a straight line at a constant speed unless acted upon by an external force. When the ring is removed, the force constraining the marble to a circular path is removed, so it moves in the direction of its instantaneous velocity, which is tangential to the circle.

questions.question: What is the key difference between distance travelled and displacement for a child completing one revolution on a merry-go-round?

options.option: Both distance travelled and displacement are zero.

questions.explanation: Distance travelled is the total path covered, which is the circumference (2πR) for one revolution. Displacement is the straight-line change in position from start to end; since the child returns to the starting point, the displacement is zero.

questions.question: Why is uniform circular motion considered an 'idealised model' for real-world situations like planetary motion or vehicles making turns?

options.option: Real conditions are not exactly circular or constant speed.
Because it only applies to microscopic particles.
Because it simplifies motion to one dimension.

questions.explanation: Uniform circular motion assumes perfectly constant speed and a perfectly circular path, which are rarely achieved precisely in complex real-world scenarios. Therefore, it serves as an idealised model allowing for foundational understanding.

questions.question: Which of the following solution scatter light?
questions.explanation: As the particle size of both colloidal & suspension is large they are able to scatter light.

questions.question: Which of the following methods would you use to separate cream from milk?
questions.explanation: In centrifugation by churning the milk at a high speed, the cream collects at the center and being lighter than milk floats at the top of the mixture. As the cream is lighter than milk.

questions.question: Blood and seawater are:-

options.option: Blood is a mixture whereas seawater is a compound
Blood is a compound and seawater is a mixture

questions.explanation: Blood and seawater are both mixtures as blood contains many components like RBC and WBC and many more, as well as seawater also contain NaCl and other components as a mixture.

questions.question: Sol and Gel are examples of

options.option: Sol is a solid-liquid colloid and Gel is a liquid-solid colloid
Sol is a solid-solid colloid and Gel is a solid-liquid colloid
Sol is a liquid-solid colloid and Gel is a liquid-liquid colloid

questions.explanation: Sol is a solid-liquid colloid and Gel is a liquid-solid colloid

questions.question: Sugar dissolves in water to form a sugar solution. What is the name of the solute?
questions.explanation: Sugar dissolves in water to form a sugar solution. Here sugar is solute and water is solvent.

questions.question: Dissolving sugar is an example of:
questions.explanation: We can get back sugar crystals by evaporating water.

questions.question: Name the technique to separate camphor from salt.
questions.explanation: The process of sublimation will help in separating camphor from salt. Camphor  is a sublimating substance and on heating it converts directly into gases.

questions.question: The substance which does not form a true solution in water is:
questions.explanation: Egg albumin has a particle size of the order 10-9 to 10-12 i.e. of the order of colloidal size. Due to which it is not able to form a clear solution in water and results into a colloidal solution.

questions.question: The components of a mixture of iron filings and sulphur powder are separated by:
questions.explanation: Iron fillings are removed with the help of magnet due to their magnetic properties while sulphur is left behind as it lacks magnetic properties.

questions.question: When you add carbon disulphide in a test tube containing a mixture of iron fillings and sulphur powder, then what would you observe after shaking the test tube well?

options.option: Sulphur dissolves to from colourless solution and iron filings settles down
After sometimes, carbon disulphide, sulphur and iron filings form three separate layers in the test-tube
Yellow solution is formed and iron filings settles down.

questions.explanation: Sulphur dissolves to from colourless solution because carbon disulphide is a colourless volatile liquid and a non polar solvent. Being heavy iron filings settles down under the effect of gravity.

questions.question: The aqueous mixture of salt and sand can be separated by the following method:
questions.explanation: To separate the mixture of salt and sand; first, we will make the aqueous solution, salt gets dissolved in water and sand particle remain undissolved, so, the mixture can be separated by filtration and evaporation.

questions.question: Conversion of solid directly into its vapour form is called:
questions.explanation: Sublimation is the phase transition of a substance directly from the solid to the gas phase without passing through the intermediate liquid phase.

questions.question: Sublimation can be used to separate:
questions.explanation: Sublimation is the process in which sublimable volatile solid components can be separated from non-volatile solids by converting it into its vapours.

questions.question: The color of the pure ammonium chloride powder is ________.
questions.explanation: Ammonium chloride is an inorganic compound with the formula NH4Cl and a white crystalline salt that is highly soluble in water.

questions.question: Which sublimate substance is used to protect warm clothes?
questions.explanation: Naphthalene is an organic compound with formula C10H8. It is a sublimable volatile substance, Naphthalene has been used as a household fumigant. Its vapours keep insects away.

questions.question: Metanil yellow, an adulterant used in Arhar dal is basically:
questions.explanation: Metanil yellow is a yellow colour dye, generally used colouring food material, for e.g. starch when dyed with metanil yellow looks like turmeric. Poor quality dal when dyed with metanil yellow looks superior quality dal.

questions.question: A food sample turned blue-black after addition of a few drops of iodine solution. This sample contains:
questions.explanation: Many different food groups contain a carbohydrate known as starch. Using an iodine solution, you can test for the presence of starch. When starch is present, the iodine changes from brown to blue-black or purple.

questions.question: Which one of the following will form a translucent solution in water?
questions.explanation: Starch forms a colloidal solution. Colloidal solutions are translucent and their particles can pass through filter paper to give a translucent filtrate.

questions.question: Which will not give a stable solution even when stirred for sometimes?
questions.explanation: Egg albumin in water will not give a stable solution. The protein in egg albumin coagulated to form a lump in hot water.

questions.question: The system when starch is added to hot water is:
questions.explanation: The colloid of starch is prepared by the dispersion method. 2-3 g of powdered/crushed starch is dissolved in 3- 4 ml of water to make a thin paste. This paste is added to100 ml of boiling water while stirring. Allow the solution to cool and filter. The filtrate is colloid of starch.

questions.question: Which type of solution is formed when sand and water are mixed thoroughly and then kept undisturbed for some time?
questions.explanation: Sand will form a suspension in water. If you shake a container of sand and water, the sand spreads through the water, forming a cloudy liquid. The sand will then settle to the bottom of the container as sediment. Suspensions often need to be shaken or stirred before use to spread the sediment through the liquid.

questions.question: The steam of the funnel must be kept, white during filtration:

options.option: no touching the inner wall of the beaker
touching the inner wall of the beaker

questions.explanation: If solid is to be separated from the liquid using a filtering process, then the filter paper has to be properly prepared. fold the filter paper in half, then fold again to 10 degrees of a 90-degree fold. tear off the corner of the outer fold. Place snugly into a funnel and moisten the filter paper with a solvent of the mixture being filtered, then press the paper against the top wall of the funnel to form a seal. It saves time because otherwise droplets will be formed which time to fall down.

questions.question: Which of the following settles down when allowed to stand undisturbed doe sometimes?
questions.explanation: Muddy water will settle down because particles are heavy and settle due to gravity. Setting down of coarse particles under the influence of gravity is called sedimentation. During sedimentation, heavier particles settle down faster than finer particles.

questions.question: Tyndall effect is observed in which one of the following?
questions.explanation: Starch forms a colloid in water (hot water).

questions.question: A student mixed the white of an egg with water and stirred it well. After sometimes he observe that:

options.option: egg white settles down at the bottom
egg white floats on the surface of the water

questions.explanation: Egg white is translucent.  Egg albumin forms colloidal solution which is translucent.

questions.question: Choose the correct observation about a solution of sugar in water prepared by you in the laboratory.

options.option: Solid particles settle at the bottom after sometimes
Its components can be separated by filtration
Its particles are seen by naked eyes

questions.explanation: A solution is always transparent, light passes through it without scattering as the solute particles are very small in size. So, the solution of sugar in water is transparent.

subtopics.name: Separating the Components of a Mixture

questions.question: The particles of the colloidal solution are:
questions.explanation: The size of particles of the colloidal solution lies between 10-7 cm to 10-4 cm in diameter. So, The particles of colloidal solutions are visible with a powerful microscope.

questions.question: To prepare a colloidal solution of starch, we should:

options.option: add the thin paste of starch to hot water with stirring
add starch powder to cold water and boil
add the starch powder to boiling water and cool
heat starch, add it to cold water and then bring it to boil

questions.explanation: The colloid of starch is prepared by the dispersion method. 2-3 g of powdered/crushed starch is dissolved in 3-4 ml of water to make a thin paste. This paste is added to100 ml of boiling water while stirring. Allow the solution to cool and filter. The filtrate is colloid of starch.

questions.question: A student by mistake mixed iron filings and sulphur powder. He wanted to separate them from each other. The method you would advise him to use it to dissolve the mixture in
questions.explanation: Addition of carbon disulphide to a mixture containing iron filings and sulphur powder leads to the formation of a clear yellow solution when sulphur powder dissolves in carbon disulphide, on gentle shaking. Iron fillings being insoluble settle in the bottom. These can be separated by filtration. When the solution is allowed to evaporate, the powder of solid sulphur is obtained.

questions.question: Carbon disulphide should be kept away from flame because:
questions.explanation: Carbon disulphide can be combustible in the presence of open flames and sparks.

questions.question: Move magnet over (i) mixture of iron filings and sulphur (ii) over FeS and choose the correct observation

options.option: Iron filings and FeS both are attracted by a magnet
Iron filings are attracted towards the magnet, FeS is not
FeS is attracted by magnet whereas iron does not
Both (i) and (ii) are not attracted by a magnet

questions.explanation:
Move magnet over a mixture of iron filings and sulphur Iron filings are attracted to the magnet but sulfur is not. This proves that the mixture is not yet a compound, and is a mixture, as it can be separated by physical means.
Compound FeS is not attracted by a magnet.

questions.question: Add dil. HCl to (i) mixture of Fe and sulphur (ii) iron sulphide and choose the correct observation:

options.option: A mixture of iron and sulphur reacts with HCl to give sulphur
FeS reacts with HCl to give H2 gas
Mixture of iron and sulphur reacts with HCl to give H2S gas

questions.explanation:
If we will take a mixture of Fe and sulfur and add Dilute HCl only iron will react to form FeCl2, the reaction will take place as follows
Fe + 2HCl {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} FeCl2 + HCl
But if the mixture of Fe and S is heated they form FeS. If we add HCl in FeS it will release H2S,  Reaction takes place as follows:
FeS + 2HCl {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} FeCl + H2S
H2S gas is given off and iron chloride is produced.

questions.question: The process of evaporation is fast when the mixture is:
questions.explanation: Evaporation is a type of vaporization, that occurs on the surface of a liquid as it changes into the gaseous phase. When heating is done and the mixture is not covered at that time evaporation is fast.

questions.question: In the laboratory, what precaution has to be taken with carbon disulphide?
questions.explanation: Carbon disulphide is an extremely flammable and dangerous fire hazard so, it should be kept away from the flame as it is inflammable.

questions.question: Which one of the following will result in the formation of a mixture?

options.option: Breaking of ice cubes into small pieces
Crushing of a marble tile into small particles
Agitating a detergent with water in a washing machine

questions.explanation: Detergent in water will form a mixture, others will not form a mixture.

questions.question: What is observed when carbon disulphide is added to a mixture of iron filings and sulphur powder taken in a test tube?

options.option: Sulphur powder dissolves to give a colourless solution

questions.explanation: Addition of carbon disulphide to a mixture containing iron filings and sulphur powder leads to the formation of a clear yellow solution when sulphur powder dissolves in carbon disulphide, on gentle shaking. The sulphur powder dissolves in carbon disulphide to form a colourless solution. Iron fillings being insoluble settle in the bottom. These can be separated by filtration. When the solution is allowed to evaporate, the powder of solid sulphur is obtained.

questions.question: A mixture of iron filings and sulphur is heated in a hard boiling tube. Which of the following will be observed?

options.option: The mixture becomes red hot without melting

questions.explanation: When we start heating a mixture of iron filing and sulphur, the sulphur melts reacting exothermically (that is with the production of heat) with the iron filings to form the compound Iron (II) sulfide. The compound formed has properties that are different from both sulfur and iron. For instance, the compound does not have the magnetic properties of iron, and thus a magnet that could have easily separated iron nails and sulphur initially, will not be able to separate iron and sulphur from Iron (II) sulphide.

questions.question: The process used to separate a mixture of iron fillings, iodine, and common salt is:

options.option: dissolution in water followed by sublimation and magnetic separation

questions.explanation:
Sublimation is the best method for separating iodine from common salt (NaCl). Since iodine is sublimable, it will change to vapour state directly from solid when heated slightly and the iodine vapours can be collected while common salt remains as such.
Magnetic Separation method of separation is exemplified by the separation of iron filings.

questions.question: When iron filings and powdered sulphur are mixed together in a china dish:

options.option: the constituents can be separated by a magnet
the constituents present can easily be seen

questions.explanation: Iron filings and sulphur powder will form a heterogeneous mixture, particles can be easily seen and iron filings can be easily seen and iron filings can be removed by a magnet.

questions.question: A mixture of iron filings and sulphur is heated, the colour of the mixture will change
questions.explanation:
A mixture of iron filings and sulphur is heated, the colour of the mixture will change yellow to black.
S is black in colour and FeS is black in colour.
Iron + sulphur {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} Ferrous sulphide
Fe + S {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} FeS

questions.question: What is not observed when a magnet is moved repeatedly through a mixture of iron filings and sulphur placed on a paper?

options.option: A black mass of iron sulphide is formed
Sulphur powder is left on the paper

questions.explanation: Iron filling are attracted to the bar magnet. A black substance is formed on heating. Sulphur dissolves in CS2 and a yellow solution is formed. Solid sulphur reappears when CS2 is evaporated. So all statements are correct.

questions.question: What is the order of methods applied to separate the components of a mixture of salt, sand, and ammonium chloride?

options.option: Sublimation, dissolving in water, filtration and evaporation
Dissolving in water, filtration, evaporation, and sublimation
Moving a magnet, dissolving in water and sublimation

questions.explanation: Ammonium chloride sublime and can be separated from the mixture of salt and sand by the process of sublimation. Sand can be separated from the mixture of sand and salt by dissolving salt in water and then by filtration. Salt can be separated through the process of evaporation.

questions.question: A mixture of common salt and water can be separated completely by the process of:
questions.explanation: A mixture of common salt and water can be separated completely by the process of distillation. The distillation can be used to separate a liquid from dissolved non-volatile solids. The process of distillation is used to obtain drinking water from sea-water in many countries.

questions.question: Recovery of salt solution in water can be done by:
questions.explanation: Although the salt is dissociated in solution, it still retains the property that it has a different boiling point from water. The water will evaporate first, leaving salt crystals behind.

questions.question: A mixture of sulphur and carbon disulphide is

options.option: heterogeneous and does not show Tyndall effect
homogeneous and does not show Tyndall effect

questions.explanation:
Sulphur and carbon disulphide do not form a uniform composition and the properties of the mixture are not same throughout.
Therefore, it forms a heterogeneous composition.
Moreover, it shows a Tyndall effect, because in water sulphur remains suspended whereas carbon disulphide settles down as a layer at the bottom.

questions.question: Tincture of iodine has antiseptic properties. This solution is made by dissolving
questions.explanation: Tincture of iodine solution is made by dissolving iodine in alcohol. It contains around 2 - 7% iodine dissolved in a mixture of ethanol and water.

questions.question:
Which of the following are homogeneous in nature

  1. ice
  2. wood
  3. soil
  4. air
questions.explanation:
A mixture is said to be a homogeneous mixture if its constituents are distributed uniformly and are not physically distinct. Wood and soil are heterogeneous mixtures. Ice is made up of water and water is a pure compound. Air is a mixture of various gases.
Ice and air are homogeneous in nature.

questions.question: Arun has prepared 0.01% (by mass) solution of sodium chloride in water. Which of the following correctly represents the composition of the solutions?
questions.explanation:
Mass% = {tex}\frac{\text { mass of solute } }{ \text { (mass of solute }+\text { mass of solvent) } }\times 100{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{0.01 g \times 100}{(0.01+99.99) 0}=\frac{0.01 \times 100}{100.00}=0.01 \%{/tex}
Hence, the mass of solute =0.01 g  and the mass of solvent = 99.99gms.

questions.question: Rohan, a class 9 student mixed some iron filings and sulphur in a China dish and heated them strongly to obtain a residue. Which of the following is not a characteristic property of the residue?

options.option: Its appearance is different from iron and sulphur but it shows chemical properties of both iron and sulphur.
It cannot be separated into sulphur and iron filings by normal physical methods.
Its properties are completely different from iron and sulphur.

questions.explanation: Iron reacts with sulphur on heating to form a new compound, iron sulphide which has totally different properties than that of iron and sulphur and cannot be separated into iron and sulphur by normal physical methods.

questions.question: Preeti heated a mixture of iodine and common salt by keeping an inverted funnel on it. After some time, she observed that

options.option: Violet particles deposit on the neck of the funnel
White particles deposit on the neck of the funnel
White fumes come out from the mixture

questions.explanation: Iodine being a sublimable solid, sublimes from the mixture of iodine and common salt as violet iodine vapours and condense on the neck of the funnel as violet particles.

questions.question: Priyanka poured 20 g of salt into 200 mL of water in a beaker. She stirred the water to dissolve the salt completely. Then she heated the solution until it was reduced to half. How many grams of salt can be recovered from the remaining solution?
questions.explanation: Same amount of salt will be recovered as in the process of evaporation only solvent (i.e., water) evaporates and the solute (i.e., salt) is left behind in the beaker in the form of residue.

questions.question:
A food sample was tested for the presence of components P, G, R and S and the following chromatogram was obtained.

The components not present in food sample are
questions.explanation: Chromatogram of food sample does not match with the chromatograms of components Q and S.

questions.question: The constituents of a heterogeneous mixture are X, Y and Z. If mixture containing X and Y is taken, X can be separated using magnetic separation, Y and Z can be separated using distillation method. Which of the following best identifies X, Y and Z respectively?
questions.explanation: 'X' is magnetic in nature so it can be iron. Distillation method is generally used to separate salt and water from the salt-water solution.

questions.question: What is the function of X in the given experimental set-up?

options.option: To apply pressure to the mixture in distillation flask
To allow the water from the mixture to move out
To allow only lower boiling component to escape

questions.explanation: X is the water condenser which allows the vapours to cool and condense.

questions.question: Which of the following solutions has the highest mass by mass percentage?

options.option: 20 g of sodium carbonate in 90 g of water
15 g of sugar in 160 g of water
10 g of sodium chloride in 200 g of water
60 g of potassium permanganate in 200 g of water

questions.explanation:
Mass by mass percentage = {tex}\frac{\text { Mass of solute }}{\text { Mass of solution }} {/tex}  {tex}\times{/tex} 100
Concentration of solution I = {tex}\frac{10}{10+200}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 100 = 4.76%
Concentration of solution II = {tex}\frac{15}{160+15}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 100 = 8.57%
Concentration of solution III = {tex}\frac{60}{200+60}{/tex}  {tex}\times{/tex} 100 = 23.07%
Concentration of solution IV = {tex}\frac{20}{90+20}{/tex}  {tex}\times{/tex} 100 = 18.18%

questions.question:
Select the correct statement(s).

  1. A solution in which size of the solute particles is about 10-10 m, is called true solution.
  2. A solution which contains maximum possible amount of solute at any given temperature is called a saturated solution.
  3. In suspension, the size of particles is of the order of 10-7 m or larger.
  4. A colloid is a heterogeneous system.
questions.explanation: I, II, III and IV

questions.question:
Which of the following groups of students employed the correct method to separate the components of given mixtures?

Group Mixture Separation technique
I Ethyl alcohol + Water Separating funnel
II Ammonium chloride + Sodium chloride Sublimation
III Iron pins + Sand Magnetic separation
IV Mud particles in water Sedimentation and decantation
questions.explanation: Ethyl alcohol and water are miscible liquids so, cannot be separated by using separating funnel. This mixture can be separated by distillation method.

questions.question:
Study the given flow chart and select the correct statements.

If X is water and Y is carbon disulphide then P, Q and R could be respectively
questions.explanation: Common salt is soluble in ‘X' (i.e., water) while sulphur is soluble in 'Y' (i.e., carbon disulphide), both the solutes will be recovered from the filtrate by evaporation. But, coal is insoluble in both. It will be left behind as residue.

questions.question: Harsh dissolved a mixture into water and then filtered it. Solid X was left behind as residue in the filter paper and solid Y was obtained by evaporating the filtrate to dryness. X and Y could be respectively
questions.explanation: Calcium carbonate is insoluble in water hence, it is left behind in the filter paper. Sodium chloride is soluble in water and is obtained by evaporation of solution.

questions.question:
Which of the following statements about the given experimental set-up is/are incorrect?

  1. It is used to separate a mixture of two or more miscible liquids for which the difference in boiling points is more than 25 K.
  2. It is used for the separation of different gases from air.
  3. It is used to separate acetone and water from their mixture.
  4. It is based on the principle of repeated cooling and condensation of vapours by providing surface for the vapours.
questions.explanation:
The given experimental setup shows fractional distillation method. This method is used to separate a mixture of two or more miscible liquids for which the difference in boiling points is less than 25 K.
Acetone (b.p. 329 K) and w a te r (b.p. 373 K) are separated from their mixture by simple distillation method, as the difference in boiling points is more than 25 K.

questions.question:
Choose the correct options, and explain the reason for the correct and incorrect options.
Which among the following mixtures show the Tyndall Effect?
A mixture of:

  1. air and dust particles
  2. copper sulfate and water
  3. starch and water
  4. acetone and water
questions.explanation: The Tyndall Effect is shown by colloids (particles large enough to scatter light), not by true solutions.

questions.question: Which of the following mixtures are correctly classified as homogeneous (Hm) and heterogeneous (Ht)? Choose the correct option.

options.option: Copper sulfate solution - Hm, Salt solution - Hm, Milk - Hm, Bronze - Hm

questions.explanation:
Mixtures are classified as homogeneous or heterogeneous based on whether their composition is uniform or non-uniform.

  • Homogeneous mixtures have a uniform composition throughout (e.g., air, salt solution, brass).
  • Heterogeneous mixtures do not have a uniform composition and may show visible different components or dispersed particles (e.g., milk, fog, smoke, muddy water, blood).

questions.question: What is the primary reason some medicines are prepared as emulsions?

options.option: To make them easily palatable and reduce a greasy feeling when dispersed in water.
To concentrate the active ingredients for higher efficacy.
To enhance their direct absorption into the bloodstream.
To increase their shelf life by preventing bacterial growth.

questions.explanation: Medicines are prepared as emulsions to enable dispersion in water, making them easier to consume (palatable) and reducing any greasy sensation.

questions.question: What is the role of emulsifying agents in emulsions?
questions.explanation: Emulsifying agents are present to stabilize emulsions.

questions.question: Which of these is an example of an oil-in-water emulsion?
questions.explanation: Milk is a common example of an oil-in-water emulsion.

questions.question: What kind of colloids are formed when both the dispersed phase and the dispersion medium are liquids?
questions.explanation: When both the dispersed phase and the dispersion medium are liquids, the colloids are called emulsions.

questions.question: Which of the following is true regarding light scattering in different types of mixtures?

options.option: Light scattering occurs only in pure substances.
Light scattering occurs when it passes through a colloid or a suspension.
Light scattering is absent in all mixtures.

questions.explanation: Scattering of light occurs when it passes through a colloid or a suspension, but not when it passes through a transparent solution.

questions.question: What is the component in which the dispersed phase of a colloid is suspended called?
questions.explanation: The component in which the dispersed phase is suspended is known as the dispersion medium.

questions.question: What are the solute-like components in a colloid referred to as?
questions.explanation: The solute-like component or dispersed particles in a colloid constitute the dispersed phase.

questions.question: What is the phenomenon called when light is scattered by particles, making its path visible?
questions.explanation: The scattering of light by particles, leading to a visible light path, is known as the Tyndall effect.

questions.question: What is the typical diameter range for particles in a colloid?
questions.explanation: Colloids have particle sizes typically ranging from 1-1000 nm in diameter.

questions.question: Which of these substances is given as an example of a colloid?
questions.explanation: Milk is an example of a colloid.

questions.question: Which term describes heterogeneous mixtures in which solid particles do not dissolve but remain suspended throughout the bulk of the medium?
questions.explanation: Suspensions are heterogeneous mixtures where solid particles are distributed but do not dissolve, remaining suspended.

questions.question: What is a homogeneous mixture of two or more metals, or a metal and a non-metal, called?
questions.explanation: An alloy is defined as a homogeneous mixture of two or more metals, or a metal and a non-metal.

questions.question: What is the process called when a substance changes directly from a solid state to a vapor state upon heating, without passing through the liquid state?
questions.explanation: Sublimation is the process where a solid changes directly into a vapor without first becoming a liquid.

questions.question: Which of the following describes two liquids that do not mix and form separate layers?
questions.explanation: Immiscible liquids are defined as liquids that do not mix and form separate layers, such as oil and water.

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of crystallization as a separation method for solids?

options.option: To purify solids and separate two solids where one is in small quantity and both are soluble in the same solvent.
To separate a liquid from dissolved solids by boiling.

questions.explanation: Crystallization can be used for the separation of two solids where one is in small quantity and both are soluble in the same solvent, and also for purification of solids.

questions.question: What was 'panga salt' obtained by in ancient Indian coastal communities?
questions.explanation: 'Panga salt was obtained by boiling concentrated sea brines'.

questions.question: Which traditional distillation method is used in Kannauj for making Mitti ka Ittar?
questions.explanation: The Deg-Bhapka traditional distillation method is used in Kannauj for making Mitti ka Ittar.

questions.question: Which of the following is an example of a naturally formed crystal?
questions.explanation: Snowflakes are mentioned as naturally formed crystals when water vapor freezes in the air.

questions.question: What is the process of forming crystals from a saturated solution called in laboratories?
questions.explanation: Crystallization is the specific laboratory process for forming crystals from a saturated solution.

questions.question: What does a 5% glucose solution commonly represent in terms of concentration?

options.option: 5 ml of glucose in 100 g of solution
5 g of glucose in 100 ml of solution
5 g of glucose in 100 g of solution
5 ml of glucose in 100 ml of solution

questions.explanation: A 5% glucose solution typically implies 5 grams of glucose present in 100 milliliters of the solution, which is mass by volume percentage.

questions.question: What is the formula for calculating mass by mass percentage of a solution?

options.option: (Mass of solute / Volume of solution) x 100
(Mass of solvent / Mass of solution) x 100
(Mass of solute / Mass of solution) x 100
(Volume of solute / Volume of solution) x 100

questions.explanation: Mass by mass percentage is calculated as (Mass of solute / Mass of solution) x 100.

questions.question: What is the term for the amount of solute dissolved in a given amount of solvent or solution?
questions.explanation: Concentration refers to the amount of solute dissolved in a given amount of solvent or solution.

questions.question: What is the term for the substance that dissolves the solute in a solution?
questions.explanation: The solvent is the substance that dissolves the solute.

questions.question: What distinguishes a homogeneous mixture from a heterogeneous mixture based on its appearance?

options.option: A homogeneous mixture always has color, while a heterogeneous mixture is colorless.
A homogeneous mixture always contains water, but a heterogeneous mixture does not.
A homogeneous mixture settles quickly, while a heterogeneous mixture never settles.
A homogeneous mixture appears as a single phase, while a heterogeneous mixture shows distinct components or phases.

questions.explanation: A homogeneous mixture has a uniform appearance, appearing as one phase, whereas a heterogeneous mixture has visibly distinct components or regions (phases).

questions.question: Why is a mixture of sugar and water considered homogeneous?

options.option: Because it always remains homogeneous and has a uniform composition throughout.
Because the sugar particles are larger than water molecules.
Because sugar can be easily separated from water.
Because the sugar settles at the bottom over time.

questions.explanation: A sugar and water mixture is homogeneous because its composition is uniform throughout, meaning every part of the mixture has the same properties and sweetness.

questions.question: Which of these is an example of a homogeneous mixture?
questions.explanation: Aerated drinks are cited as an example of a homogeneous mixture (solution) where carbon dioxide is dissolved uniformly in water.

questions.question: Which characteristic is typical of a heterogeneous mixture?
questions.explanation: In a heterogeneous mixture like sand and water, the particles are visible and settle over time, indicating a non-uniform composition.

questions.question: What is another term for a homogeneous mixture?
questions.explanation: A homogeneous mixture is also known as a solution, as exemplified by sugar and water.

questions.question: Which of the following describes a homogeneous mixture?

options.option: Particles are easily visible and settle over time.
It consists of distinct layers that do not mix.
Its components can be readily distinguished individually.

questions.explanation: A homogeneous mixture is characterized by its uniform composition throughout, meaning its properties are the same everywhere.

questions.question: What is fractional distillation used for in petroleum refining?

options.option: To separate components with small boiling point differences.
To remove solid impurities from crude oil.
To mix different petroleum products to create new ones.

questions.explanation: Fractional distillation is the process of separating components of a mixture with relatively small differences (less than 25 °C) in their boiling points, as used in petroleum refining.

questions.question: Which Greek words form the origin of the term 'chromatography' and what do they mean?
questions.explanation: The word 'chromatography' comes from the Greek words chroma, meaning 'colour' and graphein, meaning 'to write'.

questions.question: What is the historical significance of the 'Deg-Bhapka method'?

options.option: It is a modern industrial process for petroleum refining.
It is a technique for separating immiscible liquids.
It is a traditional distillation method for extracting fragrances.

questions.explanation: The 'Deg-Bhapka method' is a traditional distillation method used for extracting fragrances from flowers, as exemplified by Mitti ka Ittar in Kannauj.

questions.question: What is the key difference between evaporation and distillation in terms of solvent recovery?

options.option: Evaporation causes the solvent to disappear, while distillation aims to recover it.
Evaporation recovers the solvent and distillation does not.
Distillation causes the solvent to disappear, while evaporation aims to recover it.
Both methods aim to recover the solvent.

questions.explanation: When allowing a solvent to evaporate, the solvent disappears. Distillation, however, is used when recovery of the solvent is desired.

subtopics.name: Methods of Separation of Homogeneous Mixtures

questions.question: What is distillation primarily used for?

options.option: To separate liquids with at least a 25 °C boiling point difference.
To purify solids based on crystal size.
To create new compounds through chemical reactions.

questions.explanation: Distillation allows the recovery of the solvent or the separation of liquids that differ in boiling point by at least about 25 °C.

questions.question: What is the purpose of adding dilute sulfuric acid during the copper sulfate crystallization experiment?

options.option: To increase the solubility of copper sulfate.
To make pure crystals by preventing unwanted reactions.

questions.explanation: Sulfuric acid helps in making pure crystals by preventing unwanted reactions.

questions.question: What is the principle behind purifying solids through crystallization?

options.option: Differences in boiling points of the components.
Differences in the solubility of a substance at different temperatures.
Differences in the density of the substance at different temperatures.
Differences in the magnetic properties of the components.

questions.explanation: The principle of purification by crystallization is based on the differences in the solubility of a substance at different temperatures.

questions.question: What is the primary characteristic that defines a crystal?

options.option: It is a solid with particles arranged in a regular geometric pattern.
It is a liquid with irregular arrangement of particles.
It is a gas with randomly distributed particles.
It is a solution of dissolved solids and liquids.

questions.explanation: A crystal is defined as a solid made up of particles arranged in a regular geometric pattern.

questions.question: Dilip Mahalanabis developed ORS, which revolutionized rehydration therapy. What characteristic of ORS preparation makes it a critical example of applying solution concentration principles?

options.option: The preparation of ORS is simple and does not require careful measurement.
ORS relies on a specific color to indicate proper mixing.
Correct salt-sugar ratio in fixed water volume.
ORS works regardless of the concentration of its ingredients.

questions.explanation: The efficacy and safety of ORS depend entirely on precise amounts of salt and sugar in a fixed volume of water; deviating from this alters its therapeutic function.

questions.question: When farmers prepare pesticide solutions, why is it crucial to use the 'right amount' of pesticide, as opposed to simply mixing 'some' pesticide with 'some' water?

options.option: It is a legal requirement that all agricultural solutions follow strict mixing ratios regardless of effect.
Mixing 'some' pesticide makes the solution heterogeneous, which is undesirable.
Improper concentrations can lead to ineffectiveness or damage to crops, soil, and the environment.
The precise amount ensures the mixture is visually appealing.

questions.explanation: Precise concentration is vital because insufficient pesticide fails to protect crops, while excessive amounts can severely damage the crops, soil, and the surrounding ecosystem.

questions.question: Why would a laboratory choose to express a solution's concentration using mass by volume percentage (% m/v) rather than mass by mass percentage (% m/m) for a liquid solution?

options.option: Mass by volume percentage is specifically used for homogeneous mixtures.
Measuring mass is inherently more accurate than measuring volume.
Measuring the volume of a liquid is often easier and more practical than weighing it.
Mass by mass percentage is only for solid solutes.

questions.explanation: Laboratories prefer mass by volume percentage because measuring the volume of a liquid is significantly more convenient and practical than weighing it for every preparation.

questions.question: Medical professionals often use 0.9% m/v sodium chloride intravenous infusion. What does this concentration indicate about the solution?

options.option: There are 0.9 milliliters of sodium chloride in 100 milliliters of the solution.
There are 0.9 grams of sodium chloride in 100 milliliters of the solution.
The solution contains 0.9% sodium chloride by weight and 99.1% water by volume.
There are 0.9 grams of sodium chloride in 100 grams of the solution.

questions.explanation: The expression '% m/v' (mass by volume percentage) signifies grams of solute per 100 milliliters of solution. Therefore, 0.9% m/v means 0.9 g of sodium chloride in 100 mL of solution.

questions.question: If you are preparing 150 mL of orange juice with a 15 mL tablespoon of concentrate, and a total of two tablespoons of concentrate are used, what is the % v/v of orange juice concentrate?
questions.explanation:
Total concentrate volume = 2 tablespoons {tex}\times{/tex} 15 mL/tablespoon = 30 mL.
Volume by volume percentage = (30 mL/150 mL) {tex}\times{/tex} 100 = 20% v/v.

questions.question: A liquid pesticide is mixed with water to form 100 mL of a spray. If 1 mL of pesticide was used, what is the volume by volume percentage of pesticide in the spray?
questions.explanation: Volume by volume percentage = (Volume of solute / Volume of solution) × 100 = (1 mL/100 mL) × 100 = 1% v/v.

questions.question: If 5 g of glucose is dissolved in water to make 100 mL of solution, what is its concentration in mass by volume percentage?
questions.explanation: Mass by volume percentage = (Mass of solute / Volume of solution) × 100 = (5 g / 100 mL) × 100 = 5% m/v.

questions.question: A common talcum powder contains 4% m/m zinc oxide. If you have 300 g of this talcum powder, how much zinc oxide is present?
questions.explanation:
4% m/m means 4 g of zinc oxide in every 100 g of talcum powder.
For 300 g: (4 g / 100 g) {tex}\times{/tex}300 g = 12 g.

questions.question: If 10 g of salt is dissolved in 90 g of water, what is the mass by mass percentage of the solution formed?
questions.explanation:
Total mass of solution = 10 g (salt) + 90 g (water) = 100 g.
Mass by mass percentage = (Mass of solute / Mass of solution) × 100 = (10 g / 100 g) × 100 = 10 % m/m.

questions.question: Which concentration expression tells us how many grams of solute are present in 100 grams of the total solution?
questions.explanation: Mass by mass percentage specifically defines the grams of solute per 100 grams of the total solution.

questions.question: What is the general effect of increasing temperature on the solubility of gases dissolved in liquids?
questions.explanation: Unlike solid solutes, the solubility of gases in liquids generally decreases as the temperature increases.

questions.question: How does the solubility of a solid solute in a liquid solvent generally change with increasing temperature?
questions.explanation: For solid solutes in liquid solvents, solubility typically increases as the temperature rises.

questions.question: Which of the following describes a saturated solution?

options.option: A solution that can still dissolve more solute at a given temperature.
A solution where the solute settles at the bottom.
A solution that cannot dissolve any more solute at a given temperature.

questions.explanation: A saturated solution is defined as one that has dissolved the maximum amount of solute possible at a specific temperature.

questions.question: Why is the correct proportion essential when preparing solutions like ORS or pesticide sprays?

options.option: To ensure the solution has a pleasant taste.
To achieve the intended effect and avoid negative consequences.

questions.explanation: The correct proportion is essential because too little may be ineffective (e.g., pesticide) and too much can be harmful (e.g., pesticide, ORS incorrectly prepared).

questions.question: In a solution of sugar and water, which component acts as the solute?
questions.explanation: The solute is the substance that gets dissolved, while the solvent is the substance that dissolves the solute. In sugar and water, sugar is dissolved, making it the solute.

questions.question: What is the primary characteristic that defines a solution?

options.option: It is a compound formed by chemical bonding.

questions.explanation: Solutions are defined as homogeneous mixtures, meaning their composition is uniform throughout.

questions.question: An experiment requires preparing a transparent mixture that will not scatter light. Which of the following ingredients, when mixed with 50 mL of water, would best achieve this requirement?
questions.explanation: Common salt thoroughly dissolved in water forms a homogeneous mixture (solution) that is transparent and would not scatter light (Tyndall effect).

questions.question: Group A prepares a mixture of common salt and water. Group B prepares a mixture of chalk powder and water. Group C prepares a mixture of milk and water. If all three groups shine a laser through their mixtures, which group(s) would exhibit a visible laser beam path, and why?

options.option: All three groups, as laser light is always visible when passing through any mixture.
Groups B and C, because both chalk powder and milk contain particles that scatter light.
Only Group B, because chalk powder is insoluble and forms a suspension.
Only Group A, because salt solutions are transparent.

questions.explanation: Chalk powder in water (Group B) is a suspension, containing particles large enough to scatter light. Milk in water (Group C) forms a colloid, which also scatters light (Tyndall effect), making the laser beam visible. Salt in water (Group A) is a true solution and would not show a visible beam.

questions.question: A scientist observes that the particles in a liquid mixture are easily visible and settle down over time. Upon shining a laser light through it, the path of the beam is clear. Based on these observations, how would the scientist classify this mixture, and what properties support this classification?

options.option: Colloid, because colloids have visible particles that scatter light but do not settle immediately.
Solution, because solutions exhibit particle settling and light scattering.
Homogeneous mixture, because the laser path is clear, indicating uniform composition.
Heterogeneous mixture, because particles are visible, settle, and scatter light (Tyndall effect).

questions.explanation: Visible particles, settling over time, and scattering of light (making the laser beam path clear) are all defining characteristics of a heterogeneous mixture, specifically a suspension or certain colloids where larger particles are present.

questions.question: If Group B adds one spatula of chalk powder to 50 mL of water and stirs it, and then conducts the laser light experiment, what observation would be expected from the side, perpendicular to the laser beam?

options.option: The path of the laser beam will be clearly visible due to scattering by chalk particles.
The mixture will absorb all the laser light, making it invisible.
The laser light will reflect off the surface of the mixture.
The laser light will pass through undeflected, with no visible beam.

questions.explanation: Chalk powder in water forms a suspension or a heterogeneous mixture where particles are large enough to scatter light, making the beam path visible (Tyndall effect).

questions.question: A student mixes a spoonful of common salt into 50 mL of water and stirs it well. Based on the provided examples, how would this mixture be classified?
questions.explanation: The text states that a mixture of sugar and water (similar to salt and water) has a uniform composition throughout and is called a homogeneous mixture or solution.

subtopics.name: How Can We Separate the Components of Heterogeneous Mixtures?

questions.question: What safety precaution must be taken when conducting an experiment involving a laser pointer?

options.option: Use the laser pointer in a brightly lit room.
Do not look directly into the laser beam.
Observe the laser beam directly to see its path.
Ensure the light is directed at a reflective surface.

questions.explanation: The safety instruction explicitly states, 'Do not look directly into the laser beam. It can cause irreversible eye damage.'

questions.question: Which of the following is an example of a heterogeneous mixture?
questions.explanation: A stirred mixture of sand and water is heterogeneous because the sand particles are visible and settle over time.

questions.question: What is a characteristic of a solution?

options.option: Its components can be seen settling at the bottom.

questions.explanation: A solution is a homogeneous mixture, meaning its composition is uniform throughout, like sugar dissolved in water.

questions.question: A student observes that when a laser beam is passed through liquid X, the path of light is not visible. However, when the same laser beam is passed through liquid Y, the path of light is clearly visible, even though liquid Y appears to be a homogeneous mixture. Based on this observation and the principles of the Tyndall effect, what can be inferred about liquid X and liquid Y?

options.option: Liquid X is a solution, and liquid Y is a colloid.
Liquid X is a colloid, and liquid Y is a solution.
Both liquid X and liquid Y are suspensions.
Both liquid X and liquid Y are colloids.

questions.explanation: The path of light is not visible in a solution (like liquid X), but it is visible in a colloid (like liquid Y) due to scattering, even if the colloid appears homogeneous.

questions.question: What is the common application of emulsions in medicine?

options.option: To make medicines easily palatable and reduce greasy feeling.
To increase the viscosity of the medicine.
To separate active ingredients from the inactive ones.
To enhance the medicine's shelf life without refrigeration.

questions.explanation: Some medicines are prepared as emulsions to disperse them in water and make them easily palatable. This also reduces the greasy feeling of the liquid medicine.

questions.question: Which of the following types of mixtures is capable of exhibiting the Tyndall effect by scattering an incident beam of light?
questions.explanation: These mixtures contain dispersed particles significantly larger than molecules. These particles effectively scatter incident light, making the beam's path visible-a phenomenon known as the Tyndall effect.

questions.question: How does the Tyndall effect manifest when a fine beam of light enters a dark room through a small hole?

options.option: The light beam passes straight through without any change.
The temperature of the room increases significantly.
Light gets scattered by dust and smoke particles, making the beam visible.

questions.explanation: This effect can also be observed when a fine beam of light enters a dark room through a small hole. This happens because light gets scattered by the dust and smoke particles in the air.

questions.question: What are the two main components of a colloid?
questions.explanation: The components of a colloid are called the dispersed phase and the dispersion medium.

questions.question: What physical properties of colloidal particles allow for the visual manifestation of a light beam's path?

options.option: The path of light is visible due to absorption.
The path of light is only visible in concentrated solutions.
The path of light is not visible.
The path of light is always clearly visible.

questions.explanation: The path of the light beam was not visible in a solution, indicating no scattering of light.

questions.question: Which physicist first identified the process where light is scattered by colloidal particles?
questions.explanation: The scattering of light by particles is known as the Tyndall effect, and it was first explained by the scientist John Tyndall.

questions.question: If a heterogeneous mixture of solid particles in a liquid is left undisturbed, and the particles settle at the bottom, what is the most likely initial method to separate a significant portion of the liquid from the settled solids, considering the efficiency?

options.option: Decantation, carefully pouring off the liquid above the settled solids.
Using a magnet to attract the solid particles.
Heating the mixture to evaporate the liquid entirely.

questions.explanation: Once particles settle due to gravity, decantation-carefully pouring off the clear liquid—is the most efficient initial method to separate a significant portion of the liquid from the settled solids without requiring complex equipment.

questions.question: A chef wants to make cheese from milk. Which substance would act as a coagulant in this process, mimicking the principle described for muddy water?
questions.explanation: Formation of cheese involves coagulation where acid (lemon juice or vinegar) is used as a coagulant, causing milk proteins to clump.

questions.question: If you have a container of muddy water that remains cloudy even after filtration, what additional technique(s) might be employed to clarify the water?
questions.explanation: If muddy water remains cloudy after filtration, techniques like centrifugation and/or coagulation are used to clean it.

questions.question: A laboratory technician needs to separate blood components like red blood cells and plasma. Which method would be most appropriate?
questions.explanation: Centrifugation is widely used in laboratories to separate blood components.

questions.question: A student accidentally mixes iron filings, sulfur, and sand. Which initial separation technique would be most effective for separating the iron filings?
questions.explanation: Iron is magnetic, so a magnet can be used to separate it from non-magnetic substances like sulfur and sand based on their differing magnetic properties.

questions.question: What is the primary characteristic that distinguishes a suspension from a solution?

options.option: Particles in a suspension are visible to the naked eye, while solute particles in a solution are not.
Solutions always involve chemical reactions, while suspensions do not.
A suspension is always colored, while a solution is clear.
Suspensions are always formed by mixing solids and liquids, solutions only liquids.

questions.explanation: The key difference is the particle size; particles in a suspension are large enough to be seen, unlike those in a solution.

questions.question: Why are most mixtures of gases considered homogeneous?

options.option: Gas particles move freely and mix uniformly.
Gases always react to form new compounds.
Gas particles are heavy and settle quickly.

questions.explanation: Gas particles' ability to move freely and mix uniformly results in homogeneous gas mixtures.

questions.question: What happens to the lower layer in a separating funnel when immiscible liquids like mustard oil and water are separated?

options.option: It is collected first by opening the stopcock slowly.

questions.explanation: The procedure for separating immiscible liquids using a separating funnel states that the lower layer is collected first by opening the stopcock.

questions.question: Which substance is used as a coagulant in water purification to clump fine suspended particles?
questions.explanation: Alum (fitkari) is described as a white crystalline chemical substance used in water purification that causes fine suspended particles to clump together.

questions.question: The process where vapours condense back into a solid without becoming a liquid is known as:
questions.explanation: Deposition is the phase transition from gas to solid without passing through the liquid phase.

questions.question: What is an alloy?

options.option: A mixture of metals that can be separated by physical methods at room temperature.
A heterogeneous mixture of a metal and a non-metal.
A homogeneous mixture of metals or metal and non-metal.
A pure metallic element used in manufacturing.

questions.explanation: An alloy is a homogeneous mixture formed by melting two or more metals, or a metal and a non-metal, and then cooling the mixture.

questions.question: What principle is used in centrifugation to separate components of a mixture?
questions.explanation: Centrifugation uses centrifugal force to separate components, with heavier particles being forced to the outer part of the spinning tube.

questions.question: Which of the following is an example of a suspension?
