questions.question: When a stone of mass 'm' falls through a vertical height 'd', the decrease in the gravitational energy is:
questions.explanation:
Mass of the stone = m
Vertical distance, i.e. height h = d
Potential energy = mgh = mgd

questions.question: A 1 kg mass has a kinetic energy of 1 joule when its speed is:
questions.explanation:
K.E = {tex}\frac12{/tex} mv2
We'll replace K.E i.e kinectic energy by 1 J and mass (m) by 1
1 = {tex}\frac12{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 1 {tex}\times{/tex} v2
2 = v2
{tex}\sqrt2{/tex} = v
 v = 1.4 ms-1

questions.question: An iron sphere of mass 30 kg has the same diameter as an aluminum sphere whose mass is 10.5 kg. The spheres are dropped simultaneously from a cliff. When they are 10 m from the ground, they have the same.
questions.explanation: When both spheres are dropped simultaneously from a cliff, they have the same acceleration. Because during free-fall acceleration of the body becomes equals to (g= 9.8m/s2)  and 'g' depends on the mass of earth and radius of the earth.

questions.question: When a body is whirled in a circle the work done by the centripetal force is:
questions.explanation: When a body is whirled in a circle the work done by the centripetal force is zero Because work is done is defined as the distance an object is moved in the direction of the force applied and since the centripetal force acts perpendicular to the velocity (direction of motion) the work done is 0. Work done by the centripetal force is zero only when the radial position does not change.

questions.question: An object of mass 1 kg has a potential energy of 1 joule relative to the ground when it is at a height of:
questions.explanation:
P.E. = mgh
m= 1 kg P.E. = 1J g = 9.8m/s2
1 = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 9.8 {tex}\times{/tex} h
h = {tex}1 \over 9.8{/tex} = 0.102 m

questions.question: An object of mass 5 kg falls from a height of 5 m above the ground. The loss of potential energy of the mass is:
questions.explanation:
P.E. = mgh
m = 5kg h = 5m  g = 9.8m/s2
P.E. = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 9.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 5
P.E. = 25 {tex}\times{/tex} 9.8
P.E. = 245 J

questions.question: A boy throws a ball vertically upwards. As the ball rises its total energy (neglect friction):
questions.explanation: The kinetic energy changes into the potential energy when stone rises up. The potential energy then changes back to the kinetic energy, when a stone falls down. A boy throws a ball vertically upwards. As the ball rises its total energy remains the same.

questions.question: When a force retards the motion of a body the work done is:
questions.explanation:
The nature of work done in case of retarding motion is negative.
Suppose a force F brings a body moving with velocity v to rest (retards), then work done = change in KE = KEf = KEi
W = 0 - {tex}\frac12{/tex} mu2 = -{tex}\frac12{/tex}mu2

questions.question: ________ is the SI unit of work.
questions.explanation:
The SI unit of work is joule and is denoted as ‘J’, which is named after an English physicist James Prescott Joule.
The 1 joule of work done is equal to 1N {tex}\times{/tex} 1 m. or, 1 joule = Nm.

questions.question: A body at rest may have:
questions.explanation: Potential energy is the energy of a body due to its height, hence any object that is not at the center of the earth is under a gravitational pull and has an energy that can enable it to travel if unhindered towards the center. Hence all the objects on earth that are at rest, have gravitational potential energy.

questions.question: The work done by a force on a body will be positive if the:

options.option: body moves perpendicular to the direction of motion
body moves opposite to the direction of the applied force
the body moves along the direction of the applied force

questions.explanation: The work done is positive if the applied force is in the same direction as the direction of motion.

questions.question: The cgs. unit of work is:
questions.explanation: The unit of work in the cgs system is dyne cm or erg.

questions.question: One joule work is said to be done when:

options.option: a force of 1 N displaces a body by 1 cm
a force of 1 N displaces a body by 1 m
a force of 1 dyne displaces a body by 1 cm
a force of 1 dyne displaces a body by 1 m

questions.explanation:
1 joule(J) is the amount of work done when a force of 1 Newton(N) displaces an object 1 meter(m).
1J = 1N {tex}\times{/tex} 1m or 1J = 1Nm.

questions.question: The SI unit of energy is ________.
questions.explanation: SI unit of energy is the same as of work. The SI unit of energy is joule and is denoted by ‘J’.

questions.question: The potential energy of a body is by virtue of its ________.
questions.explanation: The energy possessed by an object because of its position is called potential energy.

questions.question: A mass 'm' falls from a height 'h'. At any point on its path the total energy is:
questions.explanation: When a stone is dropped from a height H, it has Potential Energy (PE) equal to mgh and Kinetic Energy (KKE) is 0. When it falls, according. to the law of conservation of energy, its Total Energy (PE + KE) will always remain constant i.e mgh.

questions.question: A mass m is moving in a circle of radius 1 m with a uniform speed of 5 m/s. The work done when its radius turns by 60o is:
questions.explanation: The object moves in a circular path. In this case, the centripetal force acts on the object at right angles to the direction of motion of an object. So, the work done by the object in a circular path is zero.

questions.question: A man holds and displaces a 20 kg mass horizontally in his stretched hand:

options.option: He does 200 joules of work per metre
He does zero work against the force of gravity
The work done against gravity cannot be estimated but non-zero.
The work done against gravity is equal to the muscular energy

questions.explanation: He does zero work against the force of gravity because no displacement is done.

questions.question: Two cars A and B approaching each other with a momentum of 50 kg m/s and 25 kg m/s respectively, stick to each other after the collision. The momentum after the collision is:
questions.explanation: The momentum after the collision is in the direction of A because of less momentum.

questions.question: In a collision:

options.option: the momentum of the system is constant

questions.explanation: The total momentum of the system just before the collision is the same as the total momentum just after the collision. So, the momentum of the system is constant.

questions.question: In the explosion of a cracker/bomb:
questions.explanation: When a cracker bursts, its pieces move out in all directions. We explain it with the help of the law of conservation of momentum. In this case, initially, the momentum of the cracker is zero. Therefore, when it bursts, its pieces move out in different directions with some specific momentum. However, if we add up the momentum of these various pieces, the sum of the momenta will be zero.

questions.question: Which of the following energy cannot be completely converted into other forms due to the transmission system?
questions.explanation: All forms of energy can be completely converted to heat, but heat cannot be completely converted into work or other forms of energy because of the entropy heat contains.

questions.question: In an oscillating simple pendulum:

options.option: Work is done by the force of gravity

questions.explanation: The pendulum swings down and the potential energy is converted to kinetic energy.  At the bottom of the swing, the potential energy is all gone (this is the lowest it goes) and it is all kinetic energy.  This is also the fastest that it goes.

questions.question: A microphone converts sound energy to:
questions.explanation: Microphones are loudspeakers in reverse, it converts sound (mechanical) energy to electrical energy.

questions.question: When a body falls freely towards the earth, then its total energy
questions.explanation: Since, total energy of the system is always conserved, so when a body falls freely towards the earth, then its total energy remains constant i.e., the sum of the potential energy and kinetic energy of the body would be same at all points.

questions.question: A car is accelerated on a leveled road and attains a velocity 4 times of its initial velocity. In this process the potential energy of the car

options.option: becomes twice to that of initial

questions.explanation: Potential energy will not change as the road is leveled and the height of the body remains the same, although its speed increases.

questions.question: In case of negative work the angle between the force and displacement is:
questions.explanation: When force is applied in opposite direction of displacement, the work done is considered as negative and the angle between the force and displacement is 180o.

questions.question: A girl is carrying a school bag of 3 kg mass on her back and moves 200 m on a leveled road. The work done against the gravitational force will be (g = 10m/s2)
questions.explanation: The work done is zero because between the road and girl the angle made is 90 so the value of cos 90 is zero so when we use formula w = f s cos90 the answer will zero.

questions.question: Which one of the following is not the unit of energy?
questions.explanation: Since energy is capacity of doing work, therefore, the SI unit of energy is same as of work.

questions.question: A tank contains water of mass 'm' to a height 'h'. The potential energy associated is

options.option: Depends on the radius of the tank

questions.explanation:
If you consider gravitational field to be constant till height 'h’ then we know that Center of the mass of tank lies {tex}\frac h2{/tex}height above ground level.
Now potential energy will be = m{tex} \times{/tex}g {tex} \times{/tex} ({tex}\frac{h}{2}{/tex}) .

questions.question: The work done on an object does not depend upon the
questions.explanation:
We know that, W = F.d cos θ
Here, F = force applied on the object, d = displacement and θ is angle between force and displacement. So, the work done on an object does not depend upon the initial velocity of the object.

questions.question: Water stored in a dam possess
questions.explanation: Water stored in a dam possesses potential energy. And once the water is falling it has kinetic energy.

questions.question: A body is falling from a height h. After it has fallen a height {tex}\frac h2{/tex} . it will possess
questions.explanation:
As we know that when a body is at height h, it has
total energy = KE + PE
and at height ‘h’, velocity of body is zero
SO, KE = 0 and PE = mgh
Now, at height {tex}\frac h2{/tex}
its PE = mg {tex}\frac h2{/tex} = {tex}\;\frac {mgh}2{/tex} .......... (i)
and KE = {tex}\frac 12{/tex} mv2
Where, velocity can be determined by equation of motion
v2 = u2 + 2gh
So, v2 = {tex}\;\frac {2gh}2{/tex} ⇒ v ={tex}\sqrt{gh}{/tex}
So, KE = {tex}\frac 12{/tex}m{tex}(\sqrt{gh)}^2{/tex} ............ (ii)
So, from equation (i) and (ii) body at ({tex}\frac h2{/tex}) posses half potential energy and half kinetic energy.

questions.question: If 1 newton of force displaces a body by 1 m, the work done is:
questions.explanation:
1 joule(J) is the amount of work done when a force of 1 Newton(N) displaces an object 1 meter(m).
1J = 1N {tex}\times{/tex} 1m or 1J = 1Nm.

questions.question: On tripling the speed of motion of a body, the change in K.E. is:
questions.explanation:
K.E. is proportional to V2.
So, when speed is tripled, KE. is nine-time.

questions.question: Which of the following statements is true for the pressure exerted by a solid body?

options.option: It depends upon the area of contact for the same magnitude of the force:
It does not depends upon the area of contact for the  magnitude of force
It does not depend upon the area of contact for the same magnitude of force
Greater the area over which the force acts, greater is the pressure

questions.explanation: {tex}P\;{/tex} = {tex}\;\frac FA{/tex} , For the same force {tex}P\;\propto\;\frac1A{/tex}

questions.question: To observe and compare the pressure exerted by solid iron cuboid, a student marked all the faces of cuboid from 1 to 6. After doing the experiment with all faces of cuboid, he observed that

options.option: opposite parallel faces of cuboid exerted unequal pressure on the sand
adjacent faces of cuboid exerted the same pressure
all the faces of cuboid exerted same pressure on the sand
opposite parallel faces of the cuboid exerted some pressure on the sand

questions.explanation: Since force per unit area remains the same, so the same conclusion is drawn by both the students.

questions.question: Two discs of the same radii and the same mass are resting over one another co-axially on the sand. If one disc is removed gently, then the pressure exerted by the single-disc will be:
questions.explanation: On removing the disc, weight reduces to half.

questions.question: A cylinder and a cone having radius r1 and r2 respectively (r1 > r2) are having the same mass. Then pressure exerted by the cylinder is:
questions.explanation:
{tex}P\;\propto\;\frac1A{/tex}
The pressure is inversely proportional to the area and ACylinder > ACone

questions.question: In doing the experiment, a student puts the solid iron cuboid in an upright position on loose sand. After that, he has laid it. In these two cases:

options.option: thrust is the same and its effects are also the same
effects of the thrust are same but different thrusts are there
the effect of thrust on sand is smaller while in an upright position than lying.
thrust is same but effects are different

questions.explanation: As the contact area changes, the effect of thrust changes.

questions.question: A solid iron cuboid is kept on a tabletop. The mass of cuboid is 6 kg and its dimensions are 30 cm × 15 cm× 10 cm. Force and pressure exerted by the cuboid on the tabletop if it made to lie on the tabletop with its side dimensions 30m {tex}\times{/tex} 15 cm is:
questions.explanation:
Thrust = Force× Weight of cuboid = 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 9.8 = 58.8N
and pressure exerted = {tex}\;\frac{Thrust}{Base\;area}{/tex} = {tex}\;\frac{58.8}{0.30\;\times0.15}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{58.8}{0.045}{/tex} = 1306.66N.

questions.question: A total of 4900 J was spent in lifting a mass of 50 kg. the mass was raised to a height of:
questions.explanation:
P.E. = mgh
m= 50 kg P.E. = 4900J g = 9.8m/s2
4900 = 50 {tex}\times{/tex} 9.8 {tex}\times{/tex} h
4900 = 490 {tex}\times{/tex} h
h = {tex}4900 \over 490{/tex} = 10 m

questions.question: To observe and compare the pressure exerted by solid iron cuboid on the sand, a student takes a cuboid of 5 kg and has a dimension of its base 30 cm ×15 cm and measures its pressure, then he mounts the cuboid on a cylinder having a radius of base 7 cm and weight equal to 1 kg. The pressure of the whole set-up on the sand is:
questions.explanation:
P1 = {tex}\;\frac{F_1}{A_1}{/tex} {tex}=\;\frac{M_1g}{A_1}{/tex}{tex}=\frac{5\times\;9.8}{0.30\times0.15}{/tex} =1.09 {tex}\times{/tex} 103N/m2
P2 ={tex}\;\frac{F_2}{A_2}{/tex} = {tex}\;\frac{(M_1\;+\;M_2)g}{\mathrm{{tex}\pi{/tex}r}^2}{/tex} ={tex}\frac{(5\;+1)\times\;9.8}{0.0154}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{58.8}{0.0154}\;{/tex} = 3.82 {tex}\times{/tex} 103N/m2
{tex}\;\frac{P2}{P_1}{/tex}= {tex}\;\frac{3.82\;\times10^3}{1.09\;\times10^3}{/tex} = 3.5

questions.question: Two masses m and 2m are dropped from a certain height 'h'. Then on reaching the ground,

options.option: K.E. of the lighter is 4 times the K.E. of the heavier
K.E. of the heavier is more than that of the lighter.
K.E. of the heavier is 4 times the K.E. of the lighter

questions.explanation:
P.E. of object1 = mgh
P.E. of object 2 = 2mgh
On reaching the ground the P.E. of both the objects gets completely transformed into K.E.
Therefore K.E. of object 2 > K.E. of object 1.

questions.question: Calculate the potential energy of a person having 60 kg mass on the summit of Mt. Everest. Height of Mt. Everest is 8848 m from sea level. (g = 9.8 m/s2).
questions.explanation:
Mass m = 60 kg
height h = 8848 m
g = 9.8 m/s2
Potential energy U = mgh= (60 {tex}\times{/tex} 9.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 8848) = 52,02,624 J

questions.question: A ball dropped from a height of 10m. if the energy of the ball reduces by 40% after striking the ground, how much high can the bounce back? (g = 10m/s2)
questions.explanation:
Energy = mgh =  m {tex}\times{/tex} 10 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 = 100m Joule.
Energy is reduced by 40% then the remaining energy is 60m Joule.
Therefore 60 m = m {tex}\times{/tex} 10 {tex}\times{/tex} h`
or h` = 6 m

questions.question: A women draws water from a well using a fixed pulley. The mass of the bucket and water together is 6 kg. The force applied by the women is 70 N. The mechanical advantage is
questions.explanation:
The mechanical advantage of a fixed pulley
{tex}=\frac{\operatorname{Load}(L)}{\text { Effort }(E)}{/tex}
Effort (force applied), E = 70 N
Weight of the bucket = mg
= 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 9.8 = 60 N
Hence, mechanical advantage = {tex}\frac{60}{70}{/tex}
Hence, mechanical advantage = 0.86

questions.question: A boy uses a single movable pulley to lift a load of 50 kgf to some height. Another boy uses a single fixed pulley to lift the same load to same height. The ratio of efforts applied by them is
questions.explanation:
Mechanical advantage (MA) given as,
MA = {tex}\frac{\operatorname{Load}(L)}{\text { Effort }(E)}{/tex}
Hence, MA and efforts are inversely proportional to each other.
The MA of single fixed pulley = 1
For single movable pulley, MA = 2
Load on both the pulleys = 50 kgf
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Effort for single fixed pulley
{tex}=\frac{\text { Load }}{M A}=\frac{50}{1}{/tex} = 50 kgf ...(i)
Effort for single movable pulley = {tex}\frac{\text { Load }}{\mathrm{MA}}=\frac{50}{2}{/tex}
= 2.5 kgf ...(ii)
On dividing Eq. (i) by Eq. (ii), we get
{tex}\frac{\text { Effort single fixed pulley }}{\text { Effort single movable pulley }}=\frac{50}{25}=\frac{2}{1}{/tex}
Hence, {tex}\frac{\text { effort single movable pulley }}{\text { effort single fixed pulley }}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex}

questions.question: A block and tackle system has VR = 5. Rahul exerts a pull of 150 kgf. What is the maximum load he can raise with this pulley system if its efficiency = 75%?
questions.explanation:
As, velocity ratio = {tex}\frac{\text { distance travelled by effort }}{\text { distance travelled by load }}{/tex}
Efficiency = {tex}\frac{M A}{V R}=\frac{\text { Mechanical advantage }}{\text { Velocity ratio }}{/tex}
Given, VR = 5, efficiency = 75%
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{75}{100}=\frac{M A}{5}{/tex}
MA = 5 {tex}\times \frac{75}{100}{/tex}
But, MA = {tex}\frac{\text { load }}{\text { effort }}=\frac{L}{150 \mathrm{kgf}}{/tex}
L = 5 {tex}\times \frac{75}{100}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 150 = {tex}\frac{1125}{2}{/tex} = 562.5

questions.question: A pulley system has a VR = 5. If the efficiency of the system is 80%, find its MA.
questions.explanation:
Given, VR = 5, efficiency = 80%
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{\mathrm{MA}}{\mathrm{VR}}{/tex} = Efficiency
80 = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{MA}}{5}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} MA = {tex}\frac{80}{100}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 5 = 4

questions.question: A pulley system has MA = 4. If a load of 10 kgf is pulley by a distance of 2 m in 10 s, calculate the power developed by the effort.
questions.explanation:
Given, MA = 4, load = 10 kgf = 10 {tex}\times{/tex} 9.8 N
Distance = 2 m, time = 10 s
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Power = {tex}\frac{\text { Work done }}{\text { Time taken }}{/tex}
MA = 4 = {tex}\frac{\text { Load }}{\text { Effort }}=\frac{10 \times 9.8}{E}{/tex}
Effort = {tex}\frac{10 \times 9.8}{4}{/tex}
Now, power = {tex}\frac{\text { Work }}{\text { Time }}=\frac{F \times s}{t}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{10 \times 9.8}{4} \times \frac{2}{10}=\frac{49}{10}{/tex} = 4.9 W

questions.question: A fulcrum divides a crow-bar in the ratio 3 : 1. What weight will be lifted if an effort of 100 N is applied at the end of its longer arm?
questions.explanation:
As, clockwise moment = anti-clockwise moment
Load {tex}\times{/tex} Load arm = Effort {tex}\times{/tex} Effort arm
Let load arm = x
As given, effort = 100 N, effort arm = 3x
Hence, 3x {tex}\times{/tex} 100 = x {tex}\times{/tex} load
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Load = {tex}\frac{3 x \times 100}{x}{/tex} = 300 N

questions.question: The MA of sugar tongs is
questions.explanation:
{tex}\therefore{/tex} MA = {tex}\frac{\text { Load }}{\text { Effort }}{/tex}
In case of sugar tongs, its a 3rd class lever, effort lies between fulcrum arm and load, hence MA < 1, effort is greater than load
and VR = {tex}\frac{d E}{d L}{/tex}, dE < dL
Hence, MA < 1

questions.question: A machine is driven by a 100 kg mass that falls 8 m on 4 seconds. It lifts a load of mass 500 kg. If the efficiency of the machine is 75%, what is the power output?
questions.explanation:
As we know, power = {tex}\frac{\text { work done }}{\text { time taken }}=\frac{F \times s}{t}{/tex}
Given, m = 100 kg, g = 10 m/s2, t = 4 s, h = 8 m
Hence, power {tex}=\frac{m g h}{t}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{100 \times 10 \times 8}{4}{/tex} = 2000 W

questions.question: The MA of a machine is 5 and its efficiency is 80%. It is used to lift a load of 200 kgf to a height of 20 m. What is the effort required?
questions.explanation:
Given, efficiency = 80%
Load = 200 kgf = 200 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 N
Mechanical advantage = 5
{tex}\therefore{/tex} MA = {tex}\frac{\text { Load }}{\text { Effort }}{/tex}
5 = {tex}\frac{200 \times 10}{E}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} E = {tex}\frac{200 \times 10}{5}{/tex}
= 400 N = 40 kgf

questions.question: A body of mass 2 kg is thrown up vertically with a kinetic energy of 490 J. If the acceleration due to gravity is 9.8 m s-2, the height at which the kinetic energy of the body becomes half of the original value is
questions.explanation:
We know, if a body is thrown up vertically its KE goes on decreasing and PE goes on increasing. There is conversion of KE into PE. When kinetic energy becomes half of original value, the other half is converted into potential energy.
Therefore, {tex}\frac{490}{2}=2 \times 9.8 \times h{/tex}
or h = {tex}\frac{490}{2 \times 2 \times 9.8}{/tex} or h = 12.5
Thus, the required height is 12.5 m.

questions.question:
Statement 1: If a light body and a heavy body possess the same momentum, the lighter body will possess more kinetic energy.
Statement 2: The kinetic energy of a body varies as the square of its velocity.

options.option: Statement 1 is true but statement 2 is false.
Both statements 1 and 2 are true and statement 2 is the correct explanation of statement 1.
Both statements 1 and 2 are true but statement 2 is not the correct explanation of statement 1.

questions.explanation:
Relation between momentum and KE (EK) is
{tex}p=\sqrt{2 m E_k}{/tex}
If mass of body decreases, then KE should increase accordingly to maintain constant momentum.
Also, {tex}E_K=\frac{1}{2} m v^2{/tex} or {tex}E_K \propto v^2{/tex}

questions.question:
Statement 1: A man carrying a bucket of water and walking on a rough level road with a uniform velocity does no work while carrying the bucket.
Statement 2: The work done on a body by a force (F) in giving it a displacement (S) is defined as W{tex}=\vec{F} \cdot \vec{S}=F S \cos \theta, {/tex} where {tex}\theta{/tex} is the angle between vectors {tex}\vec{F} {/tex} and {tex}\vec{S}{/tex}.

options.option: Both statements 1 and 2 are true and statement 2 is the correct explanation of statement 1.
Statement 1 is true but statement 2 is false.
Both statements 1 and 2 are true but statement 2 is not the correct explanation of statement 1.

questions.explanation:
While carrying a bucket, angle between vectors F and S becomes 90°.
So, {tex}W=\vec{F} \cdot \vec{S}=F S \cos \theta{/tex} = FS cos 90o = 0
Hence, work done is zero.

questions.question:
Match the columns and select the correct option from the codes given here.

Column I Column II
(a) Work done by all the forces (i) Change in potential energy
(b) Work done by conservative forces (ii) Change in kinetic energy
(c) Work done by external forces (iii) Change in mechanical energy
(d) Work done in uniform velocity (iv) Zero
A truck and a car having equal kinetic energies are stopped by applying equal retarding force. If S1 and S2 are the distances covered by truck and car respectively, before stopping, then
questions.explanation:
Let m1, m2 be the masses and u1, u2 be the initial velocities of the truck and car respectively.
As KE of both are equal.
So, {tex}\frac{1}{2} m_1 u_1^2=\frac{1}{2} m_2 u_2^2 {/tex} or {tex}\frac{m_1}{m_2}=\frac{u_2^2}{u_1^2}{/tex}
As equal retarding force is applied on both then
{tex}0=u_1^2-\frac{2 f S_1}{m_1} \Rightarrow S_1=\frac{m_1 u_1^2}{2 f}{/tex}
and {tex}0=u_2^2-\frac{2 f S_2}{m_2} \Rightarrow S_2=\frac{m_2 u_2^2}{2 f}{/tex}
So, {tex}\frac{S_1}{S_2}=\frac{m_1}{m_2} \frac{u_1^2}{u_2^2} {/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{S_1}{S_2}=\frac{1}{1} {/tex}  or S1 = S2

questions.question: A box of 5 kg mass moves up a ramp from point A to point Bat a constant speed. Given that the surfaces are smooth (frictionless). What is the gain in the potential energy by the box? (g = 10 m/s2)
questions.explanation:
Gravitational potential energy
E = mgh = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 {tex}\times{/tex} 8 = 400 J

questions.question: A graph of the total energy of a freely falling body from a height is
questions.explanation: Total energy remains constant.

questions.question:
Statement 1: Potential energy of a stretched or compressed spring is directly proportional to square of extension or compression.
Statement 2: Graph between potential energy of a spring versus the extension or compression of the spring is a straight line.

options.option: Both statement 1 and statement 2 are true but statement 2 is not the correct explanation of statement 1.
Both statement 1 and statement 2 are true and statement 2 is the correct explanation of statement 1.
Statement 1 is true but statement 2 is false

questions.explanation:
Potential energy (U){tex}=\frac{1}{2} k x^2 {/tex} i.e. {tex} U \propto x^2{/tex}
This is the equation of parabola, so graph between U and x is a parabola, not straight line.

questions.question:
Potential energy of an object at a height h is Ep = mgh. This equation is true only if

  1. The acceleration due to gravity is constant.
  2. The potential energy is path dependent.
questions.explanation: Only (i) is true.

questions.question: A skier of mass 50 kg stands at point P at the top of the ski jump and moves from P to Q and takes off his jump at Q as shown in figure. If 50% of the change in the gravitational potential energy of the skier between points P and Q becomes the kinetic energy at Q, then the speed at which the skier arrives at Q is
questions.explanation:
Change in gravitational potential energy of the skier, EP = mgh = 50 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 {tex}\times{/tex} [60 - 20]= 20000 J = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 104 J
Kinetic energy, EK = 50% of EP {tex}=\frac{1}{2} \times 2 \times 10^4 \mathrm{~J}=1 \times 10^4 \mathrm{~J}{/tex}
{tex}E_K=\frac{1}{2} m v^2 \Rightarrow v=\sqrt{\frac{2 E_K}{m}}{/tex}{tex}=\sqrt{\frac{2 \times 1 \times 10^4}{50}}=20 \mathrm{~m} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}{/tex}

questions.question: A mass of M kg is suspended by a weightless string. The horizontal force that is required to displace it until the string making an angle of 45° with the initial vertical direction is
questions.explanation:
Work done in displacement is equal to gain in potential energy.

Work done ={tex}F \times I \sin 45^{\circ}=\frac{F I}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex}
Gain in potential energy
= Mg(l - lcos45°)
{tex}=M g I\left(1-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right){/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{F I}{\sqrt{2}}=\frac{M g I(\sqrt{2}-1)}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex}
or {tex}F=M g(\sqrt{2}-1){/tex}

questions.question: A body moves under a variable force. For the body a plot of velocity versus time is shown in figure. The correct statement is

options.option: In moving from B to C, work done by the force on the body is positive
In moving from A to B, the body does work on the system
In moving from O to A, work done by the force on the body is negative
In moving from C to D, work done by the force on the body is positive

questions.explanation: In moving from C to D, slope of graph is negative which means acceleration (force) is negative and displacement is also negative as the area under curve CD lies below the time axis. Hence, work done by the force on the body is positive.

questions.question: Two inclined frictionless tracks, one gradual and the other steep meet at point A from where two stones (I and II) are allowed to slide down from rest, one on each track as shown in figure. Which of the following statements is correct?

options.option: Both the stones reach the bottom with the same speed and stone I reaches the bottom earlier than stone II.
Both the stones reach the bottom with the same speed and stone II reaches the bottom earlier than stone I.
Both the stones reach the bottom at the same time but not with the same speed.
Both the stones reach the bottom at different times and with different speeds.

questions.explanation:
AB and AC are two smooth planes inclined to the horizontal at different angles.
As height of both the planes are same, therefore, both the stones will reach the bottom with same speed.
{tex}\because v=\sqrt{2 g h}{/tex} {tex}\therefore {/tex} v1 = v2

From figure, acceleration, {tex}a_1=g \sin \theta_1{/tex}
acceleration, a2 = g sin{tex}\theta_2{/tex}
As {tex}\theta_2{/tex} > {tex}\theta_1{/tex}, so a2 > a1
Using, v = u + at
v1 = u1 + a1t1 ; v1 = a1t1
t1 = {tex}\frac{v_1}{a_1}{/tex}
Similarly, t2 = {tex}\frac{v_2}{a_2} {/tex} {tex}\because{/tex} a2 > a1 So, t2 < t1
Hence, stone II takes less time to reach the bottom.

questions.question:
The diagram shows an object released from point X and travelling on a frictionless track. If a heavier object is taken in consideration then

  1. It will move with a greater speed at Y.
  2. The speed of the object at M is 10 m s-1.
questions.explanation:
The speed of the object is affected by the height from which it is released.
{tex}m g h=\frac{1}{2} m v^2 {/tex} or {tex}v=\sqrt{2 g h} {/tex} or {tex}v \propto \sqrt{h}{/tex}
So, heavier object will not move with a greater speed at Y.
Speed of the object at M:
Loss in PE = KE
mg(25 - 20) = {tex}\frac{1}{2} m v^2 {/tex} {tex}\therefore {/tex} v2 = 100 or v = 10 ms-1

questions.question: Which of the statement is not true for a machine?

options.option: It can change the direction of applied force
Its output can be more than input

questions.explanation: Its output can be more than input.

questions.question: Which of the statement is not true for an actual machine?

options.option: Its mechanical advantage is less than velocity ratio.
Its mechanical advantage is greater than velocity ratio.
Output of machine is always less than input.

questions.explanation: Its mechanical advantage is greater than velocity ratio.

questions.question: A lever which always has mechanical advantage less than 1 has:

options.option: Effort and load act at same point.
Effort between the load and the fulcrum.

questions.explanation: Effort between the load and the fulcrum.

questions.question: The correct relationship between the effort (E) load (L) and mechanical advantage (M.A.) is:
questions.explanation: M.A. {tex}\times{/tex} E = L

questions.question: The correct relationship between the velocity ratio (V.R) the distance through which effort act (d) and the distance through which load moves (D)
questions.explanation: V.R. {tex}\times{/tex} D = d

questions.question: The correct relationship between the efficiency {tex}(\eta){/tex}, mechanical advantage (M.A.) and velocity ratio (V.R.) of an actual machine is:

options.option: both {tex}\eta{/tex} = M.A. {tex}\div{/tex} V.R. and {tex}\eta{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} V.R. = M.A.

questions.explanation: {tex}\eta{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} V.R. = M.A.

questions.question: A fire tong is a lever of:
questions.explanation: Third order

questions.question: A person standing vertically upon his toes is an example of lever of:
questions.explanation: Third order

questions.question: A lever which always have mechanical advantage more than 1 is:
questions.explanation: Lever of second order

questions.question: A lever which can have mechanical advantage either less than one or more than one depending upon the position of fulcrum is a lever of:
questions.explanation: First order

questions.question: In operating a water pump, a resistance of 480 N is overcome by an effort of 72 N. If the distance of the fulcrum from the point where the resistance act is 0.2 m. Find the distance from the fulcrum where effort is applied.
questions.explanation:
Load = resistance = 480 N
Effort E = 72 N Load arm = 0.2 m
Effort arm x = ?
Effort {tex}\times{/tex} Effort arm = Load {tex}\times{/tex} Load arm
72 {tex}\times{/tex} x = 480 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.2
x = {tex}\frac{480}{72} \times \frac{2}{10}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{4}{5}{/tex} = 1.33 m

questions.question: A uniform plank of a see-saw is 5 m long and supported at its centre. A boy weighing 40 kgf sits at a distance 1.5 m from one end of the see-saw where must a girl weighing 25 kgf sit on the other end of the see-saw so as to balance the boy?
questions.explanation:
AC = 1.5
{tex}\therefore{/tex} AF = 2 : 5 - 1.5 = 1 m
Effort {tex}\times{/tex} effort arm = Load {tex}\times{/tex} load arm
25 {tex}\times{/tex} x = 40 {tex}\times{/tex} 1
x = {tex}\frac{40}{25}=\frac{8}{5}{/tex} = 1.6 m = BF

{tex}\therefore{/tex} distance from one end D = BD
BD = 2.5 - 1.6 = 0.9 m

questions.question: The handle of a nutcracker is 16 cm long and a nut is placed 2 cm from its hinge. If a force of 4 kgf is applied at the end of the handle to crack it, what weight, if simply, placed on the nut will crack it?
questions.explanation:
1. Effort arm = 16 cm = {tex}\frac{16}{100}{/tex} m
    Effort E = 4 kgf
    Load arm = 2 cm = {tex}\frac{2}{100}{/tex} m
    Load = L suppose
    L {tex}\times{/tex} load arm = E {tex}\times{/tex} effort arm
    L {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac{2}{100}{/tex} = {tex}4 \times \frac{16}{100}{/tex}
    L = {tex}\frac{64}{100} \times \frac{100}{2}{/tex} = 32 kgf

questions.question: An effort of 50 kgf is applied at the end of a lever of the second order, which supports a load of 750 kgf, such that the load is at a distance of 0.1 m from the hinge. Find the length of the lever. (Assume that the lever is weightless)
questions.explanation:
Length of lever = Effort arm = AE
Load arm AB = 0.1 m
Effort E = 50 kgf
Load L = 750 kgf
{tex}\therefore{/tex} AE {tex}\times{/tex} 50 = 750 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.1
AE = {tex}\frac{750}{50} \times \frac{1}{10}{/tex} = 1.5 m

questions.question: A walnut can be broken by applying a direct force of 50 kgf. If the walnut is placed in a nut crackers, the length of whose handle is 18 cm and nut is placed 2 cm from the pivot, calculate the minimum force required to crack the nut.
questions.explanation:
Nut cracker is a lever of IInd order

Effort arm BE = 18 - 2 = 16 cm
Load arm AB = 2 cm
E = ? L = 50 kgf
E {tex}\times{/tex} 16 = 50 {tex}\times{/tex} 2
E = {tex}\frac{100}{16}{/tex} = 6.25 kgf

questions.question: A key is 5 cm long the turns the levers of a lock at a distance 1 cm. If the lock is opened by an effort of 10 N, calculate the resistance offered by the levers of the lock.
questions.explanation:
Lock and key IInd order lever
L {tex}\times{/tex} Load arm = E {tex}\times{/tex} Effort arm
L {tex}\times{/tex} 1 = 10 {tex}\times{/tex} 4
L = 40 N

questions.question: A door is 1 m wide. It can be closed by an effort of 25 N, when effort is applied at a distance of 0.4 m from the hinge. What effort is needed, if it is applied at its extreme end?
questions.explanation:
E {tex}\times{/tex} Effort arm
25 N {tex}\times{/tex} 0.4 m
{tex}\because{/tex} work done in both cases is same
Now effort EN {tex}\times{/tex} 1 m = 10
E = {tex}\frac{10}{1}{/tex} = 10 N

questions.question: A nut can be opened by a lever of length 0.25 m by applying a force of 80 N. What should be the length of lever, if a force of 32 N is enough to open the nut?
questions.explanation:
32 N {tex}\times{/tex} Force arm = 80 N {tex}\times{/tex} 0.25 m
Force arm = {tex}\frac{80}{32} \times \frac{25}{100}{/tex} = 0.625 m

questions.question: A single fixed pulley is used because:

options.option: it changes the direction of applied effort

questions.explanation: it changes the direction of applied effort

questions.question: The actual mechanical advantage of a single movable pulley is:
questions.explanation: less than 2, but more than 1

questions.question: A sheaf pulley system has:

options.option: both efficiency more than 100% and multiplies speed

questions.explanation: multiplies effort

questions.question: A pulley system has 5 pulley in all. Its velocity ratio is:
A single movable pulley has:

options.option: velocity ratio 2, and actual mechanical disadvantage more than 2
velocity ratio 2, and actual mechanical advantage less than 2
velocity ratio 2, and actual mechanical advantage more than 2

questions.explanation: velocity ratio 2, and actual mechanical advantage less than 2

questions.question: For an ideal machine, the efficiency is:
questions.explanation: equal to unity

questions.question: Mechanical advantage of a machine is defined as:
questions.explanation: {tex}\frac{\text { Load }}{\text { Effort }}{/tex}

questions.question: The mechanical advantage of a lever is equal to:
questions.explanation: {tex}\frac{\text { Effort arm }}{\text { Load arm }}{/tex}

questions.question: A pulley is used because it:

options.option: requires more effort to raise a less load
has the mechanical advantage greater than one
helps to apply the force in a convenient direction

questions.explanation: helps to apply the force in a convenient direction

questions.question: Wheel is used with axle because:

options.option: rolling friction is less than the sliding friction
sliding friction is less than the rolling friction
they help us to change the direction of force

questions.explanation: rolling friction is less than the sliding friction

questions.question: In which simple machine is the direction of application of effort conveniently changed?
questions.explanation: A pulley changes the direction of effort force, making it convenient to pull loads.

questions.question: If a block and tackle system with convenient direction has 3 movable pulleys, then its velocity ratio
questions.explanation:
Given, Number of movable pulleys {tex}=3{/tex}
∴ So, total pulleys can be double the movable or plus one.

questions.question: A pulley system has a single fixed pulley and a single movable pulley. Its mechanical advantage is
questions.explanation:
A single fixed pulley has mechanical advantage as
{tex}{MA}_{f}=\frac{T}{T}=1{/tex}
The single movable pulley has mechanical advantage
{tex}{MA}_{m}=\frac{2 T}{T}=2{/tex}
So, the given pulley system has mechanical advantage as, {tex}{MA}={MA}_{f} \times {MA}_{m}=1 \times 2=2{/tex}

questions.question: A person draws 10 kg water from a well at depth of 5 m using a single fixed pulley by applying an effort of 120 N. Then, the efficiency of the machine is (Take, {tex}g=10 {~ms}^{-2}{/tex})
questions.explanation:
Weight of water {tex}=10 \times 10=100 {~N}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \eta =\frac{\text { Work output }}{\text { Work input }}=\frac{L \times d_{L}}{E \times d_{E}}=\frac{100 \times 5}{120 \times 5}{/tex}
{tex}\eta =\frac{100}{120} \times 100 \simeq 83 \%{/tex}

questions.question: A pulley system has {tex}{MA}=4{/tex}. If a load of 10 kgf is pulled by a distance of 2 m in 10 s, calculate the power developed by the effort.
questions.explanation:
{tex}M A=4{/tex}
Load {tex}(L)=10 {kgf}{/tex}
{tex}{MA}=\frac{L}{E} \Rightarrow E=\frac{10}{4} {kgf}=\left(\frac{10}{4} \times 10\right) {N}{/tex}
∴ Work done is moving effort by a distance of 2 m
{tex}=\left(\frac{100}{4} \times 2\right) {J}=50 {~J}{/tex}
∴ Power developed {tex}=\frac{W}{t}=\frac{50}{10}=5 {~W}{/tex}

questions.question: During his experiments with a single movable pulley, Jas determined the effort, mechanical advantage and efficiency as {tex}X, Y{/tex} and {tex}Z{/tex}, respectively. Subsequently, after lubricating the pulley thoroughly, he recalculated the effort, MA and efficiency. {tex}X^{\prime}, Y^{\prime}{/tex} and {tex}Z^{\prime}{/tex} respectively. Which of the following relationships accurately represents the scenario?
questions.explanation: In the above scenario, the effort reduces since lubrication reduces friction. The mechanical advantage increases since the effort decreases and hence the overall efficiency of the system increases.

questions.question:
A woman draws water from a well using a fixed pulley. The mass of the bucket and the water together is 10 kg. The force applied by the woman is 200 N. The mechanical advantage is
(Take, {tex}g=10 {~m} / {s}^{2}{/tex})
questions.explanation:
Given load {tex}={/tex} weight of water
{tex}L=10 \times 10=100 N{/tex}
Effort = force applied
{tex}E=200 {~N}{/tex}
Now, MA {tex}=\frac{\text { Load }}{\text { Effort }}=\frac{100}{200}=0.5{/tex}

questions.question: For a lever, a graph is plotted with load on {tex}Y{/tex}-axis and effort on {tex}X{/tex}-axis. Which of the following represents the slope of the graph?
questions.explanation:
Mechanical advantage {tex}=\frac{\text { Load }}{\text { Effort }}{/tex}
and slope of a graph {tex}=\frac{\Delta Y}{\Delta X}{/tex}

questions.question: A movable pulley is used as

options.option: a device to change the direction of effort

questions.explanation: By using a single movable pulley, a load can be lifted with an effort equal to half the load. So, it acts as a force multiplier.

questions.question: Which of the following is a class III lever?
questions.explanation: In third class lever, effort is situated between the load and the fulcrum. Human forearm is a class III lever.

questions.question: Class II lever are designed to have
questions.explanation: In class II lever, the effort arm is always longer than the load arm, so its Mechanical Advantage (MA) is always more than 1 , i.e. MA {tex} \gt 1{/tex}.

subtopics.name: Work Done by a Constant Force

questions.question: What is the kinetic energy of an object at rest?
questions.explanation: An object that does not move is commonly defined to have zero kinetic energy, as kinetic energy depends on motion.

questions.question: The energy stored by an object as a result of its deformation is called
questions.explanation: Potential energy is the energy stored by an object as a result of its deformation or in a system of objects due to their relative positions.

questions.question: What energy transformation occurs when a ringing bell produces sound?
questions.explanation: A ringing bell converts mechanical energy into sound energy.

questions.question: Which of the following describes a situation where work done is equal to zero?
questions.explanation: Work done is zero if a force acts in a direction perpendicular to the displacement of an object.

questions.question: What is the formula for mechanical advantage?
questions.explanation: Mechanical advantage is defined as the ratio of the load to the effort.

questions.question: What is the SI unit of power?
questions.explanation: The SI unit of power is the watt (W).

questions.question: What is the mathematical formula for average power (P) in terms of work done (W) and time taken (t)?
questions.explanation: The formula P = W / t correctly defines average power as the work done divided by the time taken.

questions.question: Which physical quantity describes the difference between performing the same work in different time intervals?
questions.explanation: Power is defined as the rate at which work is done, explaining the difference when work is done over varying time intervals.

questions.question: What happens to the kinetic energy of an object if positive work is done on it and its velocity increases?
questions.explanation: When positive work is done on an object and its velocity increases, its kinetic energy also increases.

questions.question: The sum of the kinetic energy and the potential energy of an object is called its:
questions.explanation: Mechanical energy is defined as the sum of an object's kinetic energy and potential energy.

questions.question: Which of the following objects possesses kinetic energy?
questions.explanation: All moving objects possess kinetic energy. A rolling ball is in motion.

questions.question: What is the SI unit of kinetic energy?
questions.explanation: The SI unit of kinetic energy is the joule (J), which is also the unit for work and potential energy.

questions.question: Which form of energy stored in the food we consume is used by our muscles and converted into mechanical energy?
questions.explanation: The chemical energy in the food we eat powers our muscles and gets converted into mechanical energy.

questions.question: In an electric water heater, what conversion of energy takes place?
questions.explanation: In an electric water heater, electrical energy is converted into the thermal energy of the water.

questions.question: Which of the following describes a method by which energy can be transferred between objects other than through mechanical work?
questions.explanation: 'Energy can also be transferred as heat.'

questions.question: What happens to an object when positive work is done on it?
questions.explanation: 'When positive work is done on an object, it gains energy.'

questions.question: What is the fundamental relationship between a force and displacement for work to be done?
questions.explanation: Work is defined as force multiplied by displacement in the direction of the force, meaning both a force and a displacement in its direction are necessary.

questions.question: When is work done by a force on an object considered negative?
questions.explanation: Work done is negative when the displacement is in the direction opposite to that of the force.

questions.question: When is work done by a force on an object considered positive?
questions.explanation: Work done is positive when the displacement is in the same direction as the applied force.

questions.question: How is 1 joule defined in terms of newtons and metres?
questions.explanation: 1 joule of work is done when a constant force of 1 newton displaces an object by 1 metre in the direction of the force.

questions.question: What is the SI unit of work done?
questions.explanation: The SI unit of work done is the joule (J).

questions.question: Which statement best contrasts work and power?

options.option: Total energy vs rate of energy transfer.
Depends on time vs independent of time.

questions.explanation: Work is defined as the energy transferred when a force causes displacement, representing a total amount. Power is defined as the rate at which work is done (or energy is transferred), focusing on how quickly that transfer occurs. This distinction is crucial in understanding their respective physical meanings.

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of simple machines in everyday life?
questions.explanation: Simple machines help in doing work by changing either the magnitude of the force needed or its direction, making the task feel easier.

questions.question: How is 'mechanical advantage' defined for a machine?
questions.explanation: Mechanical advantage is defined as the ratio of the load (force to be overcome) to the effort (force applied).

questions.question: What is the mechanical advantage of a fixed pulley?
questions.explanation: For a fixed pulley, the effort and the load are equal in magnitude, so the mechanical advantage is 1.

questions.question: What is the primary function of a fixed pulley?
questions.explanation: A fixed pulley provides convenience by allowing one to pull downward to lift a load, thus changing the direction of the effort but not its magnitude.

questions.question: What is the effect of making an inclined plane less steep (reducing the angle between the plank and the base)?
questions.explanation: The activity demonstrates that as the plank becomes less steep (smaller angle), the force required to pull the cart up decreases.

questions.question: What are the three main parts of a lever?
questions.explanation: A lever consists of a fulcrum (fixed point), a load (force to overcome), and an effort (force applied).

questions.question: In a lever, if the effort arm is increased, how does this affect the force applied to the load (F2) compared to the applied effort (F1)?

options.option: F2's relation to F1 depends solely on the load.

questions.explanation: By increasing the effort arm, the lever applies a larger force F2 to the load compared to the applied effort F1, demonstrating mechanical advantage greater than 1.

questions.question: Which of the following is an example of a Class I lever?
questions.explanation: In scissors, the fulcrum (pivot point) is between the effort and the load, classifying it as a Class I lever.

questions.question: Which of the following is an example of a Class II lever?
questions.explanation: In a bottle opener, the load (the cap) is between the fulcrum and the effort, making it a Class II lever.

questions.question: What is the primary characteristic of a Class I lever?

options.option: Fulcrum is in between the load and effort.
Effort and load are on the same side of the fulcrum.
Effort is in between the fulcrum and load.
Load is in between the fulcrum and effort.

questions.explanation: In a Class I lever, the fulcrum is situated between the effort and the load.

questions.question: What remains constant for a person moving an object up an inclined plane at a constant speed, regardless of the steepness?
questions.explanation: The work done (product of force and displacement) is the same in all cases, assuming the same height is reached and ignoring friction.

questions.question: A person uses an inclined ramp to raise an object over a step 30 cm high. The ramp has a width of 40 cm. What is the mechanical advantage of this ramp?
questions.explanation:
Given height (h) = 30 cm.
The width (base) = 40 cm.
Using the Pythagorean theorem for the right-angled triangle formed by the height, width, and ramp length (L): {tex}L^2 = h^2 + \text{width}^2{/tex}.
So, {tex}L^2 = 30^2 + 40^2 = 900 + 1600 = 2500{/tex}. Thus, {tex}L = \sqrt{2500} = 50{/tex} cm.
The mechanical advantage (MA) of an inclined plane is given by {tex}MA = L/h{/tex}.
{tex}MA = 50 \text{ cm} / 30 \text{ cm} = 1.666... \approx 1.67{/tex}.

questions.question: Why are roads on hills often built to wind around in gentle slopes rather than going straight up?
questions.explanation: Winding roads act as inclined planes. By increasing the length (L) for a given height (h), the slope becomes gentler, and the effort (force) required to move up the hill decreases (MA = L/h). The total work done remains the same, but the force applied is smaller, spread over a longer distance.

questions.question: A system of pulleys is used to lift heavy objects. Which statement accurately describes its mechanical advantage?
questions.explanation: Movable pulleys or a system of pulleys allow the load to be shared by multiple rope segments, effectively reducing the effort required and thus achieving a mechanical advantage greater than 1.

questions.question: Consider a situation where you are lifting a heavy box. You have the option to lift it directly or push it up an inclined plank. Analyze the work done and force required in both scenarios, ignoring friction.

options.option: Less work, more force (lift); more work, less force (ramp).

questions.explanation: The work-energy theorem states that the work done to raise an object to a certain height against gravity is equal to the potential energy gained (mgh). This work is the same whether lifted directly or pushed up an inclined plane. The inclined plane, however, reduces the required force (effort) by spreading it over a longer distance.

questions.question: A seesaw has seats A, B, D, E and a fulcrum at C. AC = EC = 2 m and BC = DC = 1 m. If a child of mass 15 kg sits on seat A, where should a child of mass 30 kg sit to balance the seesaw?

options.option: It is impossible for them to balance.

questions.explanation:
Using the principle of moments for balance: {tex}F_1 \times d_1 = F_2 \times d_2{/tex}.
Let's consider mass as force for simplicity in balancing calculation.
Child 1:
mass {tex}m_1 = 15{/tex} kg,
distance {tex}d_1 = AC = 2{/tex} m.
Child 2: mass {tex}m_2 = 30{/tex} kg,
distance {tex}d_2 = ?{/tex}.
{tex}15 \text{ kg} \times 2 \text{ m} = 30 \text{ kg} \times d_2{/tex}.
{tex}30 = 30 \times d_2{/tex}. {tex}d_2 = 1{/tex} m.
Seat D is at a distance of 1 m from the fulcrum C (DC = 1 m).

questions.question: What happens to the effort required to move a load with a lever if the effort arm is increased?
questions.explanation: The principle of levers states that by increasing the effort arm, the effort required to balance or move a given load is reduced.

questions.question: If an object gains 1000 J of kinetic energy over 5 seconds while accelerating uniformly, and a second object gains the same amount of kinetic energy over 2 seconds, how does the average power applied to the second object compare to that of the first?
questions.explanation:
For the first object:
Work done (W1) = 1000 J
Time (t1) = 5 s
Power (P1) = W1 / t1 = 1000 J / 5 s = 200 W
For the second object:
Work done (W2) = 1000 J
Time (t2) = 2 s
Power (P2) = W2 / t2 = 1000 J / 2 s = 500 W
To compare P2 to P1: P2 / P1 = 500 W / 200 W = 2.5.
Therefore, the power applied to the second object is 2.5 times that of the first.

questions.question: An engine is described in terms of its horsepower (hp). If an engine has a power output of 2 hp, how much work can it do in 10 seconds?
questions.explanation:
Given: Power = 2 hp Time (t) = 10 seconds
First, convert horsepower (hp) to watts (W): 1 hp = 746 W
So, 2 hp = {tex}2 \times 746 \text{ W} = 1492 \text{ W}{/tex}
Next, calculate the work done (W) using the formula P = W/t:
{tex}W = P \times t{/tex}
{tex}W = 1492 \text{ W} \times 10 \text{ s}{/tex}
{tex}W = 14920 \text{ J}{/tex}
Therefore, the engine can do 14920 J of work in 10 seconds.

questions.question: A machine applies a constant force of 50 N to move an object 10 meters in 2 seconds. What is the power exerted by the machine?
questions.explanation:
Given: Force (F) = 50 N
Distance (d) = 10 m
Time (t) = 2 s
First, calculate the work done (W): {tex}W = F \times d{/tex}
{tex}W = 50 \text{ N} \times 10 \text{ m}{/tex}
{tex}W = 500 \text{ J}{/tex}
Next, calculate the power (P): {tex}P = \frac{W}{t}{/tex}
{tex}P = \frac{500 \text{ J}}{2 \text{ s}}{/tex}
{tex}P = 250 \text{ W}{/tex}
Therefore, the power exerted by the machine is 250 W.

questions.question: If two individuals perform the same amount of work, but one completes it in half the time of the other, what can be concluded about their power output?
questions.explanation:
Power (P) is defined as work (W) divided by time (t), i.e., P = W/t.
If work (W) is constant and time (t) is halved (t/2), then
Pnew = W/(t/2) = 2W/t = 2P.
Therefore, the power output is doubled.

questions.question: A car of mass 1000 kg starts from rest and reaches a speed of 72 km/h in 10 seconds. Calculate the power of the engine required to achieve this start.
questions.explanation:
Given: Mass (m) = 1000 kg
Initial velocity (u) = 0 m/s (starts from rest)
Final velocity (v) = 72 km/h
Time (t) = 10 s
First, convert final velocity from km/h to m/s:
{tex}v = 72 \text{ km/h} = 72 \times \frac{1000 \text{ m}}{3600 \text{ s}} = 20 \text{ m/s}{/tex}
Next, calculate the acceleration (a): {tex}v = u + at{/tex}
{tex}20 = 0 + a \times 10{/tex}
{tex}a = \frac{20}{10} = 2 \text{ m/s}^2{/tex}
Now, calculate the force (F) required by the engine: {tex}F = ma{/tex}
{tex}F = 1000 \text{ kg} \times 2 \text{ m/s}^2 = 2000 \text{ N}{/tex}
To find the power, we can use the formula P = Fv, where v is the average velocity or considering the work done.
Since acceleration is constant, the work done (W) can be calculated as the change in kinetic energy:
{tex}W = \frac{1}{2}mv^2 - \frac{1}{2}mu^2{/tex}
{tex}W = \frac{1}{2} \times 1000 \text{ kg} \times (20 \text{ m/s})^2 - \frac{1}{2} \times 1000 \text{ kg} \times (0 \text{ m/s})^2{/tex}
{tex}W = \frac{1}{2} \times 1000 \times 400 - 0{/tex}
{tex}W = 200,000 \text{ J}{/tex}
Finally, calculate the power (P): {tex}P = \frac{W}{t}{/tex}
{tex}P = \frac{200,000 \text{ J}}{10 \text{ s}}{/tex}
{tex}P = 20,000 \text{ W}{/tex}
Therefore, the power of the engine required is 20,000 W.

questions.question: A weightlifter lifts a 75 kg mass by 2 m in 5 seconds. What is the power required for this task? Assume g = 10 m/s2.
questions.explanation:
Given:
Mass (m) = 75 kg
Height (h) = 2 m
Time (t) = 5 s
Acceleration due to gravity (g) = 10 m/s2
First, calculate the work done (W): {tex}W = mgh{/tex}
{tex}W = 75 \text{ kg} \times 10 \ { m/s}^2 \times 2 \text{ m}{/tex}
{tex}W = 1500 \text{ J}{/tex}
Next, calculate the power (P): {tex}P = \frac{W}{t}{/tex}
{tex}P = \frac{1500 \text{ J}}{5 \text{ s}}{/tex}
{tex}P = 300 \text{ W}{/tex}
Therefore, the power required is 300 W.

questions.question: Which scenario best illustrates the storage of potential energy due to relative positions in a system?

options.option: A car speeding up on a flat road.
A spring returning to its original shape.

questions.explanation: When unlike poles of two magnets are separated, they can store energy due to their relative positions because they attract each other. When released, they move towards each other, gaining kinetic energy from the stored potential energy.

questions.question: An object's potential energy decreases while its kinetic energy increases as it falls. If the increase in kinetic energy is equal to the decrease in potential energy (both {tex}\frac{1}{2}mg^2t^2{/tex}), what does this indicate about the object's total mechanical energy?
questions.explanation: When an object's lost potential energy is converted entirely into kinetic energy during motion, its total mechanical energy (sum of kinetic and potential) remains constant.

questions.question: What is the change in the kinetic energy of an object when its initial velocity is u = 0, and it moves with a final velocity v?
questions.explanation:
If the initial velocity of the object is {tex}u = 0{/tex}, the change in the energy of the object is equal to its final kinetic energy.
The kinetic energy is given by {tex}K = \frac{1}{2}mv^2{/tex}.

questions.question: An object of mass 'm' is lifted to a height 'h'. What is the expression for its gravitational potential energy 'U' at this height, assuming 'g' is the acceleration due to gravity?
questions.explanation: The gravitational potential energy for an object of mass 'm' at a height 'h' is given by the formula {tex}U = mgh{/tex}.

questions.question: A child slides down a frictionless slide. If the height of the slide is 'h', what will be the magnitude of the child's velocity at the bottom of the slide, assuming the child starts from rest?
questions.explanation:
At the top, potential energy = {tex}mgh{/tex} and kinetic energy = 0.
At the bottom, potential energy = 0 and kinetic energy = {tex}\frac{1}{2}mv^2{/tex}.
By conservation of mechanical energy (neglecting friction), {tex}mgh = \frac{1}{2}mv^2{/tex}.
Cancelling 'm' from both sides: {tex}gh = \frac{1}{2}v^2{/tex}.
So, {tex}v^2 = 2gh{/tex}, and {tex}v = \sqrt{2gh}{/tex}.

questions.question: A jet aircraft of mass 15000 kg lands on an aircraft carrier. A hook on the tail catches a wire, exerting a constant backward force of 367500 N and stopping the jet within 100 m. What was the initial velocity of the aircraft just before the wire caught the hook?
questions.explanation:
Given mass (m) = 15000 kg, force (F) = 367500 N, displacement (s) = 100 m.
Initial kinetic energy = {tex}\frac{1}{2}mv^2{/tex},
Final kinetic energy = 0.
Change in kinetic energy = {tex}0 - \frac{1}{2}mv^2{/tex}.
Work done by the force = {tex}F \times s{/tex}.
Since the force is backward, work done is negative:
{tex}W = -367500 \times 100 = -36750000 \text{ J}{/tex}.
By work-energy theorem, {tex}-\frac{1}{2}mv^2 = -36750000{/tex}.
{tex}\frac{1}{2} \times 15000 \times v^2 = 36750000{/tex}.
{tex}7500v^2 = 36750000{/tex}.
{tex}v^2 = \frac{36750000}{7500} = 4900{/tex}.
{tex}v = \sqrt{4900} = 70 \text{ m/s}{/tex}.

questions.question: A cricket ball has a mass of 0.2 kg and is bowled at a velocity of 43 m/s. Calculate its kinetic energy.
questions.explanation:
Given mass (m) = 0.2 kg, velocity (v) = 43 m/s.
The formula for kinetic energy (K) is {tex}K = \frac{1}{2}mv^2{/tex}.
Substituting the values: {tex}K = \frac{1}{2} \times 0.2 \text{ kg} \times (43 \text{ m/s})^2 {/tex} {tex}= 0.1 \times 1849 = 184.9 \text{ J}{/tex}.

questions.question: A vehicle with mass 'm' and initial velocity 'v' has a kinetic energy of (1/2)mv2. If its velocity doubles to '2v', what will its new kinetic energy be?
questions.explanation:
Given mass = m, initial velocity = v.
Initial Kinetic Energy = {tex}\frac{1}{2}mv^2{/tex}.
If the velocity doubles to 2v, the new Kinetic Energy = {tex}\frac{1}{2}m(2v)^2 = \frac{1}{2}m(4v^2) = 4 \times \frac{1}{2}mv^2{/tex}.
Thus, the new kinetic energy will be 4 times the original value.

questions.question: If an object's velocity remains constant and no work is done on it, what can be concluded about its kinetic energy?
questions.explanation: If no work is done by the force on the object (W = 0) and its velocity does not change, then its kinetic energy remains constant.

questions.question: What is the SI unit of gravitational potential energy?
questions.explanation: The unit of potential energy is the joule (J), which is the same as the unit of work done or that of kinetic energy.

questions.question: The energy stored when a spring is compressed or stretched is primarily due to what?
questions.explanation: While held in a deformed state, the energy of the spring is due to its configuration or shape.

questions.question: What is the primary factor determining the depth of the depression created when a ball is dropped into sand from different heights?

options.option: The height from which the ball is dropped.

questions.explanation: The depression is deepest when the ball is dropped from the greatest height, indicating that greater height leads to greater potential energy and thus a deeper depression.

questions.question: How is the potential energy of an object related to its deformation or its relative position within a system?

options.option: Energy stored due to position or deformation.
It is always zero for objects in motion.
It is directly proportional to its motion.

questions.explanation: The energy stored by an object as a result of its deformation or in a system of objects due to their relative positions is called the potential energy.

questions.question: What does the work-energy theorem state regarding the work done by a force on an object?
questions.explanation: From the work-energy theorem, the work done by the force acting on the object translates into the change in the energy of the object.

questions.question: Under what condition does an object have zero kinetic energy?

options.option: When it is at a high altitude.
When a force is applied to it.

questions.explanation: An object that does not move is commonly defined to have zero kinetic energy, meaning it starts from rest.

questions.question: Why is it common to define an object that does not move as having zero kinetic energy?
questions.explanation: Kinetic energy is the energy of motion, mathematically dependent on velocity. If an object does not move, its velocity is zero, hence its kinetic energy is zero.

questions.question: An object's kinetic energy is a direct result of which characteristic?
questions.explanation: Kinetic energy is defined as the energy possessed by an object due to its motion.

questions.question: Which of the following describes mechanical energy?
questions.explanation: Mechanical energy is defined as the energy that an object possesses due to its motion or position.

questions.question: If electrical energy is used to power an electric water heater, what form of energy is primarily produced?
questions.explanation: An electric water heater's function is to heat water, converting electrical energy into thermal energy.

questions.question: A car with an initial velocity of 0 m/s accelerates to a final velocity of {tex}v{/tex} m/s under the action of a constant force {tex}F{/tex} over a distance {tex}s{/tex}. If the work done by the force is {tex}W = F \times s{/tex}, what does this work represent in terms of the car's energy?

options.option: The thermal energy generated by the car's engine.

questions.explanation: According to the work-energy theorem, since the car starts from rest, the work done by the net force equals the change in kinetic energy, which in this case is the final kinetic energy (K).

questions.question: An object starts from rest and acquires a velocity {tex}v{/tex} under the influence of a force {tex}F{/tex} over a displacement {tex}s{/tex}. According to the work-energy theorem, how is the work done by the force related to the object's kinetic energy?
questions.explanation: The work-energy theorem states that the net work done on an object equals the change in its kinetic energy. Since the object starts from rest, the work done equals the final kinetic energy gained.

questions.question: A ringing bell converting mechanical energy into sound energy is an example of which phenomenon?
questions.explanation: The conversion of mechanical energy into sound energy by a ringing bell is an example of energy transformation, where energy changes from one form to another.

questions.question: Which form of energy allows us to see?
questions.explanation: Light energy is the form of energy that allows us to see.

questions.question: What type of energy is possessed by an object due to its motion?
questions.explanation: Kinetic energy is defined as the energy possessed by an object due to its motion.

questions.question: Which of the following is defined as the capacity to do work?
questions.explanation: Energy is defined as the capacity to do work.

questions.question: In a carrom game, a striker hits a white coin, which then hits a black coin. The striker does positive work on the white coin. What can be inferred about the white coin's energy and the work done by the white coin on the striker?
questions.explanation: The striker does positive work on the white coin, increasing its energy. By Newton's third law, the white coin applies an opposite force and does negative work on the striker, decreasing its energy.

questions.question: A fielder throws a cricket ball, providing it with energy. When the ball hits the wicket, it causes the wicket to fall. Which of the following best describes the energy transfer in this scenario?
questions.explanation: The ball gains energy from the work done by the fielder in throwing it. When it hits the wickets, it transfers its energy to them, making them move, which is an application of the work-energy principle.

questions.question: How can energy be transferred between objects in contact at different temperatures?
questions.explanation: When two objects at different temperatures come in contact, energy flows from the hotter one to the colder one.

questions.question: An object possesses energy. What does this imply about the object?

options.option: It has the capacity to do work.
It is currently moving at a high speed.

questions.explanation: An object having the capacity to do work is said to possess energy.

questions.question: What must occur for work to be done on an object?
questions.explanation: Work is done when a force is applied and an object is displaced.

questions.question: What is the relationship between work done on an object and its energy, according to the work-energy theorem?
questions.explanation: The work-energy theorem states that work done on an object appears as a change in its energy.

questions.question: Which of the following is the SI unit for both work and energy?
questions.explanation: The SI unit of energy is the same as the SI unit of work, the joule (J).

questions.question: Assuming a constant gravitational force, if you lift a 5 kg wheat bag slowly to different heights, how does the work done relate to the height?

options.option: Work done remains constant regardless of height.
Work done is directly proportional to the height.
Work done is inversely proportional to the height.

questions.explanation: Since the force (mg) is constant, and work done = force × displacement (height in this case), then W ∝ h. Therefore, work done is directly proportional to the height.

questions.question: When you push a wheelchair and its displacement is in the same direction as your applied force, what type of work are you doing on the wheelchair?
questions.explanation: When the displacement is in the same direction as the applied force, the work done on the object is positive.

questions.question: A weightlifter holds a barbell perfectly stationary above their head. Is the weightlifter performing work on the barbell?
questions.explanation: Work done requires both a force and a displacement in the direction of that force. If the barbell is held steady, its displacement is zero, thus no work is done on it.

questions.question: A force-displacement graph shows a constant force of 10 N applied over a distance of 1 m. What is the work done?
questions.explanation:
Given: {tex}F = 10 \text{ N}, s = 1 \text{ m}{/tex}
Using the formula for work done: {tex}W = F \times s{/tex}
Substituting the values: {tex}W = 10 \text{ N} \times 1 \text{ m}{/tex}
{tex}W = 10 \text{ J}{/tex}

questions.question: A goalkeeper stopped a ball by moving their hand back by 15 cm. If the goalkeeper applied a force of 200 N, what was the work done on the ball? (Assume the force was opposite to the ball's motion.)
questions.explanation:
Given: {tex}F = 200 \text{ N}{/tex}
Since the force is opposite to the motion, the displacement is negative: {tex}s = -15 \text{ cm} = -0.15 \text{ m}{/tex}
Using the formula for work done: {tex}W = F \times s{/tex}
Substituting the values: {tex}W = 200 \text{ N} \times (-0.15 \text{ m}){/tex}
{tex}W = -30 \text{ J}{/tex}

questions.question: A person applies an upward force of 200 N to lift an object. If the object is displaced by 1.5 m in the direction of the force, calculate the work done.
questions.explanation:
Given: {tex}F = 200 \text{ N}, s = 1.5 \text{ m}{/tex}
Using the formula for work done: {tex}W = F \times s{/tex}
Substituting the values: {tex}W = 200 \text{ N} \times 1.5 \text{ m}{/tex}
{tex}W = 300 \text{ J}{/tex}

questions.question: What does it mean for work done to be 'directly proportional' to both force applied and displacement?
questions.explanation: Applying a larger force over the same distance, or the same force over a larger distance, both proportionally do more work. This indicates a direct proportionality.

questions.question: If you lift 3 bags together to the same height, requiring a force 3 times greater than for a single bag, how does the work done compare to the work done for lifting 1 bag, assuming the same height?
questions.explanation: If a force 3 times larger is applied over the same distance, the work done (W=F×s) will be 3 times greater than that required to lift 1 bag.

questions.question: Why is it important to specify the force (or agency) doing the work and the object on which the work is done while describing work?
questions.explanation: Specifying the force/agency and the object clarifies the context of work being done, as work involves an interaction between an agent and an object.

questions.question: A girl carries a box while walking horizontally. Why is the work done by the upward force she applies on the box considered zero?

options.option: The force is perpendicular to the displacement.
The box is moving at a uniform velocity.

questions.explanation: When a girl carries a box while walking, she applies an upward force to balance its weight, while the box moves horizontally. Since the force and displacement are perpendicular to each other, no work is done by this force on the box.

questions.question: Why might a person feel tired after pushing against a rigid wall, even though no work is done on the wall in a scientific sense?
questions.explanation: To apply a force, the muscles in the body repeatedly expand and contract, using up the internal energy of the body, leading to tiredness even without scientific work being done on the object.

questions.question: In a force-displacement graph, what does the area under the curve represent?
questions.explanation: The work done on an object by the force is equal to the area under the force-displacement graph.

questions.question: What is the work done when a force acts perpendicularly to the displacement of an object?
questions.explanation: If a force acts in a direction perpendicular to the displacement of an object, the work done by that force is zero.

questions.question: What is the work done on an object if the applied force is zero, regardless of displacement?
questions.explanation: If the force acting on an object is zero (F=0), then no work is done on the object, as W = F × s.

questions.question: A neutron is:
questions.explanation:
A neutron is a neutral sub-atomic particle and has mass.
Mass of neutron is 1.0086654 u or 1.6749 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-27 kg.

questions.question: {tex}\beta {/tex} -particles are represented as:-
questions.explanation: {tex}{}_{ - 1}^0e{/tex} is the correct representation of a beta particle. A beta particle is denoted by the lower-case Greek letter beta (Symbol {tex}\beta{/tex}). It is a high energy, high-speed electron or positron emitted in the radioactive decay of a nucleus. It is sometimes called a beta ray. An electron has negligible mass - hence the superscript 0. An electron has a negative charge - hence a subscript -1.

questions.question: {tex}{}_{18}^{40}Ar{/tex} and {tex}{}_{20}^{40}Ca{/tex} are:
questions.explanation: Isobars. They have the same mass number (i.e. the same number of nucleons) but different atomic number (i.e. different number of protons).

questions.question: The maximum number of electrons in the L shell is:
questions.explanation: L shell can accommodate a maximum of 8 electrons.

questions.question: An atomic number of an element is equal to:
questions.explanation: Atomic number (Z) of a chemical element is the number of protons in the nucleus of that atom. It is a characteristic of the element and determines its place in the periodic table. Atomic number = number of protons.

questions.question: The charge of a proton (p+) is:
questions.explanation: A proton is a positively charged sub-atomic particle. The charge on a proton is +1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-19 C.

questions.question: {tex}_{10}^{20}Ne{/tex} and {tex}_{10}^{22}Ne{/tex} are:
questions.explanation: {tex}_{10}^{20}Ne{/tex} and {tex}_{10}^{22}Ne{/tex} have the same atomic number (Z = 10) but different mass numbers. They are isotopes (Atoms of the same element - Neon - with the same atomic number but a different mass number).

questions.question: Helium {tex}\left( {_2^4He} \right){/tex} has :
questions.explanation: Helium {tex}\left( {_2^4He} \right){/tex} has 2 protons (p+) and 2 neutrons (no). Mass number (A) = 4. Atomic number (Z) = number of protons = 2. Number of neutrons = A - Z = 4 - 2 = 2

questions.question: How many electrons will Na+ have in its outermost shell?
questions.explanation: Sodium (Na) has an atomic number of 11 (Configuration 2, 8, 1) and 1 electron in its outermost shell. It loses 1 electron easily to form Na+ ion. Na+ ion will have 8 electrons in its outermost shell.

questions.question: The atomic number of an element during a chemical reaction:
questions.explanation: Atomic number (number of protons) of an element remains constant during a chemical reaction.

questions.question: Electronic configuration of calcium is:
questions.explanation: The atomic number of calcium is 20. Number of electrons = number of protons = 20. Therefore, the electronic configuration of calcium is 2, 8, 8, 2.

questions.question: Neutron has:
questions.explanation: A neutron has mass but it has no charge.

questions.question: Rutherford's alpha-particle scattering experiment was responsible for the discovery of:
questions.explanation: Rutherford's alpha-particle scattering experiment was responsible for the discovery of the atomic nucleus. It was discovered in 1911 by Ernest Rutherford based on the 1909 Geiger–Marsden gold foil experiment.

questions.question: Isotopes of an element have:
questions.explanation: Isotopes of an element are atoms with the same number of protons but a different number of neutrons. Since isotopes of an element have the same atomic number, they show the same physical properties and chemical properties.

questions.question: Number of valence electrons in Cl– ion is:
questions.explanation: Atomic number of Chlorine is 17 (Configuration 2, 8, 7). Since the atom gains an electron during the formation of Cl– ion, the number of valence electrons in Cl– ion is 8.

questions.question:
{tex}{}_{88}^{226}A,{\rm{ }}{}_{87}^{228}B,{\rm{ }}{}_{88}^{228}C,{\rm{ }}{}_{90}^{228}D{/tex}
Which of the following nuclei are isotopes?
questions.explanation: Isotopes are the elements with the same atomic number but different mass numbers. The elements A and C are having the same atomic number 88 but different mass number 226 and 228 respectively.

questions.question: The mass number of an isotope of an element is 295, if its nucleus has 196 neutrons. What is its atomic number?
questions.explanation: Atomic number is the number of protons present in the given element and can be calculated by subtracting neutrons from mass number.

questions.question:
Isotopes of an element have:

  1. Same chemical properties
  2. Same atomic masses
  3. Different atomic numbers
  4. Atoms of the same element
questions.explanation: Isotopes are atoms with the same number of protons but a different number of neutrons. Since the atomic number is equal to the number of protons and the atomic mass is the sum of the number of protons and neutrons, it can also be said that isotopes are atoms of the same element with the same atomic number but a different mass number. They have the same chemical properties.

questions.question: The order of the energy shells is:
questions.explanation: The energy of the shells increases from the inner-most to the outer-most shell. So the correct order of the energy of energy shells is K < L < M < N < O.

questions.question: Out of electron, positron, alpha particle and proton, which particle is regarded as the universal particle?
questions.explanation: Electron is considered as a universal particle as it is present in all atoms. It also provides the chemical properties to the atoms.

subtopics.name: How are Electrons Distributed In Different Orbits (Shells)?

questions.question: The {tex}\frac{charge}{mass}{/tex} ratio of electron

options.option: depends upon both nature of gas and nature of electrodes

questions.explanation: The {tex}\frac{charge}{mass}{/tex} on the type of ray one is passing. Since we are talking about electron the cathode ray particles are nothing but a beam of electrons. Since the charge, as well as mass, is constant for electron no matter any gas we take in during the experiment in the discharge tube. So, their charge/mass ratio remains constant for electron.

questions.question: Rutherford's scattering experiment fails for very small scattering angles because
