options.option: whole nuclear charge of the target atom is not screened by its electrons at all.
the impact parameter between the {tex}\alpha{/tex} - particle source and the nucleus of the target is very large as compared to the size of the nucleus.

questions.explanation:
1. An atom consists of a minute positively charged body at its center called a nucleus. The nucleus contains all the protons and neutrons. Since the total mass of the atom is because of protons and neutrons, hence the entire mass located at the centre of the atom. Hence few alpha particles deflected due to positive charge of the nucleus.
  2. An atom consists of a sufficient number of extremely small negatively charged electrons distributed around the nucleus to balance the positive charge of the nucleus. Hence the maximum volume of an atom remains empty so many alpha particles moved without any deflection. Since a very less number of alpha particles deflected, hence the volume of nucleus is very less compared to the whole atom. The size of nucleus is less than 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-14 m while the size of an atom is around 10-10 m.

questions.question: 1 mole of diatomic element X2 contains 34 and 40 moles of electrons and neutrons respectively. The isotopic formula of the element is
questions.explanation:
Since it contains 34 moles of electrons the atomic number is 17. The molecule contains 40 moles of neutrons hence it has 20 neutrons per atom. The formula for the element will be 17X37.
Elements having the same atomic number but different atomic masses are known as Isotopes. So, 17X40 is isotope of 17X37.

questions.question: The conclusion of Rutherford's scattering experiment does not include

options.option: Scattering follows coulomb's law, i.e., same charges repel each other.
{tex}\alpha{/tex}-particles can come within the distance of order of 10-14 m of the nucleus.
The radius of nucleus is less than 10-14 m.
The positively charged particles of atom move with very high velocity.

questions.explanation:
Conclusion of Rutherford's scattering experiment:

  1. Most of the space inside the atom is empty because most of the {tex}\alpha{/tex}-particles passed through the gold foil without getting deflected.
  2. Very few particles were deflected from their path, indicating that the positive charge of the atom occupies very little space.
  3. A very small fraction of {tex}\alpha{/tex}-particles were deflected by very large angles, indicating that all the positive charge and mass of the gold atom were concentrated in a very small volume within the atom.
  4. Since a very less number of alpha particles deflected, hence the volume of the nucleus is very less compared to the whole atom. The size of the nucleus is less than 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-14 m while the size of an atom is around 10-10 m.
    So, the positively charged particles of atom move with very high velocity do not include the conclusion of Rutherford's scattering experiment.

questions.question: An element has 3 valence electrons in its outermost 3rd shell, the name of element is
questions.explanation:
Number of electrons = 2 + 8 + 3 = 13.
The atomic number 13 belongs to the element Aluminum (Al).

questions.question: The electronic configuration of Cl– ion is
questions.explanation:
Neutral Chlorine (17Cl) has an electronic configuration as 2, 8,7.
Now we observe that Chlorine is one electron less than achieving the stable electronic configuration of its nearest Noble gas, Argon. So Chloride gains (steals) an electron (not from empty space, either from metal, semi-metal etc.) and becomes Chloride ion (so it becomes stable like Argon). So the electronic configuration of Chloride ion will be 2, 8, 8.

questions.question: Isotopes of an element have
questions.explanation: Elements having same atomic number but different atomic masses are known as Isotopes. Isotope have the same number of protons (and electrons), but different numbers of neutrons.

questions.question:
Which of the following are isotopes and which are isobars?
Argon (Ar), Deuterium (D), Calcium (Ca), Tritium (T), Protium (P)

options.option: Ar, Ca are isobars and D, T, P are isotopes
Ar, Ca are isotopes and D, T, P are isobars

questions.explanation:
Elements having the same atomic number but different atomic masses are known as Isotopes.
Protium(Hydrogen) -1, Deuterium – 2, Tritium -3 are three isotopes of hydrogen.
Atoms having the same atomic mass and different atomic numbers are known as Isobars. Example Argon, Calcium.

questions.question: The triad which is isoelectronic
questions.explanation:
An Isoelectronic series is a group of atoms/ions that have the same number of electrons.
Examples: N3-, O2-, F-, Ne, Na+, Mg2+, Al3+
This series each have 10 electrons.
P3-, S2-, Cl-, Ar, K+, Ca2+, Sc3+
This series each have 18 electrons.

questions.question: An element has electronic configuration 2, 8, 4. It will be classified as
questions.explanation:
There are some elements which have properties of metals and non-metal, such elements are known as Metalloids. Si have electronic configuration of 2, 8, 4.
Examples of Metalloids: Boron (B), Silicon (Si), Germanium (Ge), Arsenic (As), Antimony (Sb), Tellurium (Te), Polonium (Po), Astatine (At) etc.

questions.question: The isotope of carbon which has same number of neutrons as 8O16, is used in radiocarbon dating to determine age of old samples of living organisms?
questions.explanation: Carbon-14, 6C14, or radiocarbon, is a radioactive isotope of carbon with an atomic nucleus containing 6 protons and 8 neutrons. Its presence in organic materials is the basis of the radiocarbon dating method.

questions.question: The isotopes of hydrogen which contain same number of electrons, protons and neutrons?
questions.explanation: Hydrogen element has three isotopes: protium, deuterium, and tritium having the same atomic number of 1 but different mass number 1, 2, 3 respectively. Deuterium is isotopes of hydrogen which contain the same number of electrons, protons, and neutrons.

questions.question: Which of the following has the highest n/p ratio?
questions.explanation:
Number of neutron of {tex} {}_1^3H\; {/tex}= 3 – 1 = 2
Number of proton of {tex} {}_1^3H\; {/tex} = 1
N/p ratio {tex} {}_1^3H\; {/tex} = {tex}\begin{array}{l}\frac21\\\end{array}{/tex} = 2
N/P ratio of  {tex}{}_{92}^{235}U\;{/tex} = 1.55
N/P ratio of  {tex}{}_6^{14}C{/tex} = 1.33
N/p ratio of  {tex}{}_{88}^{222}Ra{/tex} = 1.52

questions.question: Atomic number is equal to

options.option: difference in mass number and number of electrons

questions.explanation: The number of protons in the nucleus of the atom is equal to the atomic number (Z). The number of electrons in a neutral atom is equal to the number of protons. The mass number of the atom (M) is equal to the sum of the number of protons and neutrons in the nucleus. The number of neutrons is equal to the difference between the mass number of the atom (M) and the atomic number (Z).

questions.question:
Fill in the gap using given analogy
Atomic number : Number of protons :: Mass number : _______.
questions.explanation:
Atomic number: The total number of protons in the nucleus of an atom gives us the atomic number of that atom. It is represented by the letter Z. All the atoms of a particular element have the same number of protons, and hence the same atomic number. Atoms of different elements have different atomic numbers.
Mass number: The number of protons and neutrons combined give us the mass number of an atom. It is represented using the letter ‘A.’ As both protons and neutrons are present in the nucleus of an atom, they are together called nucleons.

questions.question: The highest value of e/m of anode rays has been observed when the discharge tube is filled with
questions.explanation: The e/m ratio is charge by mass ratio. Anode rays are the positively charged nuclei of the element in the discharge tube. It is a general observation that as the molecular mass of the gas increases its e/m ratio decreases. In the case of anode rays maximum value for the e/m ratio is observed in the case of Hydrogen gas (H2). The reason is that Hydrogen is the only element that does not carry any neutron. In anode ray discharge it carries 1 positive charge and mass is also 1. So the e/m ratio is 1. But for other gases as the molecular mass of the gas increases, the number of neutrons also increases so charge(number of protons) by mass (number of proton + number of the neutron) does not remain 1 but it becomes less than because mass is always greater than charge except for hydrogen.

questions.question: When a gold sheet is bombarded by a beam of {tex}\alpha{/tex}-particles, only a few of them got deflected whereas most go straight, undeflected. This is because

options.option: the volume of nucleus is smaller than that of atom.
the force of attraction exerted on α- particles by electrons is insufficient.
the force of repulsion acting on fast moving α- particle is very small.

questions.explanation: A small fraction of {tex}\alpha{/tex}-particles were deflected through small angles and a few through larger angles. Some of the {tex}\alpha{/tex}-particles suffered deflection by 90 or even larger angels. For this to happen, {tex}\alpha{/tex}-particles (positively charged) must approach a heavy positively charged core inside the atom (like charges repel each other). This heavy positively charged core inside the atom was named as the nucleus. Since the number of α- particles that bounced back was very small, hence, the volume occupied by the nucleus is very small as compared to the total volume of the atom.

questions.question: Which of the following statements does not belong to Bohr's model?

options.option: Energy of the electrons in the orbit is quantized, i.e., they have fixed energies.
Electrons revolve around the nucleus in different orbits having fixed energies.
The electrons radiate energy during revolution due to force of attraction between nucleus and electrons.
The electron in the orbit nearest to the nucleus is in lowest energy state and farthest away from nucleus is in highest energy state.

questions.explanation:
Postulates of Bohr’s Model of an Atom:

  1. In an atom, electrons (negatively charged) revolve around the positively charged nucleus in a definite circular path called orbits or shells.
  2. Each orbit or shell has fixed energy and these circular orbits are known as orbital shells.
  3. The energy levels are represented by an integer (n = 1, 2, 3,…) known as a quantum number. This range of quantum numbers starts from the nucleus side with n = 1 having the lowest energy level. The orbits n = 1, 2, 3, 4, … are assigned as K, L, M, N, …. shells and when an electron attains the lowest energy level it is said to be in the ground state.
  4. The electrons in an atom move from lower energy level to higher energy level by gaining the required energy and an electron moves from a higher energy level to lower energy level by losing energy.
    The electrons radiate energy during revolution due to the force of attraction between nucleus and electrons this statement does not belong to Bohr's model.

questions.question: How many electrons, protons and neutrons will be present in X –, if atomic number of X is 9 and mass number is 19?
questions.explanation:
Atomic number = 9
Mass number = 19
No of electron = No. of neutrons = 19 – 9 = 10
No. of proton = 19 – 10 = 9

questions.question: Which of the following are isobars?
questions.explanation: Atoms having the same atomic mass and different atomic numbers are known as Isobars. {tex}_{18}^{40}Ar\;,\;_{20}^{40}Ca{/tex}. Both the elements have the same atomic mass equal to 40 but different atomic numbers, i.e. argon has an atomic number equal to 18 and calcium has an atomic number equal to 20.

questions.question: Rutherford's {tex}\alpha{/tex}- scattering experiment led to the conclusion that

options.option: the mass and the positive charge of an atom are concentrated in the nucleus.

questions.explanation:
The Rutherford's Model of an Atom is as follows:

  1. Most of the part in an atom is empty.
  2. There is a positively charged center in an atom, which contains nearly the whole mass of the atom. The center is called the nucleus.
  3. The size of the nucleus is very small compared to an atom.
  4. Electrons revolve around the nucleus.

questions.question: Which of the following reactions led to the discovery of the neutron?
questions.explanation:
In 1932 J. Chadwick discovered another subatomic particle called the neutron. Neutron is present in the nucleus of all atoms. And the reaction is given below.
{tex}{}_4^9Be\;\;+\;{}_2^4He\;\rightarrow{}_6^{12}C\;+{}_0^1n{/tex}

questions.question: Which of the following pairs have identical values of charge/mass?
questions.explanation: Deuterium and an {tex}\alpha{/tex} - alpha particle has identical e/m ratio. They both emit and absorb at the same frequencies. The e/m ratio of an alpha particle is 4.82245111 {tex}\times{/tex} 107 C/kg which is identical with deuterium.

questions.question: If K, L, M, N, shells of an atom are full. The total number of electrons in that atom are:
questions.explanation:
Maximum number of electrons in K-shell i.e. 1st shell = 2
Maximum number of electrons in L-shell = 8
Maximum number of electrons in M-shell = 18
Maximum number of electrons in N-shell = 32
2 + 8 + 18 + 32 = 60

questions.question: The difference between isotopes and isobars

options.option: Isotopes have same chemical properties but isobars have different.
Isotopes have same physical properties but isobars have different.
Isobars have same chemical properties but isotopes have different chemical proerties.

questions.explanation: Isotope compounds have the same number of electrons. Isotopes have the same chemical properties as the number of electrons present in them are the same because only electrons participate in chemical reactions. While isobar compounds have the same mass number but the different atomic numbers and chemical properties depend upon the number of electrons so isobars have different chemical properties.

questions.question: Which of the following correctly represent the electronic distribution in the Mg atom?
questions.explanation:
Atomic number of magnesium = 12
Therefore number of electrons = 12
Thus, electronic configuration of magnesium is Magnesium = 2,8,2.

questions.question: The number of electrons in an element x is 15 and the number of neutrons is 16. Which of the following is the correct representation of the element ?
questions.explanation:
An element that contains 15 electrons is Phosphours(P).
Now mass of this element = No. of electrons = No. of neutrons = 15 = 16 = 31.
So, the representation of the element is {tex}{}_{15}^{31}X{/tex} .

questions.question: In a sample of ethyl ethanoate (CH3COOC2H5) the two oxygen atoms have the same number electrons but different number of neutrons. Which of the following is the correct reason for it ?

options.option: One of the oxygen atom has gained neutrons
One of the oxygen atom has gained electrons

questions.explanation:
The two oxygen atoms have the same number electrons but different number of neutrons. So, two oxygen atom have same atomic number but different atomic masses. Elements having same atomic number but different atomic masses are known as Isotopes.
So, the two oxygen atoms are isotopes is true statement.

questions.question: The first model of an atom was given by
questions.explanation: J.J Thomson was the first one to give atom's model. Which is also called plum pudding model/ watermelon model/ Raisin model as in his atom's structure he said that atom is a positively charged sphere with negative charge embedded in it like black seeds embedded in watermelon.

questions.question: The electron distribution in an aluminium atom is
questions.explanation:
Atomic number of aluminium = 13.
Therefore number of electrons = 13.
Thus, electronic configuration of aluminium is (K = 2, L = 8, M = 3)

questions.question: The fundamental particle not present in hydrogen is ________.
questions.explanation: In nucleus of an atom, protons and neutrons are present. But in hydrogen atom, neutrons are not present, only 1 proton is present.

questions.question: In neutral atoms, number of electrons are equal to number of ________.
questions.explanation:
1. The number of protons in the nucleus of the atom is equal to the atomic number (Z).
  2. The number of electrons in a neutral atom is equal to the number of protons.
  3. The mass number of the atom (M) is equal to the sum of the number of protons and neutrons in the nucleus.
  4. The number of neutrons is equal to the difference between the mass number of the atom (M) and the atomic number (Z).

questions.question:
Dalton’s atomic theory successfully explained

  1. Law of conservation of mass
  2. Law of constant composition
  3. Law of radioactivity
  4. Law of multiple proportions
questions.explanation: Dalton's theory explained the law of conservation of mass, the law of constant composition, and the law of multiple proportions. Dalton's theory did not talk about any law of radioactivity.

questions.question: The ion of an element has 3 positive charges. Mass number of the atom is 27 and the number of neutrons is 14. What is the number of electrons in the ion?
questions.explanation:
Mass number (A) of the element = 27
Number of neutrons in the atom = 14
Hence, the number of electrons in the atom
= Mass number (A) - number of neutrons in the atom
= 27 - 14 = 13
Since the ion of the element has 3 positive charges, so the number of electrons in the ion is 13 - 3 = 10.
The number of electrons in the ion is 10.

questions.question: Rutherford's alpha-particle scattering experiment discovered
questions.explanation: Atomic nucleus

questions.question: Number of valence electrons in O2- is:
Which of the following is the correct electronic configuration of potassium?
Which of these is noble gas?
questions.explanation: Helium

questions.question: Electrons were discovered by
questions.explanation: J. J. Thomson

questions.question: The electronic configuration of magnesium is
Which of the following represents the correct composition of the three isotopes of carbon?
questions.explanation:
Isotopes are the atoms of the same element, having same atomic number but different mass numbers. For a neutral atom,
Atomic number = Number of protons = Number of electrons
Mass number = Number of protons + Number of neutrons.
As the mass number of isotopes are 12, 13 and 14 so, number of neutrons will be
12 - 6 = 6, 13 - 6 = 7 and 14 - 6 = 8 in C - 12, C - 13 and C - 14 respectively.

questions.question:
A thin sheet of gold foil is bombarded with {tex}\alpha{/tex}-particles as in Rutherford’s experiment. Which of the given descriptions most accurately represents the path of {tex}\alpha{/tex}-particles?

  Undeflected No. of particles deflected through a small angle No. of particles deflected through a large angle
I All None None
II Most A few None
III Most A few A few
IV A few Most A few
questions.explanation: III option is correct as most of the alpha particles are undeflected while a few alpha particles are deflected through small and large angles.

questions.question: Which of the following schematic structures represent(s) cation(s)?
questions.explanation:
Structure I shows neutral atom having equal number of protons and electrons.
Structure II represents an anion which has more number of electrons than protons while structures III and IV represent cations having more number of protons than electrons.

questions.question:
There are two atomic species X and Y, such that

Atomic species X Y
Protons 8 8
Protons 8 10

Which of the following statements is true about X and Y?

options.option: X and Y have different chemical properties.
X and Y have different physical properties.

questions.explanation: X and Y have same number of protons but different number of neutrons i.e., same atomic number but different mass numbers hence, these are isotopes. Isotopes have same chemical properties but different physical properties.

questions.question:
A brief information about two atoms X and Y is given:
X: Atomic number = 7, Mass number = 14
Y: Atomic number = 7, Mass number = 15
Which of the following is correct about these two atoms?

options.option: X has 2 electron shells while Y has 3 electron shells.
Electronic configuration of X is 2, 8, 4 while that of Y is 2, 8, 5.
Both X and Y have 5 valence electrons.

questions.explanation: X and Y are isotopes having electronic configuration as 2, 5. Hence, both have five valence electrons.

questions.question:
Statement 1: Isobars have different chemical properties.
Statement 2: The sum of protons and neutrons in isobars is always different.

options.option: Statement 1 is true and statement 2 is false.
Both statements 1 and 2 are true and statement 2 is the correct explanation of statement 1.
Both statements 1 and 2 are true but statement 2 is not the correct explanation of statement 1.

questions.explanation: Sum of protons and neutrons in isobars is same.

questions.question: The average atomic mass of boron is 10.8 u. If the isotopic mass of B-10 is 10.013 and that of B-11 is 11.009, then the relative abundance of these isotopes is respectively
questions.explanation:
10.8 = {tex}\frac{10.013 x+(100-x) 11.009}{100}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 1080 = 10.013x + 1100.9 - 11.009x
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 20.98%
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Abundance of B-10 = 20.98%
Abundance of B-11 = 100 - 20.98 = 79.02%

questions.question:
Which of the following statements are incorrect regarding the elements given below?
{tex}^{39}_{19}U{/tex}, {tex}^{127}_{53}V{/tex}, {tex}^{35}_{17}W{/tex}, {tex}^{80}_{35}X{/tex}, {tex}^{40}_{18}Y{/tex}

  1. The correct order of increasing proton number is U < W < X < V < Y.
  2. The correct order of increasing mass number is W < U < Y < X < V.
  3. There is difference in the orders of proton number and mass number.
  4. The number of protons is equal to number of neutrons in all the given elements.
questions.explanation:
Atomic number = Number of protons

Mass number = Number of protons + Number of neutrons

questions.question: Hydrogen exists in three isotopic forms, {tex}{ }_1^1 \mathrm{H},{ }_1^2 \mathrm{H},{ }_1^3 \mathrm{H}{/tex} respectively known as protium, deuterium and tritium. Why are all the isotopes neutral in nature?

options.option: All the isotopes have one electron and one proton, hence they are neutral.
All the isotopes contain same number of neutrons hence, they are electrically neutral.
All the isotopes have one proton and one neutron, hence they are neutral.
Equal number of protons and neutrons in the isotopes make them neutral.

questions.explanation: Isotopes of hydrogen are neutral due to presence of one proton and one electron.

questions.question: A compound of carbon, hydrogen and nitrogen contains these elements in the ratio 9 : 1 : 3.5. If its molecular mass is 108, what is the molecular formula?
questions.explanation:
Ratio of elements C, H and N = 9 : 1 : 3.5
Whole number ratio of elements C, H and N = 18 : 2 : 7

Element Ratio Atomic mass Atomic ratio Simplest atomic ratio
C 18 12 {tex}\frac {18}{12}{/tex} = 1.5  1.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 = 3
H 2 1 {tex}\frac 21{/tex} = 2 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 = 4
N 7 14 {tex}\frac 7{14}{/tex} = 0.5 0.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 = 1

Thus, empirical formula of the compound = C3H4N Empirical formula mass of the compound
= 12 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 + 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 4 + 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 14 = 36 + 4 + 14 = 54
n = {tex}\frac{\text { Molecular mass }}{\text { Empirical formula mass }}{/tex} = {tex}\frac {108}{54}{/tex} = 2
Molecular formula = n {tex}\times{/tex} empirical formula
= (C3H4N)2 = C6H8N2

questions.question:
Which of the given pairs of atoms contain(s) the same number of neutrons?

  1. {tex}^{114}_{48}Cd{/tex} and {tex}^{119}_{50}Sn{/tex}
  2. {tex}^{59}_{27}Co{/tex} and {tex}^{59}_{28}Ni{/tex}
  3. {tex}^{113}_{55}Cs{/tex} and {tex}^{132}_{54}Xe{/tex}
  4. {tex}^{63}_{29}Cu{/tex} and {tex}^{65}_{29}Cu{/tex}
questions.explanation:
Number of neutrons in
Cd = 66, Sn = 69; Co = 32, Ni = 31
Cs = 78, Xe = 78; 63Cu = 34 , 65Cu = 36

questions.question:
Study the given mass spectrum of magnesium carefully.

The number of protons in 26Mg, number of neutrons in 25Mg, and the relative atomic mass of Mg are respectively
questions.explanation:
No. of protons in 26Mg = 12
No. of neutrons in 25Mg = 13
Relative atomic mass of Mg
= {tex}\frac{(24 \times 78.99)+(25 \times 10)+(26 \times 11.01)}{100}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{1895.76+250+286.26}{100}{/tex} = 24.32

questions.question:
Electron distribution of two elements P and Q in their outermost shell is shown below.

Atomic numbers of P and Q are respectively
questions.explanation:
Electronic configuration of P:
{tex}\begin{array}{llll} K & L & M & N \\ 2 & 8 & 8 & 6 \end{array}{/tex}
So, its atomic number = 24
Electronic configuration of Q:
{tex}\begin{array}{lll} K & L & M \\ 2 & 8 & 7 \end{array}{/tex}
So, its atomic number = 17

subtopics.name: Rediscovering the Roots of Atomic Theory

questions.question: Which shell must be complete before the L-shell can be filled?
questions.explanation: The L-shell will be filled only after the K-shell is complete.

questions.question: In what order are electrons filled in atomic shells?
questions.explanation: Electrons are filled in shells in a stepwise manner, starting from the one closest to the nucleus and moving outward, i.e., in the order K, L, M, N, ....

questions.question: What is the maximum number of electrons that can be accommodated in the M-shell?
questions.explanation: For the M-shell (n=3), the maximum number of electrons is 2 * 32 = 18.

questions.question: What is the maximum number of electrons that can be accommodated in the L-shell?
questions.explanation: For the L-shell (n=2), the maximum number of electrons is 2 * 22 = 8.

questions.question: What is the maximum number of electrons that can be accommodated in the K-shell?
questions.explanation: The formula 2n2 dictates that for the K-shell (n=1), the maximum number of electrons is 2 * 12 = 2.

subtopics.name: A Short Historical Journey Through Atomic Models

questions.question: An atom has 2 protons and 2 neutrons. What is its mass number?
questions.explanation: Mass number = Number of protons + Number of neutrons = 2 + 2 = 4.

questions.question: How do atoms of different elements fundamentally differ from each other?
questions.explanation: It is stated that atoms of different elements differ in the number of electrons and protons in them.

questions.question: What is the collective name for protons and neutrons found within the nucleus of an atom?
questions.explanation: Protons and neutrons present in the nucleus are collectively called nucleons.

questions.question: If an atom is electrically neutral, how does the number of protons relate to the number of electrons?
questions.explanation: For an atom to be neutral, the positive charge from protons must cancel out the negative charge from electrons, meaning their numbers must be equal.

questions.question: Why do scientists use symbols instead of full names for elements?

options.option: Because they are internationally recognised and allow clear communication regardless of language barriers
To make understanding difficult for common people

questions.explanation: Scientists use symbols because they are internationally recognised and allow scientists worldwide to communicate clearly, regardless of language barriers.

questions.question: Which of the following represents the chemical symbol for iron?
questions.explanation: The symbol for iron is Fe, which comes from its Latin name 'ferrum'.

questions.question: What is the correct way to write the symbol for Aluminium?
questions.explanation: Aluminium is Al.

questions.question: Which organization currently approves the names and symbols of chemical elements?

options.option: International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry (IUPAC)
International Union of Physical and Chemical Sciences (IUPCS)

questions.explanation: The International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry (IUPAC) approves the names and symbols of elements.

questions.question: Who introduced the first pictorial symbols to represent elements?
questions.explanation: John Dalton introduced the first pictorial symbols to represent known elements in 1803.

questions.question: What is the symbol for a neutron?
questions.explanation: The symbol for a neutron is n⁰, representing its neutral charge.

questions.question: What is the relative charge of a neutron?
questions.explanation: The neutron is a neutral particle, meaning it has no electrical charge.

questions.question: Who discovered the neutron in 1932?
questions.explanation: James Chadwick, a student of Ernest Rutherford, discovered the neutron in 1932.

questions.question: Which subatomic particle has a mass nearly equal to that of a proton but no electrical charge?
questions.explanation: Neutrons were discovered as neutral particles with a mass nearly equal to that of a proton.

questions.question: In Bohr's model of the atom, what are the fixed circular paths in which electrons revolve called?
questions.explanation: Bohr proposed that electrons move in fixed circular paths called stationary states, orbits, or shells, also known as energy levels.

questions.question: Which subatomic particle was discovered and named by Rutherford?
questions.explanation: Rutherford discovered and named the proton, which carries the positive charge in the nucleus.

questions.question: What is the approximate diameter of an atom as calculated by Rutherford?
questions.explanation: Rutherford calculated the diameter of an atom to be approximately 10-10 m.

questions.question: What was Rutherford's conclusion about the positive charge of an atom based on the gold foil experiment?
questions.explanation: Rutherford concluded that the positive charge and most of the mass of an atom are concentrated in an extremely small, dense region called the nucleus.

questions.question: What kind of particles were aimed at the gold foil during the gold foil experiment?
questions.explanation: The gold foil experiment utilized a narrow beam of positively charged alpha particles.

questions.question: Which experiment was performed by Geiger and Marsden under Ernest Rutherford to test Thomson's atomic model?
questions.explanation: The gold foil experiment, also known as the α-ray scattering experiment, was conducted by Geiger and Marsden under Rutherford to test Thomson's model.

questions.question: Which scientist discovered electrons by studying the conduction of electric current through gases at low pressure?
questions.explanation: J. J. Thomson is credited with the discovery of electrons in 1897 through his experiments with cathode rays.

questions.question: Which of the following phenomena provided the first evidence that atoms are not indivisible and are actually made up of smaller subatomic particles?
questions.explanation: The discovery of radioactivity showed that certain atoms naturally break apart, emitting energy and subatomic particles. This proved that atoms have an internal structure and are composed of even smaller constituents, like protons, neutrons, and electrons, rather than being solid, indivisible spheres.

questions.question: What is the significance of the atomic number (Z) for an element?
questions.explanation: The atomic number, designated by the symbol Z, determines the identity of an element and its chemical behavior.

subtopics.name: What Components Contribute to the Mass of an Atom?

questions.question: Which subatomic particle's quantity within the nucleus defines an element's atomic number?
questions.explanation: The number of protons in the nucleus of an atom is defined as its atomic number.

questions.question: What characteristic uniquely distinguishes the atoms of one element from the atoms of another element?
questions.explanation: The identity of an element is determined by its number of protons, called the atomic number. Neutrons contribute to the atom’s mass and isotopic form. Together, the numbers of protons and neutrons define the nucleus, distinguishing atoms of different elements and isotopes.

questions.question: What is the correct way to write the symbol for the element Cobalt, according to IUPAC norms?
questions.explanation: The first letter of a symbol is always uppercase, and the second letter (if present) is lowercase. Hence, Cobalt is 'Co', not 'CO'.

questions.question: Which scientist proposed that the symbols of chemical elements should be based on their Latin names?
questions.explanation: Jöns Jacob Berzelius proposed using one or two letters from the Latin names of elements as their symbols. This system was simpler and more practical than Dalton’s pictorial symbols and became the foundation of the modern chemical notation used worldwide today.

questions.question: In which year did John Dalton first introduce his pictorial symbols to represent chemical elements?
questions.explanation: John Dalton introduced his first pictorial symbols for chemical elements in 1803. These symbols were used to represent atoms and compounds visually, supporting his atomic theory. Although later replaced by letter-based symbols, Dalton’s system was the first modern symbolic representation of elements.

questions.question: According to John Dalton’s early system of pictorial symbols for elements, which element was represented by a circle containing a plus sign (+)?
questions.explanation: John Dalton used unique pictorial symbols to represent elements before modern chemical symbols were adopted. In his system, sulphur was represented by a circle enclosing a plus sign. These symbols helped visualize atoms and compounds but were later replaced by simpler letter-based notation.

questions.question: What was the initial puzzling observation about atomic mass that led to the search for an uncharged particle in the nucleus?

options.option: Helium was four times heavier than hydrogen.

questions.explanation: Scientists expected helium, with two protons, to have roughly twice the mass of hydrogen. Instead, its mass was about four times greater. This discrepancy suggested the presence of additional massive particles in the nucleus, leading to the discovery of the neutron.

questions.question: How did the discovery of the neutron contribute to the 'atomic age'?
questions.explanation: The discovery of the neutron made it possible to initiate nuclear reactions more effectively. Scientists could create artificial radioactive elements and induce uranium fission. These developments led to nuclear power generation, medical applications of radioisotopes, and the development of nuclear weapons.

questions.question: Which element has a nucleus that contains only a proton and no neutrons?
questions.explanation: The most common isotope of hydrogen, called protium, has a nucleus containing only one proton and no neutrons. This makes hydrogen the simplest element. Helium, carbon, and uranium nuclei all contain both protons and neutrons, making them more complex.

questions.question: Which subatomic particle is represented by the symbol e⁻ and carries a relative electrical charge of −1?
questions.explanation: The electron is a negatively charged subatomic particle represented by the symbol e⁻. It has a relative charge of −1 and is located outside the nucleus. Protons carry a +1 charge, neutrons are neutral, and positrons have a +1 charge.

questions.question: In which subatomic particle is the mass of an atom primarily concentrated, according to Rutherford's model?
questions.explanation: Rutherford's model showed that most of the mass of an atom is concentrated in its nucleus.

questions.question: Suppose that in Bohr’s atomic model, electrons could move randomly between energy levels without absorbing or emitting specific amounts of energy. What effect would this have on the emission spectrum of an element?
questions.explanation: Bohr’s model explains spectral lines through fixed energy levels and specific energy transitions. If electrons could jump randomly between levels, photons of any energy could be emitted. This would produce a continuous spectrum instead of the characteristic discrete emission lines observed experimentally.

questions.question: According to Rutherford’s model, if an atom were enlarged to the size of a cricket ground (about 100 m in diameter) and the nucleus were represented by a tiny black pepper grain at the center, what would be the approximate ratio of the atom’s diameter to the nucleus’s diameter?
questions.explanation: Rutherford’s model shows that the nucleus is extremely small compared to the atom. The atomic radius is about 10-10m, while the nuclear radius is about 10-15m. Thus, the atom’s diameter is approximately 100,000 times greater than that of its nucleus.

questions.question: In Thomson’s atomic model, if the total positive charge of the atom were less than the total negative charge of the embedded electrons, would the atom still be electrically neutral?
questions.explanation: An atom is neutral only when its total positive and negative charges are equal. If the positive charge is less than the negative charge contributed by electrons, the charges do not balance. Consequently, the atom would possess an overall negative electrical charge.

questions.question: In Rutherford’s gold foil experiment, suppose negatively charged particles were used instead of alpha particles. Based on Thomson’s atomic model, what observation would scientists most likely expect?
questions.explanation: According to Thomson’s model, positive charge is spread uniformly throughout the atom. Negatively charged particles would therefore experience a weak attractive force rather than strong deflection. Scientists would expect most particles to pass through the foil with only minor deviations from their paths.

questions.question: What would most likely happen to the alpha particles in Rutherford’s gold foil experiment if a much thicker gold foil had been used instead of a thin one?
questions.explanation: A thicker gold foil contains more atoms, increasing the chances of alpha particles interacting with atomic nuclei. As a result, more particles would experience strong repulsive forces, causing greater deflections and increasing the number of particles scattered backward or reflected.

questions.question: Why did scientists name the electron shells in Bohr’s atomic model K, L, M, N, etc., instead of starting with A, B, C, D?
questions.explanation: The shell names originated from X-ray spectroscopy studies by physicists. Scientists began with the letter K because they were unsure whether lower-energy series existed. Starting at K left room for possible earlier discoveries, although no such series were later found.

questions.question: What did Niels Bohr introduce to explain why electrons in his model do not lose energy and spiral into the nucleus, unlike in Rutherford's model?

options.option: The concept of a neutron to balance forces.

questions.explanation: Bohr addressed this by introducing the concept of stationary states as a postulate. In a stationary state, the energy of an electron remains constant, even though it is in motion around the nucleus.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a postulate of Bohr's model of the atom?
questions.explanation: Bohr's model states, 'Electrons do not move randomly around the nucleus but follow fixed circular paths called stationary states, orbits, or shells.' This contradicts random movement.

questions.question: What condition must be met for an atom to be electrically neutral?
questions.explanation: For an atom to be electrically neutral, the number of protons must be equal to the number of electrons.

questions.question: According to Rutherford, what particles contribute to the positive charge of the nucleus, and what is their charge relative to electrons?
questions.explanation: Rutherford showed that the nucleus carries positive charge which comes from the particles called protons. Protons are much heavier than electrons and possess a charge equal and opposite to that of electrons.

questions.question: Why would an electron orbiting the nucleus in Rutherford's model theoretically cause an atom to collapse?
questions.explanation: In Rutherford’s model, electrons move around the nucleus in circular paths. Classical physics states that accelerating charged particles emit energy. As electrons lose energy, their orbits shrink, causing them to spiral into the nucleus and collapse the atom.

questions.question: Which limitation of Rutherford’s atomic model remained unexplained even though it was an improvement over Thomson’s model?
questions.explanation: Rutherford’s model proposed that electrons revolve around a central nucleus. According to classical physics, moving electrons should continuously lose energy and spiral into the nucleus. Therefore, the model could not explain why atoms remain stable and do not collapse.

questions.question: What was the expected outcome of the gold foil experiment according to Thomson's model of the atom?
questions.explanation: According to Thomson's model, the positive charge in the atom was spread out evenly. So they expected the alpha particles to pass straight through the gold foil or be deflected only slightly.

questions.question: In J.J. Thomson's 1897 experiments on electric current conduction through gases, what was the nature of the particles that formed the cathode rays?
questions.explanation: Thomson concluded the cathode rays were 'streams of negatively charged particles, with a much smaller mass than atoms'. These particles were later identified as electrons.

subtopics.name: How Are Electrons Distributed in Different Energy Levels?

questions.question: What was the primary contribution of Dalton’s atomic theory to the scientific understanding of matter?
questions.explanation: Dalton’s atomic theory (1803) was the first to combine existing laws of chemical combination into a coherent model. It proposed that all matter is composed of indivisible, unique atoms, establishing the foundation for modern chemistry.

questions.question: What served as the primary foundation for the development of John Dalton’s atomic theory?
questions.explanation: Unlike his predecessors who relied on speculation, Dalton formulated his theory by synthesizing experimental data, specifically the laws of chemical combination (such as the law of conservation of mass and definite proportions) observed in laboratory studies.

questions.question: When did John Dalton propose his atomic theory?
questions.explanation: Many centuries later, in 1808, John Dalton proposed his atomic theory.

questions.question: What is the meaning of 'atomos' in Greek, as used by Leucippus and Democritus?
questions.explanation: atomos (in Greek atomos means indivisible).

questions.question: What did Acharya Kanada call the smallest, indivisible particles of matter?
questions.explanation: Acharya Kanada called the smallest particles that can no longer be divided 'parmanus'.

subtopics.name: Combining Capacity of an Atom: Valency

questions.question: Why would an atomic mass expressed in a unified atomic mass unit (u) be more practical for measuring atoms than in kilograms (kg)?
questions.explanation: The atoms are 'too tiny to be weighed in kilograms or grams like everyday objects' and that 'a kilogram is too large for such small particles.' The unified atomic mass unit (u) provides a more appropriately scaled and convenient measure for the extremely small masses of atoms.

questions.question: If an atom has the same mass number as another atom but a different atomic number, what conclusion can be drawn about these two atoms?
questions.explanation: Atoms with the same mass number but different atomic numbers are called isobars. This means they are atoms of different elements (different atomic number) but have the same total count of protons and neutrons (mass number = total nucleons).

questions.question: Why is a weighted average atomic mass preferred over a simple arithmetic mean for elements with multiple isotopes?

options.option: The weighted average is only used for radioactive isotopes.
A simple mean is too complex to calculate for most elements.
Isotopes do not occur in equal ratios, making a simple mean inaccurate.
The simple mean only considers the lightest isotope.

questions.explanation: A weighted average atomic mass accounts for the natural abundances of each isotope, which are typically not equal. A simple arithmetic mean would imply equal abundance for all isotopes, leading to an inaccurate representation of the element's mass as it occurs in nature.

questions.question: The use of 14C in archaeology demonstrates which key aspect of isotopes?

options.option: Their distinct physical properties and decay rate.
Their identical chemical properties to other isotopes.
Their role as fuel in nuclear reactors.

questions.explanation: The application of 14C in archaeology (carbon dating) relies on its distinct physical property of radioactive decay at a measurable rate, allowing for age determination, which highlights a specific physical characteristic of this isotope.

questions.question: Consider an element with an average atomic mass of 24.31 u. It has two main isotopes: one with a mass of 24 u and another with a mass of 25 u. If the lighter isotope (24 u) has an abundance of 79%, what is the abundance of the heavier isotope (25 u)?
questions.explanation:
Let x be the abundance of the isotope with mass 24u and (1-x) be the abundance of the isotope with mass 25u.
{tex}24.31 = (24 \times 0.79) + (25 \times (1-0.79)){/tex}
{tex}24.31 = 18.96 + (25 \times 0.21){/tex}
{tex}24.31 = 18.96 + 5.25{/tex}
{tex}24.31 = 24.21{/tex}
This implies the heavier isotope (25 u) has an abundance of 21%.

questions.question: A bromine atom available in two isotopes, 79Br (49.7%) and 81Br (50.3%), has an average atomic mass of 79.99 u. Determine the approximate mass of each isotope used in this calculation to arrive at the given average atomic mass.

options.option: 79Br with mass 79.5 u, 81Br with mass 80.5 u
79Br with mass 79 u, 81Br with mass 81 u
79Br with mass 78 u, 81Br with mass 80 u
79Br with mass 80 u, 81Br with mass 82 u

questions.explanation: Given the natural abundances, the calculation is approximately: {tex}(79 \text{ u} \times 0.497) + (81 \text{ u} \times 0.503){/tex} {tex}39.263 + 40.743 = 80.006 \text{ u}{/tex} This value is very close to 79.99 u, indicating the masses used were 79 u and 81 u respectively.

questions.question: Two different atoms have 11 protons each. One has 12 neutrons, and the other has 13 neutrons. How do their atomic numbers and mass numbers compare?
questions.explanation: Both atoms have 11 protons, so their atomic number (Z) is the same (11). The first atom has 11 protons + 12 neutrons = 23 mass number. The second atom has 11 protons + 13 neutrons = 24 mass number. Thus, they have different mass numbers.

questions.question: If an element has two isotopes: Isotope P with a mass of 10 u (20% abundance) and Isotope Q with a mass of 12 u (80% abundance), determine the average atomic mass of the element.
questions.explanation:
The average atomic mass is calculated as: {tex}(10 \text{ u} \times 0.20) + (12 \text{ u} \times 0.80){/tex}
{tex}2.0 \text{ u} + 9.6 \text{ u} = 11.6 \text{ u}{/tex}

questions.question: A fictional element 'X' has two isotopes. Isotope A has a mass of 50 u and occurs at 60% abundance. Isotope B has a mass of 52 u and occurs at 40% abundance. Calculate the weighted average atomic mass of element 'X'.
questions.explanation: To calculate the weighted average atomic mass: {tex}(50 \text{ u} \times 0.60) + (52 \text{ u} \times 0.40){/tex} {tex}30 \text{ u} + 20.8 \text{ u} = 50.8 \text{ u}{/tex}

questions.question: What is the primary difference in physical properties among isotopes of the same element?
questions.explanation: While isotopes have similar chemical properties, they differ in physical properties such as boiling and melting points due to their different masses.

questions.question: Which of the following elements has three naturally occurring isotopes, 12C, 13C, and 14C, and all have six protons and six electrons?
questions.explanation: Carbon has isotopes 12C, 13C, and 14C, each with six protons and six electrons, differing in neutron count.

subtopics.name: A Deeper Look into Atomic Structure

questions.question: Which unit is used by scientists to measure the mass of atoms?
questions.explanation: Scientists use a special unit called the unified atomic mass unit (u) to measure the mass of atoms because kilograms are too large for such small particles.

questions.question: What is an isobar?

options.option: Atoms of same element, different mass numbers.

questions.explanation: Isobars are defined as atoms of different elements that have the same mass number but different atomic numbers.

questions.question: What is the primary use of 60Co?

options.option: To determine the age of archaeological artifacts.

questions.explanation: 60Co is a radioactive isotope of cobalt used in radiation treatment for cancer.

questions.question: Which of the following is an application of the isotope 235U?

options.option: Fuel in a nuclear reactor to generate electricity.

questions.explanation: 235U is being used as a fuel in a nuclear reactor to generate electricity.

questions.question: Why do isotopes of an element have similar chemical properties?
questions.explanation: Chemical properties depend mainly on the number of valence electrons. Since isotopes have the same number of electrons, they exhibit similar chemical properties.

questions.question: Which isotope of hydrogen has one proton and one neutron?
questions.explanation: Deuterium contains one proton and one neutron, giving it a mass number of 2.

questions.question: What is the mass number (A) of an atom?
questions.explanation: The mass number (A) is defined as the sum of protons (p+) and neutrons (n°) in an atom's nucleus.

questions.question: What is the atomic number (Z) of an atom?
questions.explanation: The atomic number (Z) refers to the number of protons in an atom's nucleus.

questions.question: Which characteristic defines isotopes?
questions.explanation: Isotopes are defined as 'twin atoms' with the same atomic number (number of protons) but different mass numbers (due to different numbers of neutrons).

questions.question: If scientists observe that atoms of the same element can have different numbers of neutrons, what fundamental property of the atom does this difference primarily affect?
questions.explanation: All atoms of an element have the same number of electrons and protons (atomic number), but can have different numbers of neutrons. This difference in neutron count for the same element results in variations in atomic mass and leads to the formation of isotopes.

questions.question: An unknown element 'X' readily forms a compound with hydrogen, and in this compound, one atom of 'X' combines with three atoms of hydrogen. Based on this information, what can be inferred about the combining capacity and general reactivity of element 'X'?

options.option: Combining capacity is one; usually loses electrons.
Combining capacity is two; noble gas element.
Combining capacity is three; incomplete valence shell.

questions.explanation: The number of atoms of hydrogen or chlorine with which one atom of an element can combine is its combining capacity. If one atom of 'X' combines with three atoms of hydrogen, then 'X' has a combining capacity of three. Elements with combining capacity (valency) greater than zero are reactive, implying an incomplete valence shell that allows them to form bonds.

questions.question: Carbon has an electronic configuration of 2, 4. What is its valency and how does it achieve an octet?
questions.explanation: Carbon has four valence electrons and cannot easily gain or lose them. Therefore, it can share four electrons with other atoms to complete its octet. Thus, the valency of carbon is 4.

questions.question: An element has 3 valence electrons. Does it tend to lose or gain electrons to complete its octet, and what is its likely valency?
questions.explanation: Generally, if an element has fewer than four electrons in its valence shell, it tends to lose electrons to complete its octet and become stable. Therefore, an element with 3 valence electrons would lose 3 electrons, making its valency 3.

questions.question: Oxygen has an electronic configuration of 2, 6. What is its valency?
questions.explanation: Oxygen has an electronic configuration of 2, 6. It has six valence electrons. Therefore, it can gain two electrons to attain an octet, and its valency is 2.

questions.question: Sodium has an electronic configuration of 2, 8, 1. What is its valency?
questions.explanation: Sodium has an electronic configuration of 2, 8, 1. It can get an octet by losing one electron. Therefore, its valency or combining capacity is 1.

questions.question: Atoms with incomplete valence shells tend to be:
questions.explanation: Atoms with incomplete valence shells are usually more reactive. Such elements lose, gain, or share electrons to complete their octet.

questions.question: What is defined as the number of electrons gained, lost, or shared to complete the octet of an element?
questions.explanation: The number of electrons gained, lost, or shared to complete the octet is called the valency of the element.

questions.question: What characteristic makes elements with complete octets (or two electrons for helium) largely unreactive and more stable?
questions.explanation: Elements with a complete octet of electrons (8 electrons), or 2 electrons in the case of helium, are largely unreactive and more stable.

questions.question: What is the combining capacity of an element primarily determined by?
questions.explanation: The combining capacity of an atom is decided by its electronic configuration.

questions.question: What is the term for the outermost shell containing electrons of an atom?
questions.explanation: The outermost shell containing electrons of an atom is known as its valence shell.

questions.question: Symbol of Iron is:-
questions.explanation: Symbol "Fe" for Iron has been derived from the Latin word Ferrum.

questions.question: Atomicity of Chlorine and Argon is:
questions.explanation: Atomicity is the total number of atoms present in one molecule of an element or a compound. Chlorine molecule (Cl2) is diatomic i.e. a molecule of chlorine contains 2 atoms of chlorine. Argon is an inert gas and an atom of Argon is capable of independent existence. Hence, Argon is monoatomic (Word "mono" refers to the single atom).

questions.question: The Molecular mass of water (H2O) is:
questions.explanation:
The molecular mass of H2O is calculated as the sum of the atomic masses of Hydrogen and Oxygen multiplied by the respective number of their atoms.
Therefore, the molecular mass of water (H2O) = 2 {tex}\times {/tex} Atomic mass of Hydrogen (H) + Atomic mass of Oxygen (O)
The molecular mass of water = (2 {tex}\times {/tex} 1 g) + 16 g
mass of water = 18 g.

questions.question: Atomic radius is measured in nanometers and:
questions.explanation:
Metre is a bigger unit (and the SI unit) of length. A nanometer is a smaller unit of length. Prefixes and suffixes are used before the unit to represent smaller and bigger units. Nano means 10-9 times. 
Therefore,1 nm = 10-9 m.

questions.question: 1 Mole of an element contains:
questions.explanation:
1 Mole of any entity contains units equal to the Avogadro number i.e. 6.023 {tex}\times {/tex} 1023. 
1 Mole of an element contains {tex}\times {/tex} 1023 atoms. 1 Mole of a compound will contain 6.023 {tex}\times {/tex} 1023 molecules.

questions.question: Oxygen is:
questions.explanation: Oxygen (with atomic number Z = 8) is a bivalent element. An atom of oxygen has 2 electrons in the innermost shell and 6 electrons in its outermost shell. It can accept 2 more electrons and complete an octet (8 electrons in the outermost shell).

questions.question: The molecular formula for Aluminum Chloride is:
questions.explanation: The correct molecular formula for Aluminum Chloride is AlCl3. Valency of Aluminium is +3 and that of Chlorine is -1. A chemical compound is always electrically neutral.

questions.question: Atomicity of fluorine is:
questions.explanation: The atomicity of an element is the number of atoms present in one molecule of the element. Atomicity of fluorine (F) will be the number of atoms present in one molecule of fluorine (F2).

questions.question: The molecular formula for calcium fluoride is:
questions.explanation: The molecular formula for calcium fluoride is CaF2. Valency of calcium is +2 and that of fluorine is -1.

questions.question: Nitrogen is:
questions.explanation: Nitrogen (N2) is diatomic. A nitrogen atom has 5 electrons in its outer shell. It forms three covalent bonds with another nitrogen atom and makes a nitrogen molecule.

questions.question: What is the molecular formula for Calcium Hydroxide?
questions.explanation: The molecular formula for Calcium Hydroxide is Ca(OH)2. The charge on calcium ion is 2+. Charge on [OH] radical is -1.

questions.question: The chemical formula of lead sulphate is
questions.explanation: PbSO4

questions.question: What is the ratio of magnesium and sulphur by mass in magnesium sulphide?
questions.explanation: The atomic mass of magnesium is 24. The atomic mass of sulphur is 32. Therefore, the ratio of magnesium and sulphur by mass in magnesium sulphide (MgS) is 24:32 or 3:4.

questions.question: Calculate the formula unit mass of ZnCl2? (nearest approximation)
questions.explanation:
Formula unit mass of ZnCl2 is (66 + 35.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 = 137u) 
The atomic mass of Zn is 66 and the atomic mass of Cl is 35.5

questions.question: The sample of water from a well is analysed. What will be the ratio of hydrogen and oxygen in it by mass?
questions.explanation: The atomic mass of Hydrogen is 1 u and that of Oxygen is 16 u. The formula of water is H2O. Hence, the ratio of Hydrogen and Oxygen by mass in water will be 2:16 or 1:8. The ratio will be the same irrespective of the source of the water.

questions.question:
What information do we get from the molecular formula?

  1. It represents one molecule of the substance.
  2. It does not tell the name of the substance.
  3. It tells about the type of atoms.
  4. It represents the formula mass unit of the substance.
questions.explanation: The molecular formula of a substance (an element or a compound) is a symbolic representation of the actual number of atoms present in one molecule of that substance. It represents the formula mass unit of the substance. It also conveys the name of the substance. Therefore, (a), (c) and (d) are correct.

questions.question: How many moles are present in 40 g of He?
questions.explanation: Number of moles of Helium = Mass of Helium / Molar mass of Helium = {tex}40 \over 4{/tex} = 10 moles. Atomic mass or molar mass of Helium is 4 u.

questions.question:
Which of the following elements are present in Quick lime?

  1. Calcium, Oxygen
  2. Sodium, Hydrogen, Oxygen
  3. Calcium, Bromine
  4. Calcium chloride
questions.explanation: The chemical formula of the Quick lime is CaO. So, Calcium (Ca) and Oxygen (O) elements are present in Quick lime.

questions.question: What is the formula of calcium phosphide?
questions.explanation: The valency of Calcium is 2 (Ca2+)and the valency of the Phosphide ion is 3 (P3-). A chemical compound is always electrically neutral; so the positive and negative valencies of the ions or radicals present in calcium phosphide should add up to zero. Therefore, the chemical formula of calcium phosphide is Ca3P2.

questions.question: What is the mass of 0.5 moles of a Hydrogen atom?
questions.explanation: The atomic mass of hydrogen is 1 u. Mass of one mole of Hydrogen is equal to 1 g. Therefore, the mass of 0.5 moles of Hydrogen atom will be 0.5 {tex}\times{/tex}1 = 0.5g

questions.question: How many atoms of Sulphur are present in 0.5 moles of SO2?
questions.explanation:
Each molecule SO2 contains one atom of Sulphur and two atoms of Oxygen.
So, 6.022 {tex}\times{/tex} 1023 molecules of SO2 will contain 6.022 {tex}\times{/tex} 1023 atoms of Sulphur.
6.022 {tex}\times{/tex} 1023 molecules of {tex}{\text{S}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}{/tex} make up one mole of SO2,
Since 1 mole of SO2 contains 6.022 {tex}\times{/tex} 1023 atoms of Sulphur,
Therefore, 0.5 moles of SO2 will contain  0.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 6.022 {tex}\times{/tex} 1023 atoms or 3.011 {tex}\times{/tex} 1023 atoms of Sulphur.

questions.question: How many moles are present in 2.3 g of Na?
questions.explanation:
The molecular mass of Sodium is 23 u. So, 23 g of Sodium (Na) is equal to 1 mole of Na.
Number of moles of Na present in 2.3 g of Na = Given mass of Na / Molar mass of Na = {tex}2.3 \over23{/tex} = 0.1 mole

questions.question: What is the name given to 6.022 {tex}\times{/tex} 1023?
questions.explanation: The fixed number of atoms or molecules present in the gram atomic mass of an atom or molecules is 6.022{tex}\times{/tex}1023. This number is known as Avogadro constant.

questions.question: Calculate the number of particles in 0.01 mole of carbon atoms.
questions.explanation:
The number of particles in one mole of carbon atom = 6.022 {tex}\times{/tex} 1023 atoms
Therefore, the number of particles present in 0.01 mole of carbon = 6.022 {tex}\times{/tex} 1023 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.01 = 6.022 {tex}\times{/tex} 1021 atoms.

questions.question: A sample of CaCO3 contains 3.01 {tex}\times{/tex} 1023 ions of Ca+2 and {tex}{\text{C}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{3}}}^{ - {\text{2}}}{/tex}. The mass of the sample is:
questions.explanation: The mass of one mole of CaCO3 is equal to 100 g. 6.022 {tex}\times{/tex} 1023 ions are equivalent to one mole. Therefore, mass of 3.01 {tex}\times{/tex} 1023 ions will be equivalent to ({tex}100 \over 2{/tex}) g or 50 g.

questions.question: What mass of CO2 will 3.011 {tex}\times{/tex} 1023 molecules contain?
questions.explanation:
The molecular mass of CO2 is 44 (12 + 26 {tex}\times{/tex} 2).
6.022 {tex}\times{/tex} 1023 molecules of carbon will contain 44 g.
Hence, 3.011 {tex}\times{/tex} 1023 molecules of CO2 will contain a mass of 22 g.

questions.question: Which of the following has the maximum number of molecules?
questions.explanation: 1 g of H2 (Molar mass 2 g) contains {tex}{\text{3}}.0{\text{11}} \times {\text{1}}{0^{{\text{23 }}}}{/tex}molecules, 22 g of {tex}{\text{C}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}{/tex} (Molar mass 44 g) contains {tex}{\text{3}}.0{\text{11}} \times {\text{1}}{0^{{\text{23 }}}}{/tex}molecules, 14 g of {tex}{{\text{N}}_{\text{2}}}{/tex}(Molar mass 28 g) contains {tex}{\text{3}}.0{\text{11}} \times {\text{1}}{0^{{\text{23 }}}}{/tex}molecules and 8 g of {tex}{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{4}}}{/tex}(Molar mass 16 g) contains {tex}{\text{3}}.0{\text{11}} \times {\text{1}}{0^{{\text{23 }}}}{/tex}molecules.

questions.question: Calculate the number of moles in 17 g of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2).
questions.explanation: The molecular mass of H2O2 is 34 u. So, 34 g of hydrogen peroxide will contain 1 mole. Therefore, 17 g of hydrogen peroxide will contain {tex}17\over34{/tex} moles or {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}mole.

questions.question: What weight of Calcium contains the same number of atoms as are present in 2g of Sulphur?
questions.explanation:
No. of moles present in 2g of Sulphur = {tex}\frac {2}{32}{/tex} = 0.0625 moles.
The atomic mass of Calcium = 40u.
Mass of Calcium containing 0.0625 moles of atoms = 0.0625 {tex}\times{/tex} 40 = 2.5 g

questions.question: Which has more molecules - 4g of methane or 11 g of CO2?

options.option: Both have the same number of moles.

questions.explanation:
4 g of methane will contain {tex}4\over16{/tex} moles of molecules = 0.25 moles
11 g of CO2 will contain {tex}11\over44{/tex} moles = 0.25 moles.
Hence, both will contain an equal number of moles and an equal number of molecules.

questions.question: Calculate the number of Mg atoms in 0.024 g of Mg,
questions.explanation:
Atomic mass of Mg is 24. Number of atoms in 24 g of Mg = NA = {tex}6.022 \times {10^{23}}{/tex}
Therefore, number of atoms in 0.024 g of Mg = {tex}\frac{{0.024}}{{24}} \times 6.022 \times {10^{23}}{/tex} = {tex}{\text{6}}.0{\text{22 }} \times {\text{ 1}}{0^{{\text{2}}0}}{/tex}

questions.question: How many molecules are present in one gram molecular mass of a substance?
questions.explanation:
Gram molecular mass is the mass in grams of one mole of a molecular substance. 
One mole contains  6.022 {tex}\times{/tex} 1023 molecules. This is known as the Avogadro number.
Therefore, one gram molecular mass of a substance has 6.022 {tex}\times{/tex} 1023 molecules.

questions.question: The atomic mass of calcium is 40. The number of moles in 60 g of Calcium are:
questions.explanation: Number of moles in 60 g of Calcium = {tex}Given\ mass\ of\ Calcium \over Atomic \ mass \ of \ Calcium {/tex}= {tex}60\over40{/tex} = 1.5 mol

questions.question: The number of molecules in CuSO4.5H2O bonded by H-bond is
questions.explanation: One molecule of water is linked by H-bond because only one molecule of water is present outside the coordination sphere.

questions.question: The formula for quicklime is
questions.explanation:
The formula for quick lime is CaO.
The Thermal decomposition of calcium carbonate gives calcium oxide which is also called as quick lime along with carbon dioxide.
CaCO3 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} CaO + CO2

questions.question: We can test given sample of water with  the help of
questions.explanation:
The reaction between anhydrous copper sulfate and water is reversible:
hydrated copper sulfate (blue) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} anhydrous copper sulfate (white) + water

questions.question: Why CuSO4 (anhydrous) is dirty white?

options.option: It does not absorb light from visible region

questions.explanation:
It does not absorb light from the visible region and does not radiate blue colour.
CuSO4 is a white amorphous solid. Copper sulphate compound obtains blue colour only when it gets exposed to moisture. Each copper sulphate molecule aggregates with 5 molecules of water (water of crystallization) to form blue colour crystals, commonly known as blue vitriol.

questions.question: How many molecules of H2O in CuSO4.5H2O are linked with coordinate bond?
questions.explanation: In CuSO4.5H2O, four water molecules are coordinated to Cu+2, while one is hydrogen bonded with SO42-.

questions.question:
Which of the following correctly represents 360 g of water?

  1. 2 moles of H20
  2. 20 moles of water
  3. 6.022 {tex}\times{/tex} 1023 molecules of water
  4. 1.2044 {tex}\times{/tex} 1025 molecules of water
questions.explanation:
(b) and (d) correctly represent 360g of water.
We know that,
(b) 1 mole of water = molar mass of water = 18g
Thus, 20 moles of water = 18g {tex}\times{/tex} 20 = 360g
(d) 1 mole of water = 6.022 {tex}\times{/tex} 1023 molecules of water = 18g of water
Thus, 1.2044 {tex}\times{/tex} 1025 molecules of water {tex}=\frac{18 \times 1.2044 \times 10^{25}}{6.022 \times 10^{22}}{/tex}= 360 g

questions.question: The chemical symbol for nitrogen gas is
questions.explanation:
• Nitrogen exists as nitrogen gas. It is a diatomic molecule. Therefore, it exists as N2​. The chemical symbol for the element nitrogen is N. 
  • Ni is the chemical symbol for a nickel. 
  • N+ is for nitrogen ion.

questions.question: The chemical symbol for sodium is
questions.explanation: Sodium is a chemical element with the symbol Na (from Latin natrium) and the atomic number is 11.

questions.question: Which of the following would weigh the highest?
questions.explanation:
Weight = Number pf moles {tex}\times{/tex} molar mass.

  • 0.2 moles of source (c12H22O11) = 0.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 342 = 68.4 g
  • 2 moles of CO2 = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 44 = 88 g
  • 2 moles of CaCo3 = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 100 = 200 g
  • 10 moles of H20 = 10 {tex}\times{/tex} 8 = 80 g

questions.question: Which of the following has maximum number of atoms?
questions.explanation:
• 18 g of CH4 contains a maximum number of atoms.
    The Molar mass of CH4 = The atomic mass of C + 4 {tex}\times{/tex} the atomic mass of H
    = 12 + 4 = 16 g
    Number of atoms = 1 + 4 = 5
    Thus, Number of atoms = Number of atoms in the molecule {tex}\times\frac{\text { Mass of substance }}{\text { Molar mass }} \times{/tex} NA
    {tex}=5 \times \frac{18}{16} \times \mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{A}}{/tex}
    = 5.63 NA
  • The number of atoms in 18 g of H2O:
    Molar mass of H2O = 18 g; Number of atoms = 2 + 1 = 3
    {tex}=3 \times \frac{18}{18} \times \mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{A}}{/tex}
    = 3 NA
  • The number of atoms in 18 g of O2:
    Molar mass of O2 = 32 g; Number of atoms in O2 = 1 + 1 = 2
    {tex}=2 \times \frac{18}{32} \times \mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{A}}{/tex}
    = 1.12 NA
  • The number of atoms in 18 g of CO2:
    {tex}=3 \times \frac{18}{44} \times \mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{A}}{/tex}
    = 1.23 NA

questions.question: Mass of one atom of oxygen is
questions.explanation:
Mass of one atom of oxygen
{tex}=\frac{\text { Atomic mass }}{\mathrm{N}_A}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{16}{6.022 \times 10^{23}} g{/tex}
= 2.657 {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}10^{-26}{/tex} Kg

questions.question: 3.42 g of sucrose are dissolved in 18 g of water in a beaker. The number of oxygen atoms in the solution are
questions.explanation:
Number of moles of sucrose
{tex}\frac {3.42} {342}{/tex} = 0.01 mol
1 mole of sucrose (C12 H22 O11) = 11 {tex}\times{/tex} NA atoms of O
0.01 mol of C12 H22 O11 = 0.01 {tex}\times{/tex} 11 {tex}\times{/tex} NA
= 0.11 {tex}\times{/tex} NA atoms of O
Number of moles of water {tex}=\frac{18}{18}{/tex} = 1 mol
1 mol of water contains = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} NA atoms of O
Total number of oxygen atoms = 0.11 NA + 1.0 NA
= 1.11 NA
Number of oxygen atoms in solution = 1.11 NA
= 1.11 {tex}\times{/tex} 6.022 {tex}\times{/tex} 1023
= 6.68 {tex}\times{/tex} 1023

questions.question: A change in the physical state can be brought about

options.option: only when energy is given to the system
when energy is either given to, or taken out from the system
only when energy is taken out from the system

questions.explanation: A change in the physical state can be brought about when energy is either given to or taken out of the system. It is because energy change helps in changing the magnitude of attraction forces between the particles, thus helps in changing the physical states (j.e., solid, liquid, gas) of matter.

questions.question: Which of the following represents a correct chemical formula? Name it.
questions.explanation: BiPO4 is the correct formula, its name is Bismuth phosphate.

questions.question: What are a and b?

options.option: a = multiply by 22.4 L, b = divide by 22.4 L
a = divide by 22.4 L, b - multiply by 22.4 L
a = divide by 22.4 L, b = divide by 22.4 L
a = multiply by 22.4 L, b = multiply by 22.4 L

questions.explanation:
Volume of gas at STP = No. of moles {tex}\underbrace{\times 22.4 \mathrm{~L}}_a{/tex}
No. of moles = Volume of gas at STP {tex}\underbrace{\div 22.4 \mathrm{~L}}_b{/tex}

questions.question:
Which of the following statements is/are incorrect?

  1. 5 moles of H2 and 5 moles of O2 have the same mass in grams.
  2. 100 g of NH3 has less atoms than that present in 100 g of N2.
  3. 16 g of solid sulphur contains 3.76{tex}\times{/tex} 1023 molecules.
questions.explanation:
1. 5 moles of H2 = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 5 = 10 g
    5 moles of O2 = 32 {tex}\times{/tex} 5 = 160 g
    Hence, 5 moles of H2 and 5 moles of O2 do not have the same mass in grams.
  2. 100 g of NH3:
    17 g of NH3 = 6.023 {tex}\times{/tex} 1023 {tex}\times{/tex} 4 atoms
    100 g of NH3 = {tex}\frac{6.023 \times 10^{23} \times 4}{17} \times 100{/tex} = 141.71 {tex}\times{/tex} 1023 atoms
    100 g of N2 = {tex}\frac{2 \times 6.023 \times 10^{23}}{28} \times 100{/tex} = 43.02 {tex}\times{/tex} 1023 atoms
    Hence, 100 g of NH3 has more atoms than 100 g of N2.
  3. 8 {tex}\times{/tex} 32 g of solid sulphur = 6.023 {tex}\times{/tex} 1023 molecules
    16 g of sulphur = {tex}\frac{6.023 \times 10^{23}}{8 \times 32} \times 16{/tex}
    = 0.376 {tex}\times{/tex} 1023 molecules
    = 3.76 {tex}\times{/tex} 1022 molecules

questions.question: Two flasks I and II contain oxygen (O2) and ozone (O3) respectively. Flask I contains 0.5 mole of oxygen while flask II contains 0.4 mole of ozone. Which of the following statements is correct regarding the number of oxygen atoms present in two flasks?

options.option: Flask II has greater number of oxygen atoms as compared to flask I.
Both flasks I and II have same number of oxygen atoms.
Both flasks I and II have different number of oxygen atoms.
Flask I has greater number of oxygen atoms as compared to flask II.

questions.explanation:
1 mole of O2 contains 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 6.023 {tex}\times{/tex} 1023 oxygen atoms
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 0.5 mole of O2 contains 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 6.023 {tex}\times{/tex} 1023 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.5 oxygen atoms i.e., 6.023 {tex}\times{/tex} 1023 oxygen atoms.
1 mole of O3 contains 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 6.023 {tex}\times{/tex} 1023 oxygen atoms
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 0.4 mole of O3 contains 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 6.023 {tex}\times{/tex} 1023 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.4 oxygen atoms i.e., 7.2276 {tex}\times{/tex} 1023 oxygen atoms
Thus, flask II containing ozone has greater number of oxygen atoms as compared to flask I containing oxygen.

questions.question: An element P forms an oxide with formula PO. The formulae of its sulphate and phosphate will be respectively
questions.explanation:
Formula of oxide = PO
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Valency of P = +2
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Formula of sulphate = PSO4
and phosphate = P3(PO4)2

questions.question:
Which of the following statements are incorrect?

  1. Dichromate ion is a divalent and positive ion.
  2. Barium ion is trivalent and positive.
  3. Solid sulphur is a polyatomic molecule.
  4. Ammonium ion is divalent and positive.
questions.explanation:
Dichromate ion - Cr2O72-
Barium ion - Ba2+, Ammonium ion - {tex}\mathrm{NH}_4^{+}{/tex}

questions.question: Number of aluminium ions in 0.055 g of aluminium oxide is
questions.explanation:
Molar mass of aluminum oxide (Al2O3) = 102 g
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Number of particles = {tex}\frac mM{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 6.023 {tex}\times{/tex} 1023
= {tex}\frac{0.055}{102}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 6.023 {tex}\times{/tex} 1023 = 3.24 {tex}\times{/tex} 1020 AI2O3 units
Number of Al3+ ions = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 3.24 {tex}\times{/tex} 1020 = 6.48 {tex}\times{/tex} 1020 ions

questions.question: An ionic compound of element M and chlorine has the formula, MCl3. The molecular mass of MCl3 is 118.5. What will be the molecular mass of the oxide of element M?
questions.explanation:
The valency of M in MCl3 is +3 hence, the formula of the oxide of element M is M2O3.
Mass of element M in MCl3 = 118.5 - 3(35.5) = 12
Molecular mass of M2O3 = (2 {tex}\times{/tex} 12) + (3 {tex}\times{/tex} 16) = 72

questions.question: A given sample of sulphur contains 0.25 mol of sulphur molecules. The number of moles of sulphur atoms, number of sulphur molecules and number of sulphur atoms present in the given sample are respectively
questions.explanation:
No. of moles of S atoms in 0.25 mol of S8 = 8 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.25 = 2
No. of sulphur molecules in 0.25 mol of sulphur = 6.023 {tex}\times{/tex} 1023 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.25 = 1.506 {tex}\times{/tex} 1023
No. of S atoms in 0.25 mol of S8 = 1.506 {tex}\times{/tex} 1023 {tex}\times{/tex} 8 = 1.205 {tex}\times{/tex} 1024

questions.question:
The maximum amount of sodium that can be obtained by electrolysis of 117 g of sodium chloride is
[Atomic mass of Na = 23 u, Cl = 35.5 u]
questions.explanation:
Electrolysis decomposes ionic compounds into their elements.
Mass of NaCI = 23 + 35.5 = 58.5 u
Now, 58.5 g of NaCI contains 23 g of Na
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 117 g of NaCI will contain {tex}\frac{23}{58.5} \times 117{/tex} = 46 g of Na

questions.question:
Four different experiments were conducted in the following ways:

  1. 3 g of carbon was burnt in 8 g of oxygen to give 11 g of CO2.
  2. 1.2 g of carbon was burnt in air to give 4.2 g of CO2.
  3. 4.5 g of carbon was burnt in enough air to give 11 g of CO2.
  4. 4 g of carbon was burnt in oxygen to form 30.3 g of CO2.

Law of constant proportions is not illustrated in experiment(s).
questions.explanation:
According to law of constant proportions, the ratio of carbon and oxygen by weight should always be 3 : 8 in CO2.
In experiment i - C : O = 3 : 8
In experiment ii - C : O = 1.2 : 3
In experiment iii - C : O = 4.5 : 6.5
In experiment iv - C : O = 4 : 26.3

questions.question:
Match column I with column II and mark the correct option from the given codes.

Column I Column II
(a) 28 g of He (i) 58.5 u
(b) 0.5 mole of O2 (ii) 7 mol
(c) Molecular mass of common salt (iii) 60 g
(d) 1.5 mole of Ca (iv) 16 g
The ratio of molecules present in 6.6 g of CO2 and 3.2 g of SO2, is
questions.explanation:
Molecular mass of CO2 = 44 u
Molar mass of CO2 = 44 g
No. of molecules of CO2 present in 6.6 g of CO2
= {tex}\frac{6.6}{44} {/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 6.023 {tex} \times {/tex} 1022
No. of molecules of SO2 present in 3.2 g of SO2
= {tex}\frac{3.2}{64}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 6.023 {tex}\times{/tex} 1023 = 3.011 {tex}\times{/tex} 1022
Ratio of molecules of CO2 and SO2
= {tex}\frac{9.033 \times 10^{22}}{3.011 \times 10^{22}}{/tex} = 3, i.e., 3 : 1

questions.question: Two samples X and Y of a pure substance obtained by two different methods contain elements P and Q. Substance X has a mass of 2.25 g and contains 0.90 g of Q. Substance Y is made up of 60% P and 40% Q by weight. This is an illustration of
questions.explanation:
Mass of X = 2.25 g
Mass of Q = 0.90 g
Thus, mass of P = 2.25 - 0.90 = 1.35 g
Ratio of masses of P and Q in sample ‘X' = 1.35 : 0.90 i.e., 1.5 : 1
Ratio of masses of P and Q in sample ‘Y’ = 60 : 40 i.e., 1.5 : 1
As the ratio of the masses of P and Q in two samples ‘X' and 'Y' is same i.e., 1.5 : 1 so, this is an illustration of ‘law of constant proportions’.

questions.question: Which of the following molecules contains highest number of particles?
questions.explanation:
Number of particles in 8 g of CH4
= {tex}\frac{6.023 \times 10^{23}}{16} {/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 8 = 3.0115 {tex}\times{/tex} 1023
No. of particles in 4.4 g of CO2
= {tex}\frac{6.023 \times 10^{23}}{44}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 4.4 = 6.023 {tex}\times{/tex} 1022
No. of particles in 34.2 g of C12H22O11
= {tex}\frac{6.023 \times 10^{23}}{342}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 34.2 = 6.023 {tex}\times{/tex} 1022
No. of particles in 2 g of H2
= {tex}\frac{6.023 \times 10^{23}}{2}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 2 = 6.023 {tex}\times{/tex} 1023

questions.question: The graphical representation of number of molecules of different gases is given. Which gases are placed at correct position?
questions.explanation:
2 g of H2 = 6.023 {tex}\times{/tex} 1023 molecules
1 g of H2 = {tex}\frac{6.023 \times 10^{23}}{2}{/tex} molecules
i.e., 3.011 {tex}\times{/tex} 1023 molecules
4 g of He {tex}\equiv{/tex} 6.023 {tex}\times{/tex} 1023 molecules
2 g of He {tex}\equiv{/tex} {tex}\frac{6.023 \times 10^{23}}{4} {/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 2 molecules
i.e., 3.011 {tex}\times{/tex} 1023 molecules
28 g of N2 {tex}\equiv{/tex} 6.023 {tex}\times{/tex} 1023 molecules
3 g of N2 {tex}\equiv{/tex} {tex}\frac{6.023 \times 10^{23}}{28}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 3 molecules
i.e., 0.6453 {tex}\times{/tex} 1023 molecules
16 g of CH4 {tex}\equiv{/tex} 6.023 {tex}\times{/tex} 1023 molecules
