questions.explanation: The body of Amoeba is elongated and has a constriction in the middle. Hence the given slide is the one that shows the asexual reproduction process of binary fission in Amoeba.

questions.question:
Upon observing slides showing stages of reproduction in Amoeba and Yeast, the student reported the following observations. The correct observations are:

  1. Cytokinesis was seen in the yeast cell.
  2. A chain of buds was seen due to reproduction in Amoeba.
  3. In Amoeba, the elongated nucleus was dividing to form two daughter nuclei.
  4. Single cells of Amoeba and Yeast were undergoing binary fission and budding respectively.
questions.explanation: In Amoeba, the nucleus divides, first then cytoplasm to form daughter nuclei. This is binary fission. Budding takes place in yeast.

questions.question:
A chain of Yeast cells forms because:

  1. Yeast cells do not separate after budding.
  2. Daughter cells are unable to survive without parent cells.
  3. Buds reproduce as soon as they are formed.
  4. Daughter cells stick together with the help of mucus.
questions.explanation: Yeast cells do not separate after budding because it is only after the formation of a chain of buds get separated.

questions.question:
In the list of organisms given below, those that reproduce by the asexual method are

  1. banana
  2. dog
  3. yeast
  4. Amoeba
questions.explanation:
Asexual reproduction is the mode of reproduction in single-parent produces offsprings.

  • Amoeba reproduces asexually by binary fission.
  • Asexual reproduction occurs in yeast by means of budding and in banana asexual reproduction occurs by the means of vegetative propagation from the stem. 
  • Dogs reproduced sexually.

questions.question: In a flower, the parts that produce male and female gametes (germ cells) are
questions.explanation:
• The anther is part of the stamen (male sex organ) that produce pollen (male gamete).
  • The ovary is an inferior part of the pistil (female sex organ) which contains ovule. Female gametophyte develops in the ovule.

questions.question: Which of the following is the correct sequence of events of sexual reproduction in a flower?
questions.explanation: In pollination, the pollen grains transfer from stamen (anther) to stigma after which fertilization takes place during which germ cells fuse together to form a zygote which in turn leads to embryo formation. Fertilized ovule becomes seed and seeds germinate to produce a seedling.

questions.question: In Spirogyra, asexual reproduction takes place by

options.option: division of a cell into two cells
division of a cell into many cells
breaking up of filaments into smaller bits
formation of young cells from older cells

questions.explanation: In Spirogyra, reproduction takes place by the fragmentation that involves breaking down of the filaments into smaller bits.

questions.question: The ability of a cell to divide into several cells during reproduction in Plasmodium is called
questions.explanation: The ability of a cell to divide itself into several cells during reproduction is called multiple fission. e.g., Plasmodium.

questions.question: The correct sequence of reproductive stages seen in flowering plants is
questions.explanation:
The sequence of reproductive stages seen in flowering plants is as follows:
Gametes{tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} Zygote{tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} Embryo{tex}\longrightarrow{/tex}Seedling

questions.question: The number of chromosomes in parents and offsprings of a particular species remains constant due to
questions.explanation: The number of chromosomes in parents and offspring of a particular species remains constant due to the halving of chromosomes during the gamete formation.

questions.question: In Rhizopus, tubular thread-like structures bearing sporangia at their tips are called
questions.explanation: Rhizopus is a saprobic fungus that feeds on the dead decaying organic matter. Its body is made up of branching mycelia composed of three types of hyphae- rhizoids, stolons, and unbranched sporangiophores. Rhizoids are root-like hyphae that grow downward into the soil and help in the absorption of water. A stolon is a special slender horizontal hypha that helps in the propagation of an organism. Sporangiophores are straight, unbranched tubular thread-like hyphae which bear rounded sporangia at its tips. sporangia produce non-motile multinucleate spores for asexual reproduction.

questions.question: Vegetative propagation refers to formation of new plants from
questions.explanation: Vegetative propagation is a type of asexual reproduction. In this, new plants are produced by the parts of plants like stem, roots, and leaves without the help of any reproductive organ.

questions.question:
Factors responsible for the rapid spread of bread mould on slices of bread are

  1. a large number of spores
  2. availability of moisture and nutrients in bread
  3. presence of tubular branched hyphae
  4. formation of round shaped sporangia
questions.explanation:
Factors responsible for the rapid spread of bread mould on slices of bread are:

  1. a large number of spores
  2. availability of moisture and nutrients in bread

questions.question: Length of pollen tube depends on the distance between

options.option: pollen grain on upper surface of stigma and ovule
pollen grain and upper surface of stigma
upper surface of stigma and lower part of style
pollen grain in anther and upper surface of stigma

questions.explanation: The length of the pollen tube depends on the distance between the upper surface of the stigma and the ovule.

questions.question: Offspring formed as a result of sexual reproduction exhibit more variations because

options.option: genetic material comes from two parents of different species
genetic material comes from two parents of the same species

questions.explanation: Sexual reproduction involves two parents of the same species. Thus, both of them contribute to the genetic material of the offspring and bring about the variations.

questions.question: During adolescence, several changes occur in the human body. Mark one change associated with sexual maturation in boys
questions.explanation: Cracking of voice in males is brought about by the male hormone testosterone, which is produced after sexual maturation.

questions.question: In human females, an event that reflects the onset of the reproductive phase is
questions.explanation: Menstruation is the event that reflects the onset of the reproductive phase.

questions.question: In human males, the testes lie in the scrotum, because it helps in the
questions.explanation: The testes lie outside the body in the scrotum because sperm production requires low temperature, which is provided by the scrotum.

questions.question:
Which among the following is not the function of testes at puberty?

  1. formation of germ cells
  2. secretion of testosterone
  3. development of placenta
  4. secretion of estrogen
questions.explanation:
Gonads have a dual function, production of hormones and gametes. In the male, testes produce sperms and testosterone. 
The placenta develops in females during pregnancy. It has a number of functions, like the production of hormones, nutrition, excretion, etc. Estrogen is a female hormone produced by the ovaries (female gonads).

questions.question: The correct sequence of organs in the male reproductive system for transport of sperms is
questions.explanation: Sperms are produced in the testis and then carried away by the vas deferens to the urethra.

questions.question:
To perform an experiment to identify the different parts of an embryo of a dicot seed, first of all you require a dicot seed. Select dicot seeds from the following group.
Wheat, gram, maize, pea, barley, ground-nut.
questions.explanation: Gram, pea and ground-nut

questions.question: Fertilisation is the process of

options.option: fusion of nuclei of male and female gamete
transfer of male gamete to female gamete
adhesion of male and female reproductive organs
the formation of gametes by a reproductive organ

questions.explanation: fusion of nuclei of male and female gamete

questions.question:
The diagram shows a section through the male reproductive system. What is the function of part X?

  1. The secretion of the gland neutralises acids from urine.
  2. It decreases the number of sperms damaged during ejaculation.
  3. It stimulates uterine contraction to help sperms move forward into the fallopian tube.
  4. Both (i) and (ii)
questions.explanation: Gland X is Cowper's gland that is present in males. It secretes an alkaline fluid.

questions.question: In generative fertilisation, a ________ fuses with a ________.
questions.explanation: In generative fertilisation, a male gamete fuses with a female gamete and forms diploid zygote.

questions.question:
Select the incorrect statement.

  1. Carpel is a part of androecium.
  2. One non-motile male gamete is formed inside the tube during its growth through style.
  3. Synergids have filiform apparatus that guides the entry of pollen tube into the nucellus.
  4. All of these
questions.explanation: Carpel is a part of gynoecium. Two non-motile male gametes are formed inside the tube during its growth through style. Synergids guide the entry of pollen tube that has already entered nucellus, into the embryo sac.

questions.question: ________ is a product of meiosis.
questions.explanation: Megaspore is a haploid cell that is formed by meiosis in the megaspore mother cell. It is the first cell of female gametophyte.

questions.question: New plants may be grown from groups of cells that are taken from other plants. The given diagram shows parts different of a plant X. Cell samples obtained from which of the labelled structures will grow into new plants that are genetically identical to plant X?
questions.explanation: There are two types of cells in an organism somatic cells (2n) and germ cells (n). Somatic cell samples give rise to new organisms that are genetically identical to their parents because in somatic cells, crossing over does not occur and hence variations are not produced. Germ cells produce variations. Thus, cell samples taken from part S (thalamus) of plant X will give rise to new plants that are genetically identical to plant X. Other parts P, Q and R have germ cells.

questions.question: Select the barrier method of contraception among the following.
questions.explanation: Cervical cap is a flexible rubber or plastic cover that is fitted over the cervix in the female's vagina. It checks the entry of sperms into the uterus.

questions.question: The given graph shows the hormonal changes during a normal menstrual cycle. What would be a likely consequence if the hormone represented by Q in the given graph is lacking in an adult female?

options.option: The uterine lining might not be sufficiently stable for implantation of fertilised ovum.
There would be no significant effect since the functions of the hormones overlap.
Fertilisation of ovum would fail to occur.
Levels of the hormone represented by graph P would be higher than normal.

questions.explanation: In the given graph, P represents changes in estrogen level and graph Q represents changes in progesterone level during a normal menstrual cycle. Progesterone is required for the maintenance of uterine lining which is needed to implant the fertilised ovum. Thus, if progesterone is lacking in an adult female, her uterine lining might not be stable to support an implanted embryo.

questions.question: Select the STD caused by bacteria among the following.
questions.explanation: Syphilis spreads through sexual contact and is caused by Treponema pallidum. Genital herpes and Hepatitis-B are caused by virus and giardiasis is caused by protozoa.

questions.question:
Statement 1: Subcutaneous implant is a method of contraception and it is effective for a longer period.
Statement 2: Capsules are inserted under the skin and they slowly release synthetic progesterone.

options.option: Both statements 1 and 2 are true but statement 2 is not the correct explanation of statement 1.
Both statements 1 and 2 are true and statement 2 is the correct explanation of statement 1.
Statement 1 is true but statement 2 is false.

questions.explanation: Both statements 1 and 2 are true and statement 2 is the correct explanation of statement 1.

questions.question: Which of the following is not an example of natural method of vegetative propagation in plants?

options.option: Leaves in case of Bryophyllum and Begonia.
Roots in case of sweet potato and Colocasia.
Stem in case of garlic and onion.
Stem in case of Eichhornia and strawberry.

questions.explanation: Colocasia is vegetatively propagated from underground stem.

questions.question: Which of the following is a characteristic of wind pollinated flowers?

options.option: Pollen grains are heavy and dry whereas the stigma is short and sticky.
Flowers are small with nectar and fragrance.
Pollen grains are light, small and dusty whereas the stigma is hairy and feathery.

questions.explanation: In wind pollinated flowers, pollen grains are light, small and dusty so that they can be carried by wind to distant places. Stigma is hairy and feathery to catch the wind-borne pollen grains.

questions.question:
Match column I with column II and select the correct option from the codes given below.

Column I Column II
(a) Riccia (i) Binary fission
(b) Planaria (ii) Budding
(c) Euglena (iii) Fragmentation
(d) Sycon (iv) Regeneration

The given figure shows monthly changes in the human ovary during the reproductive cycle.

Which of the following statements is correct regarding the labelled structures?

  1. Before puberty, only structure T undergoes meiosis.
  2. The hormone produced by structure R stimulates the pituitary gland to secrete luteinising hormone.
  3. The hormone produced by structure S is responsible for the development of female secondary sex characters.
  4. The hormone produced by P and Q stimulates the proliferation of the endometrial lining of the uterine wall.
questions.explanation:
In the given figure of human ovary, P is primary follicles, Q is mature Graafian follicle, R is corpus luteum, S is corpus albicans and T is secondary oocyte.
Secondary oocyte (7) is formed only after puberty in a monthly cycle (one in each cycle). The corpus luteum (R) is developed under the effect of luteinising hormone from pituitary. The hormone progesterone secreted by it, maintains the uterine endothelium and stimulates secretion of watery mucus from uterine glands. Corpus albicans (S) secretes no hormone. P (primary follicle) and Q (Graafian follicle) secrete estrogen which stimulates the proliferation of endometrial lining of the uterine wall.

questions.question:
The given diagram shows the carpel of an insect pollinated flower. What is the most likely reason for the non-germination of pollen grain Z?

  1. Pollen grains X and Y were brought to the stigma earlier, therefore, their germination inhibited the germination of pollen grain Z.
  2. Pollen grain Z was brought to the flower by wind, while pollen grains X and Y were brought to the flower by insects.
  3. Pollen grain Z lacks protrusions that allow it to adhere properly onto the stigma surface.
  4. Pollen grain Z comes from a flower of an incompatible species.
questions.explanation: Pollen grains can only germinate if the pollen grain and stigma are compatible, i.e., of the same or closely related species, Pollen grains X and Y must have come from a compatible species. Only compatible pollen grains germinate and form pollen tubes.

questions.question:
A student decides to study the impact of removing certain flower parts on fruit formation in plant species X. He chooses three separate plants that are growing in the same plot under uniform conditions. The data is given in the table below.

Plant Part removed Impact on formation
1 Anther 30% less fruit formed than average plan in the plot
2 Stigma No fruit formed
3 Petal No significant impact

Which of the following cannot be inferred from the above data?

options.option: Anthers and stigmas are crucial in sexual reproduction in species X.
Species X relies com pletely on cross-pollination.
Pollen grains are probably unable to germinate if they land on other parts of the carpel besides the stigma.
Species X is likely to be wind-pollinated.

questions.explanation: The removal of anthers affect fruit formation in plant 1, this implies that species X relies partially on self-pollination. The removal of either anthers or stigmas affects rate of fruit formation significantly. No fruits are observed when the stigmas in plant 2 are removed. This shows that pollen grains are probably unable to germinate if they land on any other part of the carpel besides the stigma. The petals do not seem to play a significant role in facilitating fruit formation. Species X is therefore likely to be wind-pollinated.

questions.question: The thread like structures that develop on a moist slice of bread in Rhizopus are
questions.explanation: The thread like structures that developed on the bread are the hyphae of the fungus Rhizopus stolon if also known as black bread mold.

questions.question: Select the INCORRECT match (between the plant and its vegetative part) from the following :
questions.explanation: Rose vegetative part is stem.

questions.question: Plants which bear unisexual flowers are:
questions.explanation: Watermelon and Papaya bears unisexual flower.

questions.question: When an X bearing sperm fertilises the egg, the resulting zygote has the following combination of chromosomes:
questions.explanation: As there are 23 pairs of chromosomes and if X sperm is fertilized the result is XX

questions.question: The number of chromosomes in parents and offsprings of a particular species undergoing sexual reproduction remain constant due to:

options.option: halving of chromosomes at the time of gamete formation.

questions.explanation: There are always half as many chromosomes in gametes as there are in an individual. As a result, chromosomes are cut in half just before gamete formation. The normal chromosome number (half from each gamete) is restored once the male gamete fuses with the female gamete.

questions.question: During adolescence, reproductive phase starts and:

options.option: general growth rate begins to slow down

questions.explanation: As the rate of general body growth begins to slow down, reproductive tissues begin to mature. This period during adolescence is called puberty.

questions.question: The bacterial and the viral infections that may be caused due to unsafe sex respectively are:
questions.explanation: Gonorrhoea and Syphilis

questions.question:
Which of the following statements is incorrect?

  1. Placenta allows exchange of materials between mother and fetus.
  2. The fetal part of the placenta consists of the cells of the chorion which produce projections called chorionic villi.
  3. Antibody cannot cross the placenta from mother to fetus.
  4. Placenta secretes pregnancy hormones required for supporting fetal growth and metabolic changes in mother during pregnancy.
questions.explanation: Placenta is an intimate connection between the fetus and uterine wall of the mother. Antibody IgG can cross the placenta from mother to fetus.

questions.question: The organism in which an outgrowth on the parent body develops into a new individual is:
questions.explanation: Yeast

questions.question: The stamen contains
questions.explanation: pollen grain

questions.question: Soil fertility is determined by its ability to:
questions.explanation: Support life

questions.question: Pseudopodia are

options.option: false feet developed in some unicellular organisms.
long, tube-like structures coming out of the mouth.
suckers which are attached to the walls of the intestines.
small hair-like structures present on unicellular organisms.

questions.explanation: false feet developed in some unicellular organisms.

questions.question: Which one of the following is not a part of the human female reproductive system?
questions.explanation: Seminal vesicle

questions.question: Which one of the following organism is represented by this diagram?
questions.explanation: Rhizopus is shown in above diagram

questions.question: Sex determination depends upon the environment in
questions.explanation: Reptiles

questions.question: In the given diagram the leaf shown belongs to which plant?
questions.explanation: Bryophyllum

questions.question:
Select from the following the conditions responsible for the rapid spread of bread mould on a slice of bread:

  1. Formation of large number of spores
  2. Presence of moisture and nutrients in bread
  3. Low temperature
  4. Presence of hyphae
questions.explanation: (i) and (ii)

questions.question: The plants that can be raised by the method of vegetative propagation are:
questions.explanation: Sugarcane, roses, grapes

questions.question: Part(s) of a flower which attracts insects for pollination is (are):
questions.explanation: petals only

questions.question: Identify the mode of asexual reproduction in the following organism:
questions.explanation: Above oragnism is hydra which reproduce by budding.

questions.question: The modes of reproduction in Spirogyra and Planaria respectively are
questions.explanation: Fragmentation and regeneration

questions.question: Select a pair of bisexual flowers from the following:
questions.explanation: Hibiscus and mustard

questions.question: The number of chromosomes in a cell division is halved. This kind of cell division is observed in:
questions.explanation: Ovary and testis both

questions.question: Bryophyllum produces new plant through:

options.option: Vegetative buds produced in the notches of the leaf
Flowers produced in the notches of the branches
Fruits formed on the branches of the plant
Apical buds formed on the tip of the plant

questions.explanation: Vegetative buds produced in the notches of the leaf

questions.question: In a bisexual flower the male gametes are present in the:
questions.explanation: anther

questions.question: Plants like rose and banana have lost the capacity to produce:
questions.explanation: seeds

questions.question: When a seed germinates the root grows downwards and a small shoot grows upward. This shoot is known as
questions.explanation: Plumule

questions.question: Which one of the following has half the number of chromosomes and half the amount of DNA as compared to the non-reproductive body cells?
questions.explanation: Both, male and female germ cells.

questions.question: The end product of meiosis in a reproductive cell in plants is:
questions.explanation: Pollen grains

questions.question: In bread mould, the structures labelled A and B respectively are :
questions.explanation: hyphae and sporangium

questions.question: A flower’s anthers are removed before it matures. Later, pollen from another plant of the same species is dusted onto its stigma and seeds are produced. Which process has been ensured here?
questions.explanation:
Removing the anthers prevents self-pollination. When pollen from another plant of the same species is placed on the stigma, it ensures that pollination occurs between different plants.

This process is called cross-pollination.

questions.question: In which group of animals does fertilization take place inside the female's body?
questions.explanation: Reptiles, birds, and mammals exhibit internal fertilization, where the fusion of gametes occurs within the female's reproductive tract.

questions.question: Which mode of reproduction is characterized by the fusion of gametes outside the body of the female parent?
questions.explanation: External fertilization occurs when the male and female gametes fuse outside the body, typically in an aquatic environment.

questions.question: What happens to the ovary of a flower after fertilisation?
questions.explanation: The text states that 'the ovary surrounding the ovules enlarges and develops into a fruit' after fertilisation.

questions.question: In the process of fertilisation in flowering plants, what is formed immediately after the fusion of the male gamete with the egg cell?
questions.explanation: The fertilised egg is called a zygote, which later develops into an embryo.

questions.question: What is the term for the transfer of pollen from the anther to the stigma of the same flower or another flower on the same plant?
questions.explanation: The transfer of pollen to the stigma of the same flower or another flower of the same plant is self-pollination.

questions.question: What is the collective term for the male reproductive part of a flowering plant?
questions.explanation: The stamen is the male part of a flower.

questions.question: Which of the following processes ensures that each gamete receives only one chromosome from each pair during sexual reproduction?
questions.explanation: Meiosis is a specific type of cell division that reduces the chromosome number by half, ensuring each gamete has only one chromosome from each homologous pair.

questions.question: Identify an example of a plant that uses its leaves for vegetative propagation.
questions.explanation: Bryophyllum leaves sprout tiny plantlets which eventually grow into new plants, an example of vegetative propagation through leaves.

questions.question: What is vegetative propagation as it relates to plant reproduction?
questions.explanation: Vegetative propagation means that new plants arise from the vegetative parts, i.e., from growing parts of a plant, such as stems, leaves, or roots, without involving seeds.

questions.question: What makes spores effective for fungal reproduction and dispersal?
questions.explanation: Spores are lightweight, usually single-celled, and float easily through air currents, allowing them to disperse widely and germinate quickly under favorable conditions.

questions.question: Which scientist proved that new life always comes from pre-existing life, strengthening the cell theory?
questions.explanation: Experiments by Louis Pasteur proved that new life always comes from pre-existing life. He also found the germ theory of disease and strengthened the cell theory.

questions.question: What is the term for a small outgrowth produced by repeated cell division at a specific site on the parent body of organisms like hydra?
questions.explanation: In organisms such as hydra, repeated cell division at a specific site on the parent body produces a small outgrowth called a bud. This bud then enlarges and separates to live independently.

questions.question: What is the primary cellular process underlying asexual reproduction that results in genetically identical offspring?
questions.explanation: The central process behind asexual reproduction is mitosis, a type of cell division that produces two daughter cells, each having the same number of chromosomes identical to the parent cell.

questions.question: Which statement accurately describes the characteristics of offspring produced through asexual reproduction?
questions.explanation: Asexual reproduction involves only one parent and thus produces individuals that are genetically identical to the parent. This similarity is often referred to as being 'clones'.

questions.question: Which of the following organisms can reproduce asexually?
questions.explanation: Asexual reproduction is observed in many unicellular organisms like bacteria, amoeba, and yeast, as well as simple multicellular organisms such as hydra and sponge.

questions.question: What is the significance of meiosis in the formation of human gametes?
questions.explanation: Meiosis reduces the chromosome number in gametes by half (to 23), so that when an egg and sperm combine during fertilization, the resulting zygote has the species-specific number of chromosomes (46).

questions.question: What happens to the inner lining of the uterus if an egg is not fertilized?
questions.explanation: If an egg is not fertilized, the thickened inner lining of the uterus, no longer needed to nourish a developing zygote, sheds from the body in a process called menstruation.

questions.question: In humans, how many chromosomes are present in a sperm or an egg, compared to a normal body cell?
questions.explanation: Gametes (sperm and eggs) have 23 chromosomes, which is half the number found in a normal human body cell (46 chromosomes). This reduction occurs during meiosis.

questions.question: What is the process called when an egg is released from one of the ovaries, typically every month?
questions.explanation: Ovulation is the release of a mature egg from an ovary, occurring approximately once a month from puberty onwards.

questions.question: Which structure in the female reproductive system connects the ovary to the uterus?
questions.explanation: The oviducts, also known as fallopian tubes, are the structures that connect each ovary to the uterus.

questions.question: What is the primary function of the scrotum in the male reproductive system?

options.option: To transfer sperm to the female body
To keep testes cooler for sperm formation.

questions.explanation: The scrotum's primary function is to maintain a slightly cooler temperature for the testes, which is essential for proper sperm formation.

questions.question: Which of the following organs is responsible for producing male germ cells (sperm) and a hormone that controls sperm production and causes physical changes in boys during puberty?
questions.explanation: The testes are the oval-shaped organs that produce male germ cells (sperm) and a hormone that controls sperm production and causes physical changes in boys during puberty.

questions.question: Which animals develop their zygote inside the female body?
questions.explanation: In mammals, the zygote grows and develops inside the female body.

questions.question: What is the typical range of eggs produced by a lizard at one time?
questions.explanation: Lizards produce 2-20 eggs at a time.

questions.question: How many eggs are typically produced by frogs undergoing external fertilization?
questions.explanation: Frogs produce '5,000-50,000 at a time' during reproduction.

questions.question: What is a characteristic disadvantage of external fertilization in aquatic animals?

options.option: Eggs are exposed to currents and predators.

questions.explanation: In external fertilization, eggs are released into the environment, making them vulnerable to environmental factors like water currents and predation, leading to lower survival rates.

questions.question: Which mode of fertilisation typically results in a lower estimated survival rate of young ones?
questions.explanation: Fish and Frog, which undergo external fertilisation, have a low estimated survival of young ones, while Lizard and Bird, with internal fertilisation, have moderate to high survival rates.

questions.question: If a farmer wants to grow high-yield fruits and eliminate virus-infected plants using the most advanced asexual reproduction technique, which method would be most appropriate?
questions.explanation: Propagation of plants by the tissue culture technique has revolutionised farming practices, providing mass-produced healthy plantlets from the shoot tip (apical meristem), which helps eliminate virus-infected plants and ensures high yields.

questions.question: Why is regular changing of menstrual products, like sanitary pads, important for hygiene?
questions.explanation: The hygiene practices suggest changing menstrual products regularly (every 4–6 hours or more often with heavy flow). This is crucial for maintaining cleanliness, preventing bacterial growth that can lead to infections, and managing odors, all of which contribute to overall health and well-being during menstruation.

questions.question: What is the primary factor that determines the biological sex of a baby?

options.option: Combination of X and Y from both parents.

questions.explanation: Females have XX and males have XY chromosomes. The mother always contributes an X chromosome to a baby and the father contributes either an X (Female: XX) or a Y (Male: XY) chromosome. Therefore, the father determines the biological sex of the baby.

questions.question: If a couple is having difficulty conceiving naturally, and traditional methods have not been successful, which advanced medical technique, could be a potential solution to help them achieve pregnancy?
questions.explanation: In-vitro Fertilisation (IVF) is described as a medical technique where an egg and a sperm are combined outside the female body in a laboratory dish. The resultant fertilised egg is then implanted in the uterus to begin a possible pregnancy. This method is used when natural conception is difficult.

questions.question: If a couple uses oral contraceptive pills but not condoms, what risks remain in terms of reproductive health?
questions.explanation: Oral contraceptive pills primarily prevent pregnancy by altering hormone levels to prevent egg release. However, they do not offer protection against Sexually Transmitted Infections (STIs), as they do not provide a physical barrier against the transmission of infectious agents. Therefore, the risk of STIs remains.

questions.question: A human zygote has just formed. How many chromosomes does it have?
questions.explanation: In humans, gametes (sperm and eggs) have 23 chromosomes due to meiosis. When a sperm and egg combine, they form a zygote with the sum of their chromosomes, which is 23 + 23 = 46. This ensures the zygote has the same number of chromosomes as the parents.

questions.question: Rina's period occurs every 28 days. Her last period was on the 5th of March. On which day is she most likely to get her next period?
questions.explanation: If Rina's period cycle is 28 days and her last period started on March 5th, her next period is expected 28 days later. March 5 + 28 days = April 2nd.

questions.question: Ravi suddenly notices that he is growing taller rapidly, his shoulders are broadening, and his voice cracks. What stage of life is he entering?
questions.explanation: Growing taller rapidly, broadening shoulders, voice cracking are all physical changes that occur during puberty, marking the transition into adolescence.

questions.question: What is the recommended proper disposal method for used sanitary pads?

options.option: Wrap in paper/wrapper, then dispose in bin.

questions.explanation: Proper disposal of used products - wrap used pads in newspaper or the wrapper they came in before disposing them in a bin. Do not flush them down the toilet.

questions.question: What is the significance of the uterus during pregnancy?
questions.explanation: The uterus is a bag-like structure where a foetus develops. The uterus protects and nourishes the baby throughout this time.

questions.question: How do condoms prevent unwanted pregnancies and STIs?

options.option: By changing hormones to stop egg release.
By acting as a barrier to block sperm and infections.
By blocking fallopian tubes or vas deferens.

questions.explanation: To prevent unwanted pregnancy, different contraceptive (pregnancy-preventing) methods can be used. Some of them act as barriers, like condoms or vaginal covers, which stop sperm from reaching the egg. Using condoms can prevent their transmission (STIs) and also help prevent pregnancy.

questions.question: What is a 'test tube baby' commonly referred to as, and how is it conceived?
questions.explanation: A baby born through this method (In-vitro Fertilisation) is commonly known as a test tube baby, although the process actually occurs in a culture dish, not in a test tube.

questions.question: Approximately how long does a typical menstrual cycle last, and what are its key phases?

options.option: 45 days, including only menstruation and menopause.
14 days, primarily focused on egg maturation.

questions.explanation: The cycle of ovulation, preparation of the uterus and menstruation repeats typically every 21 – 35 days (often around 28 days), and usually begins at puberty, between the ages of 10–14 in girls and continues till menopause i.e., around age 50.

questions.question: What is ovulation?
questions.explanation: From puberty onwards, usually one mature egg is released every month from one of the ovaries. This is called ovulation.

questions.question: Which of the following best describes the characteristics of male gametes (sperm) compared to female gametes (eggs)?
questions.explanation: In males, gametogenesis results in the production of numerous tiny motile (moving), active sperm. In females, gametogenesis results in the formation of a single large egg. Male gametes are very small, produced in millions, and actively motile, while female gametes are large, few, and non-motile.

questions.question: What is the biological term for the formation of gametes in both males and females?
questions.explanation: The process of the formation of gametes is called gametogenesis. It takes place in the testes and the ovaries.

questions.question: Which female reproductive organ is responsible for producing female germ cells (eggs) and releasing hormones?
questions.explanation: The ovaries produce the female germ cells (eggs) and also release hormones.

questions.question: If a species produces 5 eggs internally, and each egg has enough yolk to nourish the embryo until hatching, which animal group does this species most closely resemble in its reproductive strategy?
questions.explanation: The 'reptiles and birds lay eggs, and each egg contains enough yolk to nourish the developing embryo until it hatches into a young one.' Lizards (reptiles) and birds produce a low number of eggs internally (2-20 and 1-15 respectively).

questions.question: What is a common challenge faced by all animals regardless of their specific reproductive methods?
questions.explanation: All animals face the basic challenge of ensuring that the male and the female gametes meet, and that the young ones survive long enough to grow and reproduce.

questions.question: In the life cycle of a butterfly, what is the 'intermediate stage' that is essentially a feeding stage?
questions.explanation: The larva gets nutrition by eating organic wastes and grows. This stage is an intermediate stage in its development, which is essentially a feeding stage.

questions.question: If an animal's reproductive strategy involves internal fertilisation, fewer than 20 eggs produced at a time, and a moderate to high survival rate of young ones, a scientist would categorize it similarly to which of the following animals?
questions.explanation: Birds exhibit internal fertilisation, produce 1-15 eggs, and have a moderate to high estimated survival of young ones. Lizards also have internal fertilisation and produce 2-20 eggs, but their survival rate is described as moderate, not moderate to high. Fish and Frogs use external fertilisation and produce many more eggs with low survival rates.

questions.question: Consider an animal that releases 10,000 eggs into the water for fertilisation, and the young ones have a low survival rate. Based on the provided information, which animal category is this most likely to belong to?
questions.explanation: Fish and Frogs use external fertilisation, produce a large number of eggs (hundreds to tens of thousands), and have a low estimated survival of young ones. A lizard has internal fertilisation, produces 2-20 eggs, and has a moderate survival rate. A bird also has internal fertilisation, produces 1-15 eggs, and has a moderate to high survival rate. Therefore, an animal releasing 10,000 eggs with low survival most closely matches Fish or Frog.

questions.question: How do mammals ensure the nourishment and development of their zygotes?
questions.explanation: In mammals, the zygote grows and develops inside the female body, ensuring nourishment.

questions.question: Which mode of fertilisation is characteristic of reptiles, birds, and mammals?
questions.explanation: In reptiles, birds, and mammals, fertilisation takes place inside the body of the female, which is known as internal fertilisation.

questions.question: What is the primary reason for a lower survival rate of young ones in external fertilisation compared to internal fertilisation?
questions.explanation: In external fertilisation, many eggs are destroyed by water currents or eaten by other animals, leading to lower survival chances of young ones. In internal fertilisation, the fertilised egg or embryo is protected inside the female's body, increasing survival chances.

questions.question: Where does fertilisation occur in most aquatic animals like frogs and fish?
questions.explanation: In many aquatic animals, such as frogs and most fish, fertilisation occurs outside the body in the water, which is termed external fertilisation.

questions.question: A gardener notices that a new variety of hibiscus plant (known to be insect-pollinated) is not producing many fruits despite abundant flowering. Upon closer inspection, the gardener finds that few bees and butterflies are visiting the flowers. What is a probable solution to increase fruit production?
questions.explanation: Since hibiscus is an insect-pollinated plant, a lack of pollinator visits would directly reduce fruit production. Introducing more insect pollinators, such as bees and butterflies, would facilitate pollen transfer and thus increase fruit set.

questions.question: A farmer observes that his sweet pea plants produce fruits in all treatments except for those where the stamens were removed from the flower bud. What can be inferred about the necessity of pollen transfer in fruit formation?

options.option: Fruit depends only on light and water.

questions.explanation: The observation indicates that fruit formation did not occur when stamens were removed. This implies that the transfer of pollen from stamens to stigma is an essential step for fruit formation, as stamens produce pollen.

questions.question: Which of the following parts of a flower is responsible for receiving pollen grains?
questions.explanation: The stigma is located at the tip of the pistil and may be flat and/or sticky, serving to receive pollen grains.

questions.question: What happens to the ovules after fertilization in flowering plants?
questions.explanation: The ovules develop into seeds inside the fruit after fertilization.

questions.question: What is the primary function of petals in a flower?
questions.explanation: The coloured projections of flowers are called petals. They enhance the aesthetics of the plants as they are coloured and/or fragrant, which helps in attracting pollinators.

questions.question: What defines cross-pollination?
questions.explanation: If the pollen is transferred from the anther of a flower of one plant to the stigma of a flower of another plant of the same type, the pollination is called cross-pollination.

questions.question: What is the function of sepals in a flower?
questions.explanation: The green covering around all parts in the bud stage is called a sepal, and it protects flowers when they are still buds.

questions.question: Which of the following is an example of an external agent (pollinator) for pollination?
questions.explanation: Pollination depends on external agents called pollinators, such as wind, water, insects (bees, butterflies), or birds. Bees are insects that act as pollinators.

questions.question: What is the primary role of meiosis in sexual reproduction?

options.option: To give full chromosomes from one parent.

questions.explanation: Meiosis is a special type of cell division that reduces the chromosome number of a parent cell (diploid) to half (haploid) in daughter cells, which are then used as gametes for reproduction.

questions.question: A student observes a plant with underground fleshy stems sprouting new plants without producing seeds. Which type of asexual reproduction is this an example of, and what practical application does it have?
questions.explanation: Plants with fleshy underground stems, such as potato and ginger, sprout new plants without producing seeds, which is an example of vegetative propagation. This broader category of asexual reproduction is widely used in agriculture and horticulture.

questions.question: A gardener wants to propagate a specific rose variety by grafting. If they use a wild rose as Plant A and a yellow rose as Plant B, what will be the characteristics of the flowers produced by the grafted plant?

options.option: Flowers only if Plant A is yellow rose.
Mix of wild and yellow rose traits.

questions.explanation: In grafting, Plant B (the scion) determines the characteristics of the upper part of the plant, including the flowers. Thus, if a yellow rose is used as Plant B, the grafted plant will produce yellow rose flowers from that part.

questions.question: In the context of grafting, what is the purpose of protecting the wound or slit with a cotton cloth or wrapping film?
questions.explanation: Protect the wound or slit by using a cotton cloth or by wrapping film to avoid pests entering the graft until it heals.

questions.question: The methods of vegetative propagation have significantly improved which two practices?
questions.explanation: These methods are widely used to efficiently propagate plants, and have significantly improved agricultural and horticultural practices.

questions.question: What is the primary benefit of vegetative propagation for farmers?
questions.explanation: Such methods enable farmers to cultivate desirable crops on a large scale.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a method of vegetative propagation adapted by scientists and horticulturists?
questions.explanation: Pollination is a sexual reproduction process, not a method of vegetative propagation. Cutting, grafting, layering, and tissue culture are methods of vegetative propagation.

questions.question: What is the primary genetic outcome of asexual reproduction?
questions.explanation: The key point about this type of reproduction is that it involves only one parent and hence, produces genetically identical individuals.

questions.question: Which of these plants reproduces asexually through leaves that sprout tiny plantlets?
questions.explanation: Bryophyllum leaves sprout tiny plantlets which eventually grow into new plants.

questions.question: Which of the following organisms primarily undergo asexual reproduction?
questions.explanation: Asexual reproduction is seen in many unicellular organisms like bacteria, amoeba, yeast and simple multicellular organisms (hydra, sponge).

chapters.name: Patterns in Life: Diversity and Classification

subtopics.name: What is the Basis of Classification

questions.question: The lowest category of classification is
questions.explanation:
Species is the lowest category of organisms. Every organism can be classified as 7 different levels- kingdom, phylum, class, order, family, genus, and species.

Each level contains organisms with similar characteristics. The kingdom is the largest group and very broad. Each successive group contains fewer organisms, but the organisms are more similar. The species is the smallest group and is very narrow. Organisms within a species are able to mate and produce fertile offspring.

questions.question: Which of the following structures are characteristics of reptiles but not of amphibians?
questions.explanation: Scales are characteristics of reptiles but not of amphibians. Reptiles and amphibians are distantly related to each other but in spite of some similarities they can be distinguished by their physical appearance as scales or modified scales are present on the body of reptiles. These scales act as a sort of armor in reptiles to physically defend the body  whereas amphibians have sluggy and smooth skin.

questions.question: Venus flower basket is the dried skeleton of
questions.explanation:
The 'Venus flower basket' (Euplectella aspergillum) is a hexactinellid sponge in the phylum Porifera inhabiting the deep ocean. It is also speculated that the bioluminescent light of bacteria harnessed by the sponge may attract other small organisms which the shrimp eat.

Scientific classification
Kingdom: Animalia
Phylum: Porifera
Class: Hexactinellida
Order: Lyssacinosidea
Family: Euplectellidae
Genus: Euplectella
Species: E. aspergillum

questions.question: Which one of the following is not the exclusive trait of Arthropoda?
questions.explanation: The presence of wings is not the exclusive trait of Arthropoda. Arthropods are the animals having jointed appendages. It is a phylum of triploblastic, haemocoelic, segmented invertebrates having head, thorax, and abdomen, a chitinous exoskeleton, and jointed legs and appendages. It is the most successful phylum on the earth whereas wings are found in Aves phylum

questions.question: Who is the Father of Taxonomy?
questions.explanation:
Carl Linnaeus, also known as Carl von Linné or Carolus Linnaeus, is often called the Father of Taxonomy. His system for naming, ranking, and classifying organisms is still in wide use today (with many
changes).

questions.question: Well defined nucleus is absent in -
questions.explanation: blue green algae. Algae are very large and diverse group of eukaryotic organisms, ranging from microscopic unicellular Chlorella and the diatoms to giant multicellular alga.Yeast is also a eukaryotic belonging to fungi kingdom. However, blue green algae or cyanobacteria is the most primitive photosynthetic bacteria cell which is quite small and unicellular.

questions.question: Which is the simplest of the following many-celled animals?
questions.explanation: The sponge is simple multicellular organisms. They are aquatic and have no true tissues. They are sessile but have free-swimming larva. Their bodies are porous and water flows through many small openings called Ostia.

questions.question: Which one is not a flightless bird?
questions.explanation: Dove is not a flightless bird. They are stout-bodied birds with short necks and short slender fleshy ceres. They primarily feed on seeds, fruits, plants, etc THey are small, stocky birds belong to pigeon family. Doves are known for their powerful, precise flight patterns and the whirling sound created by their wings.

questions.question: In which the reproductive organs are hidden?
questions.explanation: Cryptogamae

questions.question: Pteridophyta do not have -
questions.explanation: flowers. Pteridophytes are non flowering plants that do not ever produce flowers and seeds. Ferns produce their spores on the underside of the leaves. Ferns are vascular plants that have a stem, leaf and root.

questions.question: Who is known as the father of taxonomy?
questions.explanation: Carl Linnaeus is the father of taxonomy. His system for naming, ranking, and classifying organisms is still in wide use today. His ideas on classification have influenced generations of biologists during and after his own lifetime, even those opposed to the philosophical and theological roots of his work.

questions.question: Presence of diaphragm is the characteristic feature of:
questions.explanation: Mammals have diaphragm as their main characteristics. Diaphragm, dome-shaped, muscular, and membranous structure that separates the thoracic and abdominal cavities in mammals, it is the principal muscle of respiration. The muscles of the diaphragm arise from the lower part of the sternum, lower six ribs, and the lumbar vertebrae of the spine and are attached to central membranous tendons.

questions.question: The branch of Biology dealing with classification is known as:
questions.explanation: Taxonomy is the branch of science concerned with classification, especially of the organisms. It is the science of naming, describing, and classifying organisms which include all plants, animals, and microorganisms of the world.

questions.question: Which of the following characteristics does not apply to fungi?
questions.explanation: Autotrophic are those organisms that can prepare their own food. But fungi are heterotrophic. They grow as tubular filaments called hyphae. An interwoven mass of hyphae is called mycelium whose walls are strengthened with chitin. It disperses by releasing spores.

questions.question: Naked seeds are present in ________.
questions.explanation: Naked seeds are one of the main characteristics of Gymnosperms. Pinus is a Gymnosperm while others are Angiosperms.

questions.question: The plant body has a chitinous cell wall and is made up of hyphae and mycelium:
questions.explanation: The chitinous cell wall is one of the main characteristics of Fungi. Rhizopus is a type of fungi. While the rest of the options are from Plantae.

questions.question:
Find the incorrect pair

  1. Starfish–Phylum Chordata.
  2. Leech–Phylum Annelida.
  3. Octopus–Phylum Mollusca.
  4. Liverfluke–Phylum Platyhelminthes.
questions.explanation: Starfish belongs to the phylum Echinodermata.

questions.question: Which one of the following is a cold-blooded animal?
questions.explanation: Lizard being a reptile is a cold-blooded animal.

questions.question: Which of the following has a two-chambered heart?
questions.explanation: Rohu is a fish. Pisces have a two-chambered heart.

questions.question: ________ is the basic unit of taxonomy.
questions.explanation: The species is the fundamental category of taxonomic classification, ranking below a genus or subgenus. A species is a group of similar individuals who are capable of exchanging genes and are able to produce fertile offsprings. Thus, it is a principal natural taxonomic unit.

questions.question: A plant has a woody stem and its leaves show reticulate venation?
questions.explanation: Dicot plants show reticulate venation i.e. veins are interconnected like web to form a network of it and tap root system is present in which there is one main or primary root from which arises the thin lateral secondary roots and thereby arises tertiary roots.

questions.question: Needle shaped structure in pinus plant is ________.
questions.explanation: The leaf is adapted to spiny form so as to protect itself from their grazing organisms and reduce the rate of transpiration.

questions.question: Which of the following is a monocot?
questions.explanation: Wheat has leaves with parallel venation which is a characteristic feature of monocots and it has leaf sheath which covers the stem.

questions.question: Placoid sclaes are present in ________.
questions.explanation: Placoid scales are tiny and tough scales that are meant for protection in aquatic organisms such as sharks and elasmobranchs.

questions.question: Male Ascaris can be distinguished from female Ascaris by ________.

options.option: Both Curved posterior end and Round Shape

questions.explanation: Males are shorter than females and the post-anal tail is curved while females have a straight posterior end.

questions.question: Which of the following is the largest phylum?
questions.explanation: Arthropoda is the largest phylum which constitutes of a wide variety of organisms with jointed appendages as a characteristic feature. It includes about 84% of all the known animal species.

questions.question: Air bladder is present in ________.
questions.explanation: Bony fishes have air bladder which is also known as swim bladder because it is filled with air and provides buoyancy.

questions.question: The scientific name of an organism:

options.option: may refer to more than one species
is the same for scientists all over the world
varies according to the native language of scientists

questions.explanation: Scientific names are given to make the living organisms universally recognizable by scientists all over the world because the common name or vernacular names vary from place to place which makes it difficult to recognize the organism everywhere.

questions.question: The taxonomic unit 'Phylum' in the classification of animals is equivalent to which hierarchical level in classification of plants
questions.explanation: The Division term is used in the taxonomic hierarchy in place of phylum while classifying plants.

questions.question: Members of which of the following kingdom are single cells with considerable internal complexity?
questions.explanation: Monerans are unicellular organisms with all metabolic activities going inside the single cell. Whittaker placed bacteria in their own kingdom, Monera, because of fundamental organizational differences between prokaryotic bacterial cells, which lack membrane-enclosed nuclei and organelles, and the eukaryotic cells of other organisms that possess internal membranes.

questions.question: Amphibians do not have the following:
questions.explanation:
Scales are not present in amphibians as scales are meant for protection from the enemies and harmful radiations from sun. Amphibians produce chemicals for their defence and they live in both land and water so can cope with the harmful radiations of sun.

Also, scales maintain Moisture on the skin which is not required by the amphibians as they inhabit water.

questions.question: Which of the following is not a criterion for classification of living organisms?
questions.explanation: Height of the plant is not a criteria for classification as it depends on the growth conditions. Same genus may have varying heighted plants on the basis of their environmental conditions

questions.question: The feature that is not a characteristic of protochordata
questions.explanation: Jointed legs is a characteristic feature of Arthropods.

questions.question: The locomotory organs of echinodermata are
questions.explanation: Tube feet help in locomotion feeding and respiration of echinoderms. They are located in the groves in arm and work due to hydraulic pressure.

questions.question: Corals are
questions.explanation: corals live in colony for many generations in their identical polyp phase and create a large skeleton of calcium carbonate. This is the characteristic feature of corals. They have formed coral reefs over millions of years in tropical ocean near equator.

questions.question: Who introduced the system of scientific nomenclature of organism?
questions.explanation: Carolus Linnaeus introduced the idea of scientific naming of living organisms and also gave binomial nomenclature which states that every organism should be named in two parts- first part will be it's generic name and second part will be the specific name.

questions.question: Two chambered heart occurs in
questions.explanation:
• 2 chambers heart contains one ventricul and one atrium and it can be found in fish.
  • 3 chambers heart contains one ventricle and 2 atria and it can be found in reptiles and amphibians.
  • 4 chambers heart contains 2 ventricles and 2 atria and it can be found in mammals, birds and crocodiles.

questions.question: Skeleton is made entirely of cartilage in
questions.explanation: Sharks do not have swim bladder to prevent itself from sinking so it has cartilaginous skeleton.

questions.question: One of the following is not an Annelid
questions.explanation: Urchins also called sea urchins are marine animals having spiny body and belong to phylum echinodermata

questions.question: Karl Von Linne was involved with which branch of science?
questions.explanation: Carl Linnaeus also known after his ennoblement as Carl von Linné was a Swedish botanist, physician, and zoologist, who formalised the modern system of naming organisms called binomial nomenclature. He is known by the epithet "father of modern taxonomy".

questions.question: Real organs are absent in
questions.explanation: Coelenterates are acoelomates and lack respiratory, digestive and circulatory system. Their nervous system is also diffused type.

questions.question: Hard calcium carbonate structure are used as skeleton by
questions.explanation: Echinoderms have been compared to living, moving castles. Castles are made of interlocking blocks, with a single main entrance and numerous slit windows for air and for defense. Echinoderm skeletons are made up of interlocking calcium carbonate plates and spines. This skeleton is enclosed by the epidermis and is thus an endoskeleton.

questions.question: Differentiation in segmental fashion occurs in
questions.explanation: Leech (annelids) have metameric segmentation i.e., their body is divided into segments which are identical to each other and bear set of organs in each segment.

questions.question: In taxonomic hierarchy, family comes between
Five kingdom classification was given by
questions.explanation: R.H. Whittaker proposed the five kingdoms of classification in 1969. This classification was based upon certain characters like mode of nutrition, thallus organization, cell structure, phylogenetic relationships and reproduction. This form of classification includes five kingdoms Monera, Protista, Fungi, Plantae and Animalia.

questions.question: The well-defined nucleus is absent in:
questions.explanation: Blue-green alga is a category of bacteria that can perform photosynthesis and is a prokaryotic organism. It's called cyanobacteria and belongs to the phylum monera. It does not have a well-defined nucleus. It has a nucleoid.

questions.question: The 'Origin of Species' is written by
questions.explanation: On the Origin of Species, published on 24 November 1859, is a work of scientific literature by Charles Darwin which is considered to be the foundation of evolutionary biology. Its full title was On the Origin of Species by Means of Natural Selection, or the Preservation of Favoured Races in the Struggle for Life.

questions.question: Organisms of the Kingdom Animalia are:
questions.explanation: The major group of animals is classified under the Kingdom Animalia, also known as Metazoa. This kingdom does not contain prokaryotes. All the members of this kingdom are multicellular, eukaryotes. They are heterotrophs, they depend on other organisms directly or indirectly for food.

questions.question: Which of the following is common among plants and animals?
questions.explanation: Both plants and animals have a well-defined nucleus and cell organelles i.e. both are eukaryotic.

questions.question: The binomial system of nomenclature means that every organism has:

options.option: a name was given by two scientists
one scientific name and one popular name
a number in an international catalog by which an organism is identified.
one scientific name consisting of a generic name and a specific name

questions.explanation:
According to the binomial system of nomenclature, every organism is designated a scientific name with two parts:

  1. Genus is written first and is capitalized (e.g. Homo).
  2. Species follows and is written in lower case (e.g. Homo sapiens).

questions.question: Mosses are:
questions.explanation: Mosses can be either monoecious (both sexes on the same plant body) or dioecious (both sexes on different plant bodies).

questions.question: To which group do the plants belong, that have an embryo, vascular tissue, seeds but lack fruits.
questions.explanation: Gymnosperms produce naked seeds, i.e., seeds are not enclosed in fruits but they have vascular tissue and embryo.

questions.question: Chloroplast in Spirogyra is:
questions.explanation: The chloroplast in Spirogyra is ribbon-shaped and arranged spirally. Ribbons are twisted towards the right in ascending order.

questions.question: While locating the stem of fern, the students find that it is:
questions.explanation: Ferns do not have aerial stems rather their leaves arise directly from the underground stem in the form of the rhizome.

questions.question: Funaria differs from Dryopteris in having:
questions.explanation: Vascular bundle is not present in Funaria (mosses) but is well developed in Dryopteris (ferns).

questions.question: The structure by which the nucleus in Spirogyra cell is held?
questions.explanation: Cytoplasmic strands are otherwise called as plasmodesmata. These are small extensions of cytoplasm which extend through the cell wall of plant cells to connect adjoining cells. Cytoplasmic strands also hold the nucleus in position inside the cell.

questions.question: Needle-shaped structure in Pinus plant is
questions.explanation: The leaves of pinus plant are the needle-shaped structures.

questions.question: Some students want to prepare a temporary mount of spirogyra. Where should they search for a fresh specimen?

options.option: In a pond of stagnant dirty water
In a stream of running fresh water.
In a pond of a salty water.
In a stream of running salty water.

questions.explanation: The filaments of spirogyra make a green tangled mat on the surface of ponds. Algae are generally found in stagnant water bodies like ponds.

questions.question: Which of the character is identifying feature of gymnosperms?
questions.explanation: 'Gymno' means naked and 'sperma' means seed. Gymnosperms were the first plants to have seeds. They are often referred to as having naked seeds because they do not have flowers, and their seeds develop on the surface of the reproductive structures of the plants rather than being contained in a specialized ovary. These seeds are often found on the surfaces of cones and short stalks.

questions.question: Megasporophylls and microsporophylls are associated with which of the following plants?
questions.explanation: Pinus belongs to gymnosperms and gymnosperms are characterized by having megasporophylls (female gametophyte) and microsporophylls (male gametophyte).

questions.question: Agaricus is a common edible, fleshy fungi. It is
questions.explanation: It is a characteristic feature of kingdom fungi. They feed on dead and decaying organic matter and found in moist and damp places.

questions.question: Hyphae are found in
questions.explanation: The mycelium is made up of filaments called hyphae found in fungi. A hypha consists of one or more cells surrounded by a tubular cell wall. In most fungi, hyphae are divided into cells by internal cross-walls called "septa" (singular septum). Septa are usually perforated by pores large enough for ribosomes, mitochondria, and sometimes nuclei to flow between cells.

questions.question: Which of the following is not an aquatic adaptation?
questions.explanation: The presence of hollow bones is an aerial adaptation to reduce the body weight of birds.

questions.question: The number of parts in which the body of a cockroach is seen to be divided is
questions.explanation: The cockroach is an insect and is a terrestrial animal mostly a common household pest. Cockroaches possess the various type of locomotion they can walk, climb, fly. The body is divided into three parts - head, thorax, and abdomen.

questions.question: The number of legs in a cockroach is
questions.explanation: Cockroaches have six legs. The legs are segmented, and all of them are attached to the middle part of the cockroach's body, which is known as the thorax.

questions.question: Earthworm is
questions.explanation: The earthworm has both male and female reproductive organs but cannot fertilize itself because the reproductive organs are present in different segments.

questions.question: Which one of the following is not a characteristic of an Earthworm?
questions.explanation: The earthworm is bisexual, possesses a segmented body, and locomotion is done by setae.

questions.question: Which of the following is not an aerial adaptation?
questions.explanation: The presence of gills is an aquatic adaptation.

questions.question: The abdomen of a cockroach contains:
questions.explanation: Abdomen in both males and females consists of 10 segments. A typical abdominal segment has a dorsal tergum ventral sternum and between them a narrow membranous pleuron on each side which bears spiracles.

questions.question: Respiration in fish is mainly by:
questions.explanation: In aquatic animals such as fish, respiration takes place through special respiratory organs called gills. Gills are present on both the sides of the head of fish.

questions.question: Phylum possessing jointed appendages or legs is
questions.explanation: Phylum Arthropoda Arthrd means 'jointed' and 'pod' means 'legs'. Thus, organisms belonging to phylum Arthropoda have jointed appendages. Arthropods can be distinguished from other kinds of non-chordates because arthropods are the only animals with jointed appendages (legs and antennae).

questions.question: The number of teeth in the beak of birds are
questions.explanation: Jaws are modified into beak in which teeth are absent.

questions.question: The distinguishing characteristic that places animals into the phylum chordate is
questions.explanation: All chordates have a post-anal tail. A post-anal tail is an extension of the body that runs past the anal opening.

questions.question: Body cylindrical, divided into ring-like segments with coelom and digestive system are characteristics of phylum
questions.explanation: Annelids have a cylindrical body divided into ring-like segments called as an annulus with true coelom and developed digestive system.

questions.question: A glandular collar-like thickening found in 14 -16 segments of an earthworm is called as:
questions.explanation: “ 14 -15-16 ” segment has a pink coloured layer around them. This thick collar or girdle like thickening is known as “Cingulum” or clitellum. This is made up of glandular cells.

questions.question: In a taxonomic hierarchy, as one goes from Species to Kingdom the number of common characteristics:
questions.explanation: Common characters of organisms are made more peculiar when we arrange them accordingly in the taxonomic hierarchy. Species have more characters in common while upper levels have lesser similarities in their body design, habits, and habitat.

questions.question: The broadest category of the organism in biological taxonomy is:
questions.explanation: The kingdom is the first taxonomic level and it involves the least similar characteristics. Kingdom involves basic living habits and body design as criteria to consider an organism into either of the 5 kingdoms.

questions.question: Which is the biggest plant group in the Plant Kingdom?
questions.explanation: Angiosperms are the flowering plants that undergo the sexual mode of reproduction to produce seeds. It is the largest group as it is the most adapted type of plant in the present scenario.

questions.question: A plant is diploid and well adapted to extreme conditions. They grow bearing sporophylls in compact structures called cones. Identify the group to which it belongs:
questions.explanation: Gymnosperms are just primitive to angiosperms and these are nonflowering plants. They reproduce by forming male and female cones as their reproductive organs.

questions.question: Amoeba, paramecium are examples of organisms from which kingdom?
questions.explanation: Amoeba and paramecium are the Protista with a single-celled body. They have very simple habits and habitat and all metabolic activities are performed within the cell with the help of water current.

questions.question: Kingdom Fungi are:
questions.explanation: Kingdom fungi involve eukaryotic, multicellular organisms that have a body like plants but are not able to perform photosynthesis rather they are heterotrophs.

questions.question: Monocots and Dicots are the features of:
questions.explanation: Angiosperms are seed-bearing plants and they bear two types of seeds. Seeds having single cotyledon-monocots, seeds having two cotyledons-dicots.

questions.question: Which of the following organisms is not classified as plants?
questions.explanation: Mushroom is a member of kingdom fungi and it feeds on dead and decaying organic matter.

questions.question: What is called the amphibians of the Plant Kingdom?
questions.explanation: Bryophytes are called the amphibians of the plant kingdom because like amphibians they can survive both on land and in water.

questions.question: Lichen is a symbiotic association between:
questions.explanation: Algae and fungi together live in an association which is known as lichen. Algae perform photosynthesis with the help of fungi which maintains the moisture and protects the algae. Fungi in turn get food from algae.

questions.question: Radial symmetry is found in:
questions.explanation: Echinodermata gets divided into two parts when cutting from any plane passing through the center of its body, so it has radial symmetry.

questions.question: The skeleton of starfish is made up of:
questions.explanation: Starfish belong to invertebrates and do not have actual skeleton although it has calcium carbonate plates called ossicles as an endoskeleton.

questions.question: Which of the following is an egg-laying mammal?
questions.explanation: The duck-billed platypus is a mammal as it can feed its young one but it doesn't give birth to offsprings rather it lays eggs i.e. it is an oviparous organism.

questions.question: Pteridophyte does not have:
questions.explanation: Pteridophytes are the ancient plants that bear spores and reproduce with the help of wind. They do not bear flowers as flowers are needed to perform sexual reproduction which is not a characteristic of pteridophytes.

questions.question: Identify a member of Porifera
questions.explanation: Spongilla is a poriferan having pores all over it's body which are meant for circulation of water through these pores into the canals inside its body. During this circulation spongilla takes up the food and oxygen carried with the water and mixes the waste and carbon dioxide into water for their removal.

questions.question: Which is not an aquatic animal?
questions.explanation: Filarial worms belong to nematodes and are parasitic in nature. They require two hosts for completing it's life cycle. An Arthropod as intermediate and a vertebrate as primary host.

questions.question: The Kingdom intermediate in between Kingdom Monera and multicellular organisms are:
questions.explanation: Kingdom monera follows the kingdom Protista which is followed by manifold complex kingdoms Fungi, Plantae, and Animalia.

questions.question: The class Aves have the following features except that their:
questions.explanation: Birds do not have diaphragm rather air enters and exits with a change in pressure in air sacs. They have lungs but lungs do not expand or contract due to the absence of diaphragm.

questions.question: The phylum Arthropoda contains about 80% of all animal species known to science. Which feature distinguishes this group from all other invertebrates?
questions.explanation: Jointed legs or paired appendages is the characteristic feature of Arthropods. These appendages and wings make them highly adaptable and defensive for their enemies.

questions.question: Which of the following sets of animals share a four-chambered heart?
questions.explanation: Crocodiles, Birds, Mammals have four-chambered heart so they have double circulation and oxygenated and deoxygenated blood flow separate in the heart.

questions.question: Birds and mammals share one common feature of the following characteristics
questions.explanation: Birds and mammals are Warm-blooded animals i.e. they can maintain their body temperature according to the environment.

questions.question: Hydra, jellyfish and Sea anemone belong to
questions.explanation: Hydra, jellyfish, and sea anemone are the organisms that belong to different groups of phylum Coelenterata.

questions.question: Name the phylum in which animals have soft bodies covered with a hard shell:
questions.explanation: Mollusca is a soft-bodied animal and possesses a hard shell of calcium carbonate as a protective covering.

questions.question: Which of the following pair of animals is warm-blooded?
questions.explanation: Birds and Mammals are Warm-blooded animals as they can cope with their environment in a better way by maintaining their body temperature.

questions.question: Which of the following groups of animals are all invertebrates?
questions.explanation: Earthworm, insect, and crab are invertebrates as none of these have a vertebral column.

questions.question: Which among the following produce seeds?
questions.explanation: Gymnosperms are fruitless seed plants as they do not have their seeds enclosed in fruit.

questions.question: Which one is a true fish?
questions.explanation: Dogfish is a member of shark family and has cartilaginous skeleton, Gill slits and fins.

questions.question: Which among the following is exclusively marine?
questions.explanation: Echinodermata are exclusively marine because all the species are marine and have water vascular system.

questions.question: In which group of animals, coelom is filled with blood?
questions.explanation: Arthropoda has open circulatory system. It doesn't have arteries and veins. It's body cavity (coelomic cavity) is filled with blood.

questions.question: Elephantiasis is caused by
questions.explanation: Wuchereria bancrofti is type of filarial worm which causes elephantiasis.

questions.question: Which one is the most striking or common character of the vertebrates?
questions.explanation: Notochord is present in all the vertebrates unexceptionally either at some stage or all the life stages.

questions.question:
Which among the following has specialised tissue for conduction of water?

  1. Thallophyta
  2. Bryophyta
  3. Pteridophyta
  4. Gymnosperms
questions.explanation: Pteridophyte is the first terrestrial planet to have specialized conducting tissue-Xylem and Phloem. Xylem tissues are important for the conduction of water and mineral in plants. Both xylem and phloem are present in the gymnosperms also.

questions.question:
Which among the following have open circulatory system?

  1. Arthropoda
  2. Mollusca
  3. Annelida
  4. Coelenterata
questions.explanation: The open circulatory system is common to molluscs and arthropods. Open circulatory systems pump blood into a cavity (hemocoel) with the blood diffusing back to the circulatory system between cells. Blood is pumped by a heart into the body cavities, where blood surrounds the organs. This system lacks a true heart or capillaries. The blood found in these organisms is a mix of blood and an interstitial fluid called hemolymph.

questions.question:
Which among the following have scales?

  1. Amphibians
  2. Pisces
  3. Reptiles
  4. Mammals
questions.explanation: Amphibians and mammals don't have scales on their body whereas Pisces and reptiles have scales on their body.

questions.question:
Organisms without a nucleus and cell organelles belong to

  1. Fungi
  2. Protista
  3. Cyanobacteria
  4. Archaebacteria
questions.explanation: Cyanobacteria and archaebacteria belong to kingdom-Monera. Monerans are organisms without nucleus and cell organelles and are called prokaryotes.

questions.question: The book Systema Naturae was written by
questions.explanation: Linnaeus (Carl von Linne), a Swedish botanist, is known as the Father of Taxonomy. He developed the most widely applicable binomial system of nomenclature of organisms. 'Systema Naturae' was one of his major publications. The first edition of this book was printed in the Netherlands (in 1735), which was a twelve-page work. By the time, it reached its 10th edition (in 1758), it classified 4400 species of animals and 7700 species of plants.

questions.question: Meena and Hari observed an animal in their garden. Hari called it an insect while Meena said it was an earthworm. Choose the character from the following which confirms that it is an insect.
questions.explanation: Body with jointed legs is a characteristic feature of Kingdom Arthropoda and all the insects belong to this kingdom.
